## 1.2. Scientific advice

Table 1 Scientific advice and protocol assistance

| Date             | Topic (quality/ non-clinical/ clinical)   | Reference number / Coordinator(s)   | Brief summary of the advice                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                   |
|------------------|-------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| 23 July 2020     | Non-clinical and clinical                 | EMEA/H/SA/4492/1/2 020/SME/III      | The Scientific advice pertained to the following non-clinical and clinical aspects: The adequacy of the planed non-clinical development strategy: general strategy, immunotoxicity. The proposed DDI studies, the approach to collect QT/QTc data and the need to conduct dedicated studies in patients with renal impairment; Phase 3 study plans, including study design, patient population, choice of control, study duration, primary and secondary endpoints, sample size and statistical analysis methods; overall clinical evidence plans to support MAA for treatment of acromegaly. |
| 11 November 2021 | Quality                                   | EMA/SA/0000069283                   | The Scientific advice pertained to the following quality aspects: Designation of starting materials for drug substance manufacture, tests and acceptance criteria for control and release of the starting materials, risk assessment and control strategy for potential genotoxic impurities in the drug substance; number of primary stability batches for MAA per strength                                                                                                                                                                                                                  |
| 25 May 2023      | Clinical and Quality                      | EMA/SA/0000134586                   | The Scientific advice pertained to the following quality and clinical aspects: Starting materials: acceptability, control and release criteria; risk assessment and control strategy for potential genotoxic impurities in the drug substance; number of primary stability batches for MAA per strength Need for clinical studies to support the 30 mg tablet strength                                                                                                                                                                                                                        |

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

| 22 February 2024   | Non-clinical and clinical   | EMA/SA/0000162419   | The follow-up Scientific advice pertained to the following non- clinical and clinical aspects: Adequacy of non-clinical tests for immunotoxicity and approach to environmental risk assessment Acceptability of the characterisation of arrhythmogenic potential and for not performing a thorough QT study   |
|--------------------|-----------------------------|---------------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|

## 1.3. Eligibility to the centralised procedure

The applicant Crinetics  Pharmaceutical  Europe  GmbH submitted on 4 March 2025 an application for marketing authorisation to the European Medicines Agency (EMA) for Palsonify (paltusotine), through the centralised procedure under Article 3 (2)(a) of Regulation (EC) No 726/2004. The eligibility to the centralised procedure was agreed upon by the EMA/CHMP on 25 July 2024.

The applicant applied for the following indication: treatment and long-term maintenance therapy of adult patients with acromegaly.

## 1.4. Legal basis and dossier content

## The legal basis for this application refers to:

Article 8.3 of Directive 2001/83/EC - complete and independent application

The application submitted is composed of administrative information, complete quality data, and nonclinical  and  clinical  data  based  on  applicant's  own  tests  and  studies  and/or bibliographic literature substituting/supporting certain test(s) or study(ies).

## 1.5. Information on paediatrics

Pursuant to Article 7 of Regulation (EC) No 1901/2006, the application included an EMA decision(s) (EMA/PE/0000223523) on the granting of a product-specific waiver.

## 1.6. Information on orphan market exclusivity

Paltusotine was designated as an orphan medicinal product (EU/3/25/3035) on 26 February 2025 in the following condition: 'treatment of acromegaly'.

Following the CHMP positive opinion on this marketing authorisation, the Committee for Orphan Medicinal Products (COMP) reviewed the designation of Palsonify as an orphan medicinal product in the approved indication. More information on the COMP's review can be found in the orphan maintenance assessment report published under the 'Assessment history' tab on the Agency's website: https://www.ema.europa.eu/en/medicines/human/EPAR/palsonify

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

## 1.6.1. Similarity with authorised orphan medicinal products

Pursuant to Article 8 of Regulation (EC) No 141/2000 and Article 3 of Commission Regulation (EC) No 847/2000, the applicant did not submit a critical report addressing the possible similarity with authorised orphan medicinal products, from the start of the procedure because there is no authorised orphan medicinal product for a condition related to the proposed indication.

## 1.7. Applicant's request for consideration

## 1.7.1. New active substance status

The applicant requested the active substance paltusotine contained in the above medicinal product to be considered as a new active substance, as the applicant claims that it is not a constituent of a medicinal product previously authorised within the European Union.

## 1.7.1.1. CHMP recommendation on new active substance status

Based on the review of available data on the active substance, the CHMP considers that paltusotine hydrochloride is to be qualified as a new active substance in itself, as it is not a constituent of a medicinal product previously authorised within the European Union.

## 2. Introduction

## 2.1. Therapeutic context

## Disease definition and important clinical characteristics

Acromegaly is a serious, chronic, rare disease caused by excess secretion of GH by a pituitary adenoma which in more than 90% of the cases is benign and leads to increased production of insulin-like growth factor-1 (IGF-1) from the liver and other organs.

Clinical  symptoms  include  signs  and  symptoms  attributed  to  tumour  mass  effects  compressing  the surrounding structures (e.g., headaches and vision loss), and by the chronic overproduction of both GH and IGF-1 leading to disordered growth (progressive skeletal and soft tissue overgrowth, mainly at the extremities  and  head,  organ  enlargement)  and  metabolic  complications  including  insulin  resistance, increased blood glucose levels, hyperinsulinemia, diabetes, and dyslipidaemia.

Symptoms of acromegaly include abnormal growth of hands and feet; alteration of facial features; and thickening  of  tissue  leading  to  arthritis,  diabetes,  hypertension,  joint  pain,  fatigue,  headache, hyperhidrosis, asthenia, paraesthesia, soft tissue oedema, sleep apnoea, and excessive snoring.

If  left  untreated,  acromegaly  has  a  mortality  rate  that  is  nearly 2-fold higher  than  the  general population, mainly due to cardiovascular complications and a significant decreased quality of life (QoL). Based  on  its  clinical  presentation,  acromegaly  is  regarded  as  a  serious  and  chronically  debilitating condition with high impact on patient QoL.

## Number of people affected or at risk

The prevalence estimate for each EU member state was used to calculate an estimate of the number of cases for 2023, using 2023 population estimates. Where no prevalence data was available, the highest reported prevalence (1.367 per 10,000, Iceland) was used to provide the maximum number of cases.

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

Based on an estimated 50,257 cases, the EU prevalence of acromegaly has been calculated as 1.11 cases per 10,000 (upper estimate).

## Diagnostic criteria and methods used in clinical practice

The diagnosis of acromegaly is confirmed based on elevated serum insulin-like growth factor 1 (IGF-1) and lack of growth hormone suppression after glucose administration.

In  adults,  the  diagnosis  of  acromegaly  should  be  considered  in  patients  presenting  with  acral enlargement or suggestive facial features as well as in those presenting with a constellation of symptoms, signs, or conditions associated with acromegaly, including frequent headache, excessive perspiration, hypertension, sleep apnoea, oligomenorrhea, arthralgias, carpal tunnel syndrome, and type 2 diabetes mellitus.

Randomly sampled serum GH levels, measured by immunoassays, are generally not recommended for the diagnosis of acromegaly but have been associated with biochemical outcomes of both surgical and medical treatments. Serum GH levels, measured every 30 minutes for 2 hours after the administration of an oral glucose load (75 g), can be helpful in establishing the diagnosis of acromegaly.

In the last consensus on criteria for acromegaly diagnosis and remission ( 14 th  Acromegaly consensus: Giustina et al 2024 ), in a patient with typical acromegaly features, insulin-like growth factor (IGF)-I &gt; 1.3  times  the  upper  limit  of  normal  for  age  confirms  the  diagnosis.  Random  growth  hormone  (GH) measured after overnight fasting may be useful for informing prognosis but is not required for diagnosis.

Once the diagnosis  of  acromegaly has  been  confirmed  based on  the  results  of  endocrine  testing,  a pituitary  magnetic  resonance  imaging  (MRI)  examination  should  be  obtained  to  detect  a  pituitary adenoma, which is the cause of acromegaly in most cases.

## Current Therapeutic Options

The current major treatment goals for acromegaly are to normalize IGF-1 levels for age and sex and to decrease GH levels below 1 ng/mL to reduce mortality, reverse or attenuate signs and symptoms, control tumour mass, and maintain pituitary function.

The  optimal  treatment  should  provide  robust  efficacy  in  controlling  IGF-1  levels  and  consistently minimising acromegaly symptoms, while having an acceptable safety profile. The ultimate objective is to  improve  patient  QoL  and  satisfaction.  Unmet  needs  include  optimizing  biochemical  and  symptom control, reducing treatment burden and enhancing QoL. Of note, recurring symptoms and compromised QoL can be also seen in patients that are biochemically controlled.

The existing therapeutic strategy includes surgery (first-line therapy) and/or medical treatment and/or radiotherapy.

Transphenoidal  surgery is  generally  regarded  as  first  line  therapy  for  patients  with  acromegaly; however, surgical effectiveness varies depending on various factors, such as the size and extension of the anatomic mass, and the preoperative levels of GH. Surgery is most successful in patients with blood GH levels below 40 µg/L before the operation and with pituitary tumours no larger than 10 mm in diameter (microadenomas). Most patients with a macroadenoma require post-surgical medical treatment to achieve disease control (i.e. normalization of GH and IGF-1). Overall, after surgery, the hormone levels normalise only in 40-70% of the patients ( Kumar et al 2009, Melmed 2009, Platta et al 2010 ).

Radiotherapy is generally reserved for patients with tumour remaining after surgery, for patients who are not good candidates for surgery because of other health problems, and for patients who do not respond adequately to surgery and medication. Radiation therapy lowers GH levels by about 50% over 2 to 5 years ( Wass et al, 2001 ). Radiation therapy causes a gradual loss of production of other pituitary hormones with time which can result in the undesirable effect of panhypopituitarism. Loss of vision and brain injury can be complications from radiation treatments.

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

## Current medical treatment

According to clinical practice guidelines and expert consensus, medical therapy is recommended for the treatment of acromegaly in patients who have persistent or recurrent disease despite surgery, or who are waiting for radiotherapy, or who are not candidates for surgery or radiotherapy because of poor health.

Current medical treatment options for the management of acromegaly include somatostatin receptor ligands (SRLs), GH-receptor antagonists and dopamine agonists.

Table 3 Current medical therapies for acromegaly in Europe

| Medical treatment       | Medical treatment                                                                                                    | Indication                                                                                                                                                                                                                                              |
|-------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Somatostatine analogues | octreotide acetate (Sandostatine LAR pre- filled syringe IM)                                                         | Treatment of acromegaly, in: · Patients who are adequately controlled on sc treatment with Sandostatin, · Patients in whom surgery, radiotherapy are anappropriate or ineffective, or in the interim period until radiotherapy becomes fully effective. |
| Somatostatine analogues | octreotide acetate (Sandostatine solution for subcutaneous injection)                                                | Treatment of acromegaly: When the circulating levels of growth hormone (GH) and IGF- 1 remain abnormal after surgery and/or radiotherapy or when surgery and/or radiotherapy is inappropriate                                                           |
| Somatostatine analogues | lanreotide acetate (Somatuline LAR pre- filled syringe IM or powder and solvent for solution for injection)          | Treatment of acromegaly: When the circulating levels of growth hormone (GH) and IGF- 1 is abnormal after surgery and/or radiotherapy or when surgery and/or radiotherapy is inappropriate                                                               |
|                         | pasireotide pamoate (Signifor 10 mg, 20 mg, 30 mg, 40 mg and 60 mg) powder and solvent for suspension for injection) | Treatment of acromegaly: Adult patients with acromegaly for whom surgery is not an option or has not been curative and who are inadequately controlled on treatment with another somatostatin analogue.                                                 |
|                         | octreotide acetate 20 mg (Mycapssa 20 mg) gastro-resistant hard capsules                                             | Mycapssa is indicated for maintenance treatment in adult patients with acromegaly who have responded to and tolerated treatment with somatostatin analogues.                                                                                            |
| GH receptor antagonist  | pegvisomant (Somavert, powder and solvent for solution for subcutaneous injection)                                   | Treatment of patients with acromegaly who have had an inadequate response to surgery and/or radiation therapy and in whom an appropriate medical treatment with somatostatin                                                                            |

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

|                   |                                          | analogues did not normalize IGF-1 concentrations or was not tolerated.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                               |
|-------------------|------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Dopamine agonists | bromocriptine (Parlodel 2.5 mg, tablets) | · Prolactinomas: - basic treatment of prolactinomas: micro or macroadenomas, - In particular, preparation to surgery in case of macroadenoma in which Parlodel can favour surgery in reducing the tumoral volume especially in case of extra-fossa extension. - In case of precocious or late failure of surgery: recurrence of a hyperprolactinemia |
| Dopamine agonists | cabergoline (Dostinex 0.5 mg, tablets)   | · Idiopathic hyperprolactinemia · Hyperprolactinemia due to the presence of a microadenoma or a hypopituitary macroadenoma and clinical symptoms: - in women: galactorrhoea, oligo or amenorrhoea, infertility - in men: gynaecomastia, impotence                                                                                                    |

Of note, the Marketing of Authorisation of Mycapssa has been withdrawn since the 26 February 2025. The  dopamine  agonists  (bromocriptine  and  cabergoline)  and  prolactin  inhibitors  (lisuride  if  tumour secretes prolactin) are used off-label in association with other medical treatment of acromegaly.

On 30 June 2025, Oczyesa (octreotide hydrochloride) 20 mg prolonged-release solution for injection in pre-filled pen has been authorized in Europe in the following indication: 'maintenance treatment in adult patients with acromegaly who have responded to and tolerated treatment with somatostatin analogues'.

The  treatment  strategy  depends  on  the  details  of  the  condition,  such  as  presence  of  micro-/ macroadenoma, responsiveness to treatment, and GH or IGF-1 levels (see Giustina et al 2014 ).

Somatostatin  analogues  are  used  as  first  line  pharmacological  treatments  in  patients  not  cured  by surgery/radiotherapy or requiring additional treatment pre- or postoperatively. For patients not cured by surgery or for whom surgery is contra-indicated, somatostatin receptor ligands (SRLs) are established as first-line medical treatment with potential for further improvements and lead to limited disease control with biochemical response rate of 30-55%, continued symptoms, and increased mortality.

In case of partial response with SRLs, adjunctive therapies can be considered in association to SRLs such as dopamine agonists (cabergoline, bromocriptine) which are used off-label and pegvisomant. In case of no response, pegvisomant can be considered in monotherapy.

Currently approved octreotide-based therapies have significant limitations related to their administration, including complex handling, inconvenient dosing, and a low, or very low, bioavailability, resulting in low or variable plasma exposure.

While  SRL  depot  injections  octreotide  and  lanreotide  are  currently  the  first-line  medical  therapy  for patients with persistent disease after surgery or for those who are not candidates for surgical treatment, they are associated with substantial treatment burden and technical challenges, limit patients' abilities to manage treatment of their disease, and have a significant negative impact on patient quality of life for the duration of their use in this chronic disease.

There remains an unmet need for a targeted therapy that reduces the overall treatment burden and combines rapid, consistent, and durable disease control to treat a wide range of the acromegaly patient population that requires medical treatment.

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

## 2.2. Aspects of development

The clinical development program includes 11 phase 1 clinical studies in healthy volunteers and 1 phase 1 clinical study in participants with hepatic impairment. A phase 2/3 study PA9001 in participants with acromegaly in Japan is being conducted as well.

The efficacy of paltusotine was evaluated in two completed phase 2 studies (study CRN00808-03 and study CRN00808-02 ) and 1 on-going OLE phase 2 study in participants with acromegaly (study CRN00808-05 ).

-Study  CRN00808-03 included  participants  with  acromegaly  who  were  treated  with  SRL-based treatment regimens.

-Study CRN00808-02 enrolled participants who were switched from long-acting octreotide/lanreotide monotherapy to once-daily paltusotine oral capsules.

- Both studies included the option of participants continuing in the on-going OLE study (CRN00808-05).

During Study CRN00808-05 , paltusotine participants were transitioned from once-daily paltusotine oral  capsules  to  once-daily  paltusotine  oral  tablets ,  the  intended  commercial  formulation  of paltusotine.

The  paltusotine  clinical  registration  program  includes  2  pivotal,  global,  randomized,  double-blind, placebo-controlled  clinical  phase  3  studies  ( CRN00808-08 and CRN00808-09 )  with  on-going  OLE phases:

-Study CRN00808-08 was designed to evaluate the efficacy and safety of paltusotine versus placebo as treatment (as assessed by IGF-1 levels) for biochemically uncontrolled acromegaly in participants not being treated medically at study randomization.

-Study CRN00808-09 was designed to evaluate the efficacy and safety of paltusotine versus placebo as maintenance therapy (as assessed by IGF-1 levels) in participants with acromegaly whose IGF-1 was controlled at screening on long-acting octreotide or lanreotide monotherapy.

The population studied in the phase 3 studies included participants with medically naïve and previously medically treated acromegaly, including those who were biochemically controlled on SRLs and those who washed out from SRLs, and is representative of the broad patient population with acromegaly.

## 2.3. Description of the product

Paltusotine hydrochloride is a novel orally administered nonpeptide somatostatin receptor 2 (SST2) agonist.

It  has  been  developed  to  reduce  serum  GH  and  IGF-1  levels  in  healthy  volunteers  and  acromegaly patients. Phase 3 studies in acromegaly patients are still on-going in different countries around the world, including in the European Union (EU).

Proposed indication : medical treatment and long-term maintenance therapy of adult patients with acromegaly.

Proposed posology : The recommended initial dose is 40 mg paltusotine by oral route once a day, administered as 2 × 20 mg tablets. During initiation, paltusotine may be temporarily reduced to 20 mg if needed, based on tolerability.

After at least one week, based on insulin growth factor-1 (IGF-1) levels or clinical signs and symptoms, titrate to a dose of 60 mg daily, administered as 2 × 30 mg tablets.

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

## 2.4. Inspection issues

## 2.4.1. Good manufacturing practice (GMP) inspection

No inspection required.

## 2.4.2. Good laboratory practice (GLP) inspection

No inspection required.

## 2.4.3. Good clinical practice (GCP) inspection(s)

The Committee for Medicinal Products for Human Use (CHMP) has adopted a request for a routine GCP inspection to be carried out for the following clinical trial CRN00808-09, in accordance with Article 57 of Council Regulation (EC) No. 726/2004 and article 15 of Directive 2001/20/EC.

No critical findings were identified that could have impacted negatively the data quality. The conclusion was that the data submitted was reliable and could support the benefit/risk assessment.

## 3. Quality aspects

## 3.1. Introduction

The finished product is presented as a film coated tablet containing 20 mg or 30 mg of paltusotine as active substance (as paltusotine hydrochloride).

Other ingredients are:

Tablet core: copovidone, cellulose, microcrystalline, crospovidone, silica, colloidal anhydrous, magnesium stearate, mannitol (E 421);

Film-coating (Palsonify 20 mg film-coated tablets): hypromellose, titanium dioxide (E 171), triacetin (E 1518), iron oxide, yellow (E 172), iron oxide, red (E 172);

Film-coating (Palsonify 30 mg film-coated tablets): hypromellose, titanium dioxide (E 171), triacetin (E 1518), iron oxide, yellow (E 172).

The product is available in heat induction sealed, white high-density polyethylene (HDPE) bottles closed with white child resistant polypropylene (PP) closure. Each bottle contains a silica gel desiccant.

## 3.2. Active substance

## 3.2.1. General information

Full information on the active substance is included in the dossier.

The chemical name of paltusotine hydrochloride is 3-[4-(4-aminopiperidin-1-yl)-3-(3,5difluorophenyl)quinolin-6-yl]-2-hydroxybenzonitrile hydrochloride corresponding to the molecular formula C27H22F2N4O.HCl It has a relative molecular mass of 492.95 (hydrochloride salt) or 456.49 (free base) and the following structure:

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

<!-- image -->

The active substance structure was elucidated using elemental analysis, chloride content, FTIR spectroscopy, mass spectrometry, NMR spectroscopy and UV-Vis spectroscopy tests. Physicochemical characterisation was performed by determining melting point, thermal analysis (DSC and TGA), dissociation constant (pKa), partition coefficient (Log P) and hygroscopicity. Polymorphic screening demonstrated that form A is consistently produced by the manufacturing process, and it was determined that no potential isomerism is possible as the active substance is achiral.

The active substance is a light yellow to reddish-brown crystalline solid which reversibly picks up moisture. It is slightly soluble in pH 1 acidic media but practically insoluble as pH increases.

## 3.2.2. Manufacture, process controls and process development

Evidence of GMP compliance has been provided in the QP declaration.

Manufacture of paltusotine hydrochloride is a convergent process consisting of several steps using four starting materials, defined according to a previous scientific advice procedure, with acceptable specifications.

Adequate information on the source, impurity profiles and routes of synthesis of the starting materials as appropriate, was provided. Information on the fate and origin of impurities, results from spiking and purging studies and process knowledge support the control strategy and intermediate specifications. Adequate in-process controls are applied during the synthesis. The specifications and control methods for intermediate products, starting materials and reagents have been presented.

The characterisation of the active substance and its impurities are in accordance with the EU guideline on chemistry of new active substances.

Potential and actual impurities were well discussed with regard to their origin and characterised. Mutagenic impurities were assessed and controlled according to ICH M7. A risk assessment indicated no risk of nitrosamine impurities.

Residual solvents are controlled in the active substance specification according to ICH Q3C. Class 1 and 2A elements are below 30% of the ICH Q3D option 1 limits and thus not included in the specification. Palladium, used as a catalyst in the process, is controlled to NMT 20 ppm in the active substance.

The applicant explained the history of development of manufacturing process and discussed the impact on comparability. All changes made during process development are justified and resulted in an active substance of improved quality. The in-process data, combined with the batch data, indicate that the process is capable of operating within defined parameters to generate active substance of the required quality routinely.

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

The active substance is packaged in tied linear low-density polyethylene (LLDPE) bags which comply with Commission Regulation (EU) 10/2011, as amended. The LLDPE bags containing drug substance are placed inside a second LLDPE bag which is stored in an aluminium bag inside a high-density polyethylene (HDPE) drum.

## 3.2.3. Specification

The active substance specification includes tests for appearance (visual), identification (IR, UHPLC), assay (UHPLC), related substances (UHPLC), residual solvents (GC), palladium content (ICP-MS), boron content (ICP-MS), water content (KF), residue on ignition (Ph. Eur.), and chloride content (Ph. Eur.).

Impurities present at higher than the qualification threshold according to ICH Q3A were qualified by toxicological and clinical studies and appropriate specifications have been set. Mutagenic impurities are controlled according to ICH M7.

The analytical methods used have been adequately described and non-compendial methods appropriately validated in accordance with the ICH guidelines. Satisfactory information regarding the reference standards has been presented.

Batch analysis data 11 production batches of the active substance are provided, 8 from the proposed commercial manufacturer and 3 from a previous manufacturer. The results are within the specifications and consistent from batch to batch.

## 3.2.4. Stability

Stability data from a representative number of batches stored in the intended commercial package for up to 36 months under long term conditions (25 ºC / 60% RH) and for up to 6 months under accelerated conditions (40 ºC / 75% RH) according to the ICH guidelines were provided. The analytical methods used were the same as for release and the parameters tested are according to the release specification (table 1), with the omission of tests for identity, residual solvents, elemental impurities and residue on ignition.

All results of the stability studies provided met the specified limits and no significant trends were observed.

Photostability testing following ICH guideline Q1B was performed on 2 batches indicating that paltusotine is photosensitive and thus requires secondary packaging.

A forced degradation study was carried out under thermal, acidic, basic and oxidative conditions, thereby demonstrating that the analytical methods are stability indicating. The active substance degrades under oxidative conditions and is moderately sensitive to temperature.

The provided stability data support the proposed retest period of 36 months stored at or below 25 °C in the proposed container closure system.

## 3.3. Finished medicinal product

## 3.3.1. Description of the product and pharmaceutical development

This medicine is supplied in two different strengths:

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

20 mg is presented as immediate release, oval, biconvex film-coated tablets coated with a pinkcoloured water-soluble film. It is debossed with 'PAL' on one side and '20' on the other side. 30 mg is presented as immediate release, oval, biconvex film-coated tablets with a yellow-coloured water-soluble film. It is debossed with 'PAL' on one side and '30' on the other side. The tablets are dose proportional, have the same % and w/w composition and are readily distinguished by size, colour and debossing. A Quality Target Product Profile (QTPP) was developed in accordance with ICH Q8 (R2) to guide pharmaceutical development of paltusotine tablets. Separate risk assessments were performed to identify the spray-dried dispersion (SDD) intermediate manufacturing process risks, drug product manufacturing process risks, and drug product biopharmaceutical risks. These risk assessments provide the framework for identifying finished product and SDD critical quality attributes (CQAs), critical material attributes (CMAs), critical bioavailability attributes (CBAs) and manufacturing process critical process parameters (CPPs). CQAs were presented and justified. The active substance is a BCS Class IV compound, with low solubility and low permeability. It is formulated as an amorphous spray-dried dispersion (SDD), which enhances solubility such that it behaves as a highly soluble compound. Since the first step of the manufacturing process involves dissolution of the active substance, its physicochemical properties such as particle size and crystalline form are of limited importance. Compatibility of the SDD with the other excipients has been demonstrated. Information about the functionality-related characteristics of the excipients is provided and justified. This would allow to ensure that these parameters will remain to be adequately controlled in case of any future changes in excipient suppliers. The functionality-related characteristics of pharmacopeial excipients (crospovidone, microcrystalline cellulose, mannitol, colloidal silicon dioxide and magnesium stearate) cover such parameters as particle size distribution, specific surface area and loss on drying. All excipients are well known pharmaceutical ingredients, and their quality is compliant with Ph. Eur. standards. There are no novel excipients used in the finished product formulation. The list of excipients is included in section 6.1 of the SmPC. As paltusotine.HCl is a BCS class IV substance, the initial biopharmaceutics risk classification for the finished product was determined to be medium. Potential CBAs (pCBAs) were selected from finished product failure modes that could impact bioavailability, including formulation variables, CQAs, CMAs, and CPPs. The risk of impact on bioavailability (absorption rate and extent) for each pCBAs was evaluated through failure mode and effects analysis (FMEA). Control strategies are in place for the two potential failure modes related to potential CBAs with high severity scores, crystallization of the active substance (pCBA: crystalline content, measured by XRPD at release and on stability) and disintegration of the tablet (pCBAs: tablet hardness, tablet disintegrant level). Early phase clinical trials were conducted with hot melt granulation capsules, whereas the proposed commercial formulation was used in phase 3 studies. The two formulations were successfully bridged by bioavailability studies. clinically relevant based on the similarity of the dissolution profiles (f2&gt;50) between bioequivalent 20

A dissolution test has been developed for routine quality control. The method is considered to be mg and 30 mg tablets. Discriminatory power was investigated on batches manufactured with meaningful changes in composition or material attributes of individual components. Some discrimination was generally seen at early timepoints for all modifications. However, the initially proposed specification limit was not accepted by CHMP as it was too wide to allow detection of noncompliant batches, resulting in a major objection. In response, the applicant agreed to tighten the limit for the 20 mg tablet which was considered acceptable. The limit for the 30 mg tablet was also amended. Further tightening was not possible given the limited manufacturing experience, although the applicant also tightened the acceptance limits for hardness which was considered sufficient to resolve the major objection. The CHMP recommends re-evaluating the specification for the 30 mg tablet upon completion of the first 10 commercial batches and to update the dossier as appropriate.

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

Manufacturing process development has been adequately described including identification of CPPs and an appropriate control strategy.

The primary packaging is a heat induction sealed, white HDPE bottle closed with white child resistant PP closure. Each bottle contains a silica gel desiccant. The materials comply with Ph. Eur. and EC requirements. The choice of the container closure system has been validated by stability data and is adequate for the intended use of the product.

## 3.3.2. Manufacture of the product and process controls

For all sites involved in the manufacture, control and batch release of the finished product, sufficient evidence of GMP compliance has been provided.

Representative batch formulae for paltusotine SDD intermediate and paltusotine film-coated tablets were provided. The SDD manufacturing process consists of dissolution of the components in methanol, spray drying and secondary drying.

The finished product manufacturing process consists of six main steps: blending of intra-granular excipients with the SDD, roller compaction, blending with extra-granular excipients, compression, filmcoating and packaging. Process controls were listed for each step.

The manufacturing process is a standard process in alignment with the guideline on process validation for finished products. Process validation will be performed in accordance with cGMP's prior to commercial distribution of the drug product. The validation protocols are provided and acceptable. The in-process controls are adequate for this type of manufacturing process and pharmaceutical form.

## 3.3.3. Product specification

The finished product release and shelf-life specifications are consist of tests for appearance (visual), identification (UHPLC, UV), assay (UHPLC), impurities (UHPLC), water content (KF), uniformity of dosage units (Ph. Eur.), dissolution (Ph. Eur.), crystallinity (XRPD) and microbial contamination (Ph. Eur.).

The acceptance criteria are the same at release and shelf-life except for assay, for 3 specifically identified impurities and total impurities, where wider limits are proposed at shelf-life in line with stability data. The levels of impurities have been assessed for mutagenic potential and determined to be non-mutagenic. Also, crystallinity by XRPD and TAMC, TYMC and specified microorganism are tested annually during stability. Different acceptance criteria are applied to the 20 mg and 30 mg strength paltusotine tablets as previously explained.

A risk assessment on the potential presence of elemental impurities in the finished product was provided and complies with ICH Q3D. The results of the risk assessment show that none of the evaluated elemental impurities exceed 30% of the established PDEs.

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

A risk assessment concerning the potential presence of nitrosamine impurities in the finished product was performed considering all suspected and actual root causes in line with the 'Questions and answers for marketing authorisation holders/applicants on the CHMP Opinion for the Article 5(3) of Regulation (EC) No 726/2004 referral on nitrosamine impurities in human medicinal products' (EMA/409815/2020) and the 'Assessment report- Procedure under Article 5(3) of Regulation EC (No) 726/2004- Nitrosamine impurities in human medicinal products' (EMA/369136/2020). The applicant identified risks based on the presence of 3 active substance impurities containing secondary amine moieties, and the use of excipients known to contain trace nitrite and assigned acceptable intakes based on the CPCA. Test data for two of the potential nitrosamine impurities was provided. However, the analytical method was not validated. Furthermore, test data for the third potential impurity was not available, resulting in a major objection. In response, the applicant provided the requested batch data, including data on aged samples to show that levels remain acceptable throughout shelf-life. The analytical method was shown to be validated and sufficiently sensitive. Therefore, it is considered acceptable to omit controls for nitrosamine impurities from the specification and the major objection is resolved.

The analytical methods used have been adequately described and appropriately validated in accordance with the ICH guidelines. Satisfactory information regarding the reference standards has been presented.

The batch analysis data (14 batches for 20 mg tablets and 7 batches for 30 mg tablets) were provided, including the analytical results of four registration/primary stability batches of paltusotine tablets (for each strength of tablets). The analytical results confirm the consistency of the manufacturing process and its ability to manufacture to the intended product specification.

## 3.3.4. Stability of the product

Stability data from four commercial scale batches per strength of finished product stored for up to 24 months under long term conditions (25 ºC / 60% RH) and for up to 6 months under accelerated conditions (40 ºC / 75% RH) according to the ICH guidelines were provided. The batches of medicinal products are representative of those proposed for marketing and were packed in the primary packaging proposed for marketing. Samples were tested according to the stability specification listed in table 10.

Tablets of each strength were stored in the commercial bulk packaging (two polyethylene bags sealed with a twist tie and a desiccant pack placed between the polyethylene bags). Bulk storage of up to 12 months is supported by the provided stability data. No significant changes or trends were observed.

Tablets were exposed to light as defined in the ICH Guideline on Photostability Testing of New Drug Substances and Products. Palsonify is not photosensitive.

Based on available stability data, the proposed shelf-life of 30 months without specific storage conditions as stated in the SmPC (section 6.3) is acceptable.

## 3.3.5. Adventitious agents

No materials of human and animal origin are used in the manufacturing process.

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

## 3.4. Discussion and conclusions on quality aspects

Information on development, manufacture and control of the active substance and finished product has been presented in a satisfactory manner. Two major objections were raised concerning the acceptance criteria for the dissolution method and the control strategy for potential nitrosamine impurities. Both were resolved by provision of additional data. The results of tests carried out indicate consistency and uniformity of important product quality characteristics, and these in turn lead to the conclusion that the product should have a satisfactory and uniform performance in clinical use.

At the time of the CHMP opinion, the dissolution limit for the 30 mg tablet could still potentially be tightened following manufacture of additional commercial scale batches. This point is put forward and agreed as a recommendation for future quality development.

The quality of this product is acceptable when used in accordance with the conditions defined in the SmPC. Physicochemical and biological aspects relevant to the uniform clinical performance of the product have been investigated and are controlled in a satisfactory way.

## 3.4.1. Recommendation for future quality development

In the context of the obligation of the MAHs to take due account of technical and scientific progress, the CHMP recommends the following points for investigation:

- To re-evaluate the specification for the 30 mg tablet upon completion of the first 10 commercial batches and to update the dossier as appropriate.

## 4. Non-clinical aspects

## 4.1. Introduction

The non-clinical program was developed according to ICH M3(R2). The rat and dog were selected as the species for non-clinical toxicity studies based on similarity in the metabolic profiles compared to humans. Both species are also pharmacologically relevant. The pivotal toxicity studies were performed up to 26 weeks in rats and up to 39 weeks in dogs with appropriate recovery periods. The standard genotoxicity battery recommended in ICH S2 was performed. The carcinogenic potential was assessed in 6-month rasH2 mouse and 2-year rat carcinogenicity studies. DART program is composed with a GLP fertility and early embryonic development study in rats, embryo-foetal development studies in rats and rabbits and a pre- and postnatal study in rats. GLP in vitro and in vivo studies were conducted to assess the phototoxic potential of paltusotine. Additional identified impurities associated with the paltusotine drug substance and/or drug product were evaluated for potential genotoxicity by QSAR prediction methodologies or Ames in accordance with ICH M7(R2). All pivotal studies were GLP compliant. A full ERA was provided.

## 4.2. Analytical methods

LC-MS/MS technics were conducted in two different laboratories located in China and the United States using different analytical procedures. Differences in the pre-treatment procedure (liquid-liquid extraction or protein precipitation) and in the analytical range and limit of sensitivity (2.50 to 2500 ng/mL or 10 to 10,000 ng/mL) were observed in laboratories in the US and China, respectively. The methods were validated and although the lower and upper limits of quantification differed, the reliability of quantitative data did not appear to be significantly affected.

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

## 4.3. Pharmacology

## 4.3.1. Pharmacodynamics

## 4.3.1.1. Primary pharmacodynamics

Paltusotine is a bias agonist of SST2 receptor with a CHO K1 human cells EC50= 0.25nM. This value is compared to peptide agonists: octreotide and native somatostatin-14 (SS14) respectively EC50=0.058nM and EC50=0.14nM. Paltusotine EC50 is slightly higher than octreotide and SS14 EC50 which demonstrated a lower or same effect. This trend is the same for CHO K1 cells in other species (rabbit, rat, dog and monkey). Paltusotine is a biased agonist and preferentially affect G-protein signalling and inhibition of cAMP accumulation over other pathways (internalization and desensitization via β -arrestin). Paltusotine, octreotide and SS14 were tested on these receptors and Paltusotine demonstrated the less internalization and desensitization of them. Internalization and desensitization are led to tachyphylaxis and this study demonstrated that paltusotine did not induce tachyphylaxis compared to octreotide and SS14. This may lead to a reduction in adverse events of β -arrestin. The reversibility liaison of paltusotine on its receptors has not been measured in the pharmacology studies but non-clinical and clinical studies did not suggest non-reversible liaison and effects.

The suppression effect on GH after a single oral administration of paltusotine was measured on rat. A challenge model was used for measuring acute effects of somatostatin analogues on GH secretion. GH levels were evaluated when paltusotine was orally administered at doses of 3, 10, and/or 30 mg/kg to male or female rats at 1 and/or 3 hours. Single administration of paltusotine produced significant plasma GH suppression in a dose dependant manner (ranged from 15% to 79% at 3 mg/kg, 32% to 89% at 10 mg/kg, and 85% to 97% at 30 mg/kg).

The IGF-1 level was measured on rat chronic model. The ability of orally administered paltusotine (at 10 mg/kg/day and 30 mg/kg/day) to reduce IGF-1 in rats over 14-days was assessed and compared to continuous  administration  of  240  μg/kg/day  of  octreotide delivered  via  a  surgically  implanted subcutaneous mini-osmotic pump. A better decrease for octreotide was observed at D1 and a little better for 30 mg/mL paltusotine at D14 (16% vs 12%). These results showed no difference with these 2 drugs at D14. In parallel of IGF-1 level, non-fasting blood glucose and body weight was measured. There were no treatment effects on non-fasting blood glucose levels all along the study and groups. Compared to vehicle, decreased body weight gain was observed in paltusotine and octreotide-treated rats. Octreotide induced a more important change in body weight than paltusotine (25.4-26.8% vs 16.2%) but less than vehicle control which is not normal (30.3%). 10 mg/kg/day was an effective dose in the rat (Cmax and AUC0-24 = 127 ng/mL and 975 ng×h/mL). Another study on dog was realized to measure IGF-1 levels at 6, 30, and 150 mg/kg/day once daily for 7 days. All doses of paltusotine suppressed IGF-1 on average up to 42% on Day 7 compared to baseline (0%). No dose response was evident, suggesting that the lower dose of 6 mg/kg/day was effective (Cmax and AUC0-24 = 729 ng/mL and 3700 ng×h/mL for male and 1610 ng/mL and 9040 ng×h/mL for female).

## 4.3.1.2. Secondary pharmacodynamics

CHO-K1 cells expressed hSST receptors and paltusotine was tested on 5 receptors to visualize cAMP reduction. Paltusotine is highly selective for SST2 (EC50=0.25nM) and was no selective for other SST receptors (1100 to &gt;10,000nM). Paltusotine was then compared to octreotide and SS14, peptide somatostatin agonists. SS14 was selective with any receptors and EC50 on these 5 SST receptors was comprised between 0.065 and 0.85nM. Octreotide is more selective on SST2 with EC50=0.058nM but the difference of selectivity with other SST receptors is less important than paltusotine. In fact, EC50 of SST1, 3, 4 and 5 are respectively &gt;10,000, 7.9, 470 and 2.5 nM. Paltusotine has less risk to touch other SST receptors than octreotide or SS14. Paltusotine was demonstrated to be &gt;4000-fold more selective for SST2 relative to the other SST receptors whereas octreotide was only 43-fold more. Paltusotine was tested on a selectivity panel of 87 assays. Paltusotine was not selective with most of these tar gets. The only exception was the serotonin transporter in which paltusotine at 10 μM showed 88% inhibition (Ki = 0.097 μM). Most serotonin transporters are associated with CNS and the enteric nervous system. Paltusotine do not penetrate CNS in rats and has a &gt;2000-fold selectivity over EC50 for hSST2, the interaction with the serotonin transporter did not seem to be possible at therapeutic exposure.

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

## 4.3.1.3. Safety pharmacology

SST2 and SST5 receptors are largely found in pancreas. These receptors are respectively responsible of glucagon production and insulin inhibition and can have an impact on glycaemia. An oral glucose tolerance test was realized on rat by paltusotine oral gavage at 10 and 100mg/kg. At several time, paltusotine had no effect on oral glucose tolerance. The CNS safety was evaluated by functional observational battery on rat after an oral gavage at 25, 75 and 500mg/kg at several time. No significant CNS effects on qualitative and quantitative functional observational battery evaluations at any time point and dose. At the higher dose, unbound Cmax is 21-fold higher than clinical unbound Cmax. Several studies on cardiovascular system have been conducted. Two in vitro studies have been realized and demonstrated no hERG inhibition, neither ion channels inhibition (&gt; 700-fold clinical concentration). Two non GLP cardiovascular telemetry study in beagle dogs were conducted to finally selected the higher and pertinent dose for GLP pivotal studies and are negative. Based on these studies a maximum dose of 10 mg/kg was selected for the GLP cardiovascular study. A GLP cardiovascular telemetry study in beagle dogs was conducted. Each dog (n=5) was administered vehicle (100% propylene glycol) or paltusotine at 0.5, 2, 5, and 10 mg/kg with a 7-day washout period between dosing days and cardiovascular parameters were collected continuously from approximately 2 hours predose through approximately 24 hours post dose following each dose administration. As non GLP studies, HR decrease at all doses, and this decrease was statistically significant at all doses except 5mg/kg. A slight increase for blood pressure and MAP, and no continuous effects on ECG parameters were observed. QT interval and QTc presented statistically significant change compared to vehicle at some hours. There is no dose dependant effect on these parameters. At 10 mg/kg, the unbound plasma Cmax of paltusotine was 16 ng/mL which is approximately 5.5-fold higher than the unbound steady state clinical Cmax of 2.96 ng/mL at a 60 mg dose. In these 3 cardiovascular telemetry studies in beagle dogs, HR, BP and MAP are modified by paltusotine. These effects are transitory and last few hours in 24h monitoring and need to be followed on longer studies in animals and in human. In the clinical part, bradycardia and cardiac conduct abnormalities were found too. A GLP respiratory study have been conducted on rat by oral gavage at 0, 25, 75 and 500mg/kg. At the highest dose, statistically significant increase in respiratory rate and minute volume was demonstrated at 2 and 6 hours and at 75mg/kg at 6 hours. At 24 hours, there is no change on these parameters which demonstrate the reversibility of these events. At 500 mg/kg, the unbound plasma Cmax of paltusotine was 62 ng/mL, which is approximately 21-fold higher than the unbound clinical Cmax of 2.96 ng/mL a 60 mg dose.

## 4.3.1.4. Pharmacodynamic drug interactions

No pharmacodynamic drug interaction studies have been conducted, and this was found to be acceptable.

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

## 4.3.2. Pharmacokinetics

## 4.3.2.1. Absorption

In vitro , the permeability of paltusotine in colorectal adenocarcinoma 2 cells was moderate. Absorption studies showed that paltusotine was a P-gp and BCRP substrate. As a concentration-dependent decrease in efflux was observed, a minimal impact on the absorption of paltusotine at the proposed therapeutic doses is expected.

In  vivo absorption  was  extensively  studied  mostly  exclusively  by  oral  route;  only  one  study  was performed by IV route. After IV administration of 2.5 mg/kg of paltusotine to rats, dogs, and monkeys, the mean Vss were 1.5, 3.4, and 2.3 L/kg, respectively, which ranged from 2 to 5 times of total body water in all species.

Absolute bioavailability was determined in three experiments conducted in three species i.e. rat, dog and monkey, which revealed a poor absorption of paltusotine after oral administration, 8.8, 48 ± 7.4 and 10 ± 1.6 respectively. Many studies using different formulations, salts, feeding conditions each time were secondly performed to optimize the absorption of paltusotine. All were carried out using oral gavage. AUC0-last, parameter that clearly illustrates the impregnation of the body by the drug, obtained in the different experimental conditions were compiled. The small number of animals used each time, and the large intra- and inter-assay variability did not allow for quantitate evaluation of relative bioavailability. And, even if many data are presented, they are difficult to compare since the experimental conditions are always different in terms of formulation (presence of ethanol, propylene glycol, vitamin E, HCl,), salt (di HCl, HCl, dimesylate), food regimen (fasted, not fasted, food ad libitum), dose, etc. However, some findings could be drawn from these data: some formulations seemed to promote absorption; although less  well  absorbed  HCl  salt  seemed  more  stable;  food  intake  clearly  decreased  the  absorption  of paltusotine. For lower concentration and after a single paltusotine dose, it may be considered that when dose  increased,  AUC0-last  increased.  For  higher  doses,  no  dose-concentration  correlation  was  clearly established with concentration that increased in a less than dose proportional manner. For lower doses, a linear AUC0-last - dose relation was roughly obtained with a large intra- and inter-assay variability at each point and a poor correlation. Moreover, inconsistent values may be observed possibly due to emesis episodes, unsuitable formulation, poor solubilization of paltusotine (dosing formulations with concentrations 25 mg/mL and higher considered suspensions), etc. The same observations are made when analysing the AUC 0-last data obtained after the first dose in repeated dose studies. A very careful attention should be paid to the experimental conditions carried out in safety animal studies after oral administration given the large inter-assay variability in terms of absorbed dose and resulting AUC. The risk would be to overestimate the actual dose present in the animal after oral administration and not to reach  toxic  concentrations.  This  is  particularly  true  for  high  doses  where  no  dose-concentration relationship has been shown. Note that the solubilization of paltusotine that is given by oral gavage as solution at low dose and suspension at high dose might contribute to the malabsorption of high doses. The  low  absorption  of  paltusotine  is  justified  by  different  vehicles  and  formulations  requirement  to enhance  absorption,  the  Biopharmaceutics  Classification  System  (class  IV:  low  permeability  and solubility) and vomiting animals.

Considering the short half-life, a very large accumulation of paltusotine was not expected. But, regarding the T½ of paltusotine 3.6 and 12 hours measured in rat and dog respectively after oral administration, a larger accumulation was expected in dog. In line, the mean ratio was 1.3 in rats, 2.5 in dogs.

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

Exposures reached at highest doses in 26-week rat and 39-week dog studies were 6.9- and 25-fold higher, respectively, than the anticipated steady state clinical AUC exposure at 60 mg.

Toxicokinetic  analysis  in  rats  and  dogs  showed  peak  and  total  systemic  exposures  of  paltusotine increased in a dose-dependent manner over the dose range. Conversely, AUC values did not show timedependency: in both 13- and 26-weeks toxicity studies in rats and in 13- and 39-weeks studies in dogs, exposure values decrease from day 1 to the last day of treatment.

## 4.3.2.2. Distribution

In vitro studies were performed to investigate the binding of paltusotine to plasma proteins and to determine its partitioning into whole blood and red blood cells. Paltusotine was highly protein bound (98 to 99%) in animals and humans and had a blood to plasma ratio of around 1.1, which was comparable to the blood to plasma ratio observed in the clinical study after oral administration of paltusotine to humans, suggesting minimal accumulation of paltusotine into red blood cells. In addition, in vivo studies were performed to investigate distribution of paltusotine into tissues of Sprague Dawley (non-pigmented) and Long Evans (LE, pigmented) rats following oral administration of [ 14 C]paltusotine (QWBA study). This study was performed only in male, therefore no distribution study in female reproductive organs is available. Paltusotine was widely distributed to tissues and was generally similar in the two strains. Little to no radioactivity was detected in the lens of the eye, and the central nervous system, which was confirmed by a dedicated study. Minimal brain to plasma ratio of paltusotine (&lt;0.05) was observed in rats. QWBA study demonstrated a preferential binding to melanin containing tissues, namely the meninges, uveal tract, and the pigmented skin. In a rat foetal transfer study, a placental transfer was observed after paltusotine treatment, which is confirmed in pre- and postnatal study in rats.

## 4.3.2.3. Metabolism

Exploratory metabolite profiling and identification of paltusotine was conducted in vitro using microsomes, hepatocytes and HμREL cells from humans and animal species. Metabolism was also studied in vivo , in plasma, bile (rat only), and urine samples collected from rats, dogs, and humans orally administered with paltusotine. This was followed by radiolabelled ADME studies in rats. Paltusotine is mainly metabolized to the glucuronide conjugate (M632/1, also known as CRN06377), the oxidative metabolite M472/1, and its glucuronide conjugate (M648/1). A carbamoyl glucuronide (M676/1, also known as CRN06554) was also identified as a minor metabolite. This indicated that glucuronidation and oxidation were the main metabolic pathways of paltusotine. Glucuronidation studies in human liver microsomes suggested that UGT1A1 was the main contributor in the formation of CRN06377 with minor contribution from UGT1A9. The oxidative metabolite M472/1 was CYP enzyme mediated and was primarily formed by CYP3A4/5 with minor contribution from CYP2D6. In mass balance study performed in humans, no metabolites were detected in plasma in humans. paltusotine was the only circulating radioactive component in humans and the most abundant component in rats. The human study also indicated that in human excreta, CRN06377 was the major metabolite, implying that paltusotine was primarily eliminated via glucuronidation. CRN06377 was also the major metabolite in urine and bile in the rat ADME studies. A similar metabolic profile was observed in vitro, when paltusotine was incubated with dog hepatocytes and HµREL cells. Glucuronidation to CRN06377 was the primary metabolic pathway in all species investigated. In rats, glucuronide metabolites were also observed in the plasma, unlike findings in human plasma. They are not expected to have any pharmacological activity. Therefore, glucuronidation to CRN06377 was the primary metabolic pathway in all species investigated. Rat and dogs were demonstrated to be metabolically relevant.

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

## 4.3.2.4. Excretion

Elimination was studied in rats (male and females) and in bile duct cannulated male rats. Faeces was the predominant route of paltusotine excretion in rats, as in humans. There were no sex differences. Toxicokinetic parameters measured in PPND study (see DART part of this AR) supported the excretion of paltusotine in maternal milk with milk-to-plasma ratio at 4hours postdose on LD20 ranging from 2.36- to 3.77-fold. Exposure levels measured in rat offspring on that day (Cmax and AUC0-8) were 0.10.8% of those measured in dams.

## 4.3.2.5. Pharmacokinetic drug interactions

See clinical assessment.

## 4.4. Toxicology

## 4.4.1. Single-dose toxicity

Paltusotine (di-HCl) was well-tolerated after single oral dose up to 1000 mg/kg in rats and up to 150 mg/kg in dogs.

## 4.4.2. Repeat-dose toxicity

Exploratory repeat-dose toxicity studies (non-GLP) with paltusotine were conducted in the rat and dog for up to 14 days to ensure an appropriate dose selection in pivotal GLP studies. All exploratory studies, except 14-days in dogs were performed with di-HCl salt form of paltusotine. All pivotal rat and dog studies were performed with HCl salt form and the 14-day exploratory study in dogs. In rats, an optimization of the dose formulation was conducted. Simple formulations containing citrate buffer and/or cosolvent (e.g. PG) were used in preliminary rat toxicity studies using the di-HCl salt form of paltusotine. The formulation containing a cosolvent (PG) and a non-ionic surfactant (vitamin E Dα -tocopherol polyethylene glycol succinate [TPGS]) in 0.1N HCl was able to achieve dose-dependent increases in systemic exposures at higher doses, therefore this formulation was selected for pivotal studies in rats. TK analysis was performed in rat toxicity studies and an additional investigative PK study in rats (14 days, see in PK part of this report) were performed following the lack of toxicity in the pivotal 28-day GLP study in rats in order to obtain a higher rat exposure. PK profile in rats showed a saturation of AUC was achieved at 250 mg/kg in male rats and at 500 mg/kg in female rats. For the dog, changes to the formulation did not improve systemic exposure compared to 100% PG (2 ml/kg) and, thus, PG was used in both exploratory and pivotal GLP studies. PK profile in dogs suggested a point of saturation at 75 mg/kg/day. Paltusotine was well tolerated in exploratory studies in rats, MTD was determined in rats and in dogs at the maximum tested dose which correspond to be the point of exposure saturation. MTD in rats was determined at 250 mg/kg/day up to 14-day treatment. Test article-related changes were limited to reduced body weight gain in all paltusotine-treated groups resulting in decreased body weights relative to control throughout the dosing period. In dogs, after 7day treatment the MTD was determined to be below 150 mg/kg/day based on an increased incidence and frequency of emesis, as well as decreased body weight, therefore the dose of 75 mg/kg/day was used in the first pivotal study, a 28-day study. However, no toxicity was observed at the maximum tested dose, and an additional investigative dog study was performed (14-day study) demonstrating that the point of exposure saturation was 75 mg/kg/day.

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

Pivotal repeat-dose toxicity studies have been conducted in the rat (up to 6 months) and dog (up to 9 months). 500 mg/kg/day and 75 mg/kg/day doses were selected as the highest dose in rats and in dogs respectively. This was considered acceptable given that these two doses are demonstrated to be the point of exposure saturation.

In rats, paltusotine caused non-dose dependent and non-adverse decreases in body weight and body weight  gain  at  all  doses  tested  (25,  75  and  500  mg/kg/day).  This  effect  could  be  considered  as  a pharmacologically driven effect. This effect reversed after cessation of treatment. The decreases of body weights were also observed with SST2 agonist (octreotide, pasireotide, lanreotide). No organ specific toxicity  was  observed during the rat studies. A higher incidence of progressive cardiomyopathy was observed in 13-week study at 500 mg/kg/day at final necropsy, not observed at recovery necropsy, and not observed after 26-week of treatment. A higher incidence of ovary atrophy was also observed at the high  dose  at  the  final  necropsy  and  was  not  considered  treatment  related  by  the  Applicant.  In reproduction  toxicity  studies,  treatment-related  changes  were  observed  on  female  reproductive parameters and reported in SmPC sections 4.6 and 5.3. In the 26-week rat study, NOAEL was set up at 500 mg/kg/day. This was considered acceptable. The exposure at the NOAEL of 500 mg/kg/day in rats was 6.9-fold higher than the anticipated steady state clinical exposures at the recommended therapeutic dose of 60 mg.

In  dogs,  similar  effects  were  observed.  Clinical  observations  included  emesis,  abnormal  stool (characterized  by  soft,  loose,  watery,  and/or  mucoid  stools),  loss  of  appetite/decreased  food consumption and accompanied decreased body weight. The loss of appetite/decreased food consumption and consequential decreased body weight were readily resolved by offering the standard pellet diet mixed with canned food. Emesis was dose-dependent but sporadic and generally resolved over repeated dosing. Abnormal  stool  resolved  upon  cessation  of  dosing.  These  GI  effects  could  be  considered  as  a pharmacologically  driven  effect  and  were  observed  with  SST2  agonist  (octreotide,  pasireotide, lanreotide). In the 39-week dog study, NOAEL was set up at 75 mg/kg/day, which correspond to 25-fold clinical exposure expected at the maximum recommended dose. It was considered acceptable. In 13week dog study, thymus findings were observed at final necropsy (small thymus correlated to thymus depletion at all dose groups), and thymus depletion in one male at the high dose of 75 mg/kg/day at recovery necropsy. These thymic findings were noted observed after longer time exposure, therefore the Applicant's justification that these findings could be related to stress was accepted.

## 4.4.3. Genotoxicity

In screening non-GLP genotoxicity studies, di-HCl paltusotine was negative for mutagenicity in a standard plate bacterial mutation assay and negative for induction of micronuclei in an in vitro micronucleus assay with and without S9 activation. Then, a GLP compliant standard battery was performed with HCl paltusotine. Although an important cytotoxicity effect was observed, the test could be considered negative. No mutagenicity potential was detected for paltusotine.

An in vitro micronucleus GLP study was performed with HCl paltusotine in CHO cells. In the definitive assay,  paltusotine  was  tested  at  5,  12,  16,  20  µg/ml  with  and  without  metabolic  activation  for  3h treatment and at 1, 2, 6, 10 µg/ml without metabolic activation for 24h treatment. In the S9-activated 3-hour treatment series and the non-activated 24-hour treatment series, no significant increases in the percentage of cells with micronuclei were observed at any concentration. In the non-activated 3-hour treatment series, a concentration-related response (2.55% MN cells) was identified by the statistical CochranArmitage test because at 16 μg/ml the percentage of micronuclei was slightly above the 99% control limits of the historical negative control data (0.61-1.95% MN cells). The Applicant did not consider this result as biological relevant as no statistically significant increases in the percentage of cells with micronuclei at the higher dose of 20 µg/ml was observed. However, the response at 20 µg/ml (1.75%

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

of MN cells) was slightly above the 95% control limits of the historical negative control data (0.8-1.67% MN cells). The 24h treatment without metabolic activation was tested at lower concentrations (up to 10 µg/ml) and therefore, the equivocal result observed after 3h treatment could not be ruled out. The in vitro MN test was considered as equivocal. An in vivo micronucleus GLP test was conducted in SD rat. Paltusotine (HCl) was administrated for 3 days at 250, 500, 2000 mg/kg/day by oral gavage. Paltusotine did  not  induce  cytotoxicity  in  bone  marrow  and  no  statistically  significant  increases  in  micronucleus formation at 7-fold the clinical  exposure  range  at  the  MHRD.  Therefore,  no genotoxic  potential  was identified with paltusotine at the clinical exposure range in the standard genotoxicity battery. This was reflected in the SmPC.

## 4.4.4. Carcinogenicity

The carcinogenicity potential of paltusotine was assessed in oral 26-week transgenic mice study and in oral 2-year rat study.

A dose-range finding study in transgenic mice was preliminary performed (2 phases: 5-day and 28-day treatment). After 5-day treatment, the MTD was determined to be above the highest tested dose of 1000 mg/kg/day and NOAEL after 4-week treatment was set up at the highest tested dose of 500 mg/kg. In the pivotal study, transgenic mice were administrated orally with 25, 75 and 300 mg/kg/day paltusotine for 26 weeks. Exposures in male mice increased in dose-dependent manner. However, in female mice, exposures in  75  mg/kg/day  group  measured  at  D180  were  far  higher  than  expected,  this  could  be explained  by  one  individual  animal  and  in  300  mg/kg/day  at  D180,  female  exposures  were  largely decreased but similar to male exposure. The PK profile is not completely understood. The food effect and the physicochemical property of paltusotine may explain the inconsistency observed in PK parameters. There was no carcinogenic effect related to paltusotine administration up to the maximum tested dose of 300 mg/kg/day which corresponded to 2.8-fold clinical exposure at the MHRD. The neoplastic changes observed in the study were not considered test article related, since the incidence of these findings were in the historical range and not dose-dependent effect.

Paltusotine was administrated by oral gavage in rats at 25, 75 and 500 mg/kg/day. The dose selection was based on the point of saturation observed in shorter rat studies, which was acceptable. Toxicokinetic animals were treated for 26 weeks only. The PK profile appeared coherent in this study. Due to low survival of water and vehicle control animals, the study was terminated early during Week 91 for males and Week 89 for females. However, at least 28 animals/sex in each group survived to Week 80, which provided enough animals to adequately evaluate carcinogenicity. Clinical observations were limited to orange discoloration of the faeces in animals administered 500 mg/kg/day. Mass observations noted were considered normal background observations in studies. Test-related lower body weight trends were observed at all dose levels correlated to a lower food consumption, as in the shorter rat studies (not considered adverse). Most of the neoplasms identified as the cause of death/moribund condition were considered representative of common, spontaneously occurring, age-related neoplasms that occur in laboratory rats. Paltusotine decreased incidences of pituitary and mammary neoplasms in males and/or females administered from 25 mg/kg/day was potentially attributable to the pharmacological mechanism of action of paltusotine via a direct effect of hormonal modulation and/or acting on the gastrointestinal tract. Paltusotine increased incidences of minimal to moderate cystic glandular hyperplasia in the uterus and/or minimal to slight epithelial hyperplasia in the cervix occurred for females administered from 25 mg/kg/day,  relative  to  water  and  vehicle  controls.  These  increases  were  potentially  attributable  to hormone modulating  effects  of  CRN00808  and/or  secondary  to  the  decreased  incidence  of  pituitary neoplasms. A statistical analysis of neoplastic lesion incidence data suggested the possibility of a test article-related increase in combined cervix/uterus stromal neoplasms. The combined incidence of these neoplasms was 3, 0, 2, 4, and 0 for water controls, vehicle controls, and females administered 25, 75, and  500  mg/kg/day,  respectively.  The  absence  of  these  neoplasms  in  females  administered  500

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

mg/kg/day and the similar (3 vs 4) incidence for water controls and females administered 75 mg/kg/day suggested the statistical finding was not indicative of a biological effect. Additionally, the incidences of these  neoplasms  were  within  the  historical  control  range  for  this  strain  of  rats  from  104-week carcinogenicity. During non-neoplastic finding analysis, paltusotine increased incidence and/or severity of brain mineralization, relative to control animals, was observed for males administered ≥75 mg/kg/d ay and females administered 500 mg/kg/day. This finding was reviewed by a neuropathologist expert. The parenchymal mineralization of the brain, mainly the thalamus, was confirmed to represent a spontaneous background  change  and  not  evidence  of  paltusotine-related  neurotoxicity.  The  justification  was acceptable. In addition, paltusotine was demonstrated to have a low blood brain barrier passage and dedicated safety pharmacology study in rats at same concentrations did not demonstrate functional impact. To conclude, administration of paltusotine for 2-year in rats caused some evidence of hormonal modulation (as in fertility study, see DART program). Paltusotine was considered not to have carcinogenic potential up to 500 mg/kg/day, which correspond to 33-fold the clinical exposure at the MHRD.

## 4.4.5. Developmental and reproductive toxicity

A full package of developmental and reproductive toxicology studies is available in rats and rabbits. In the fertility study conducted in rats treated at doses up to 500 mg/kg/day, there were no treatmentrelated effect on mating and fertility indices, sperm parameters and male reproductive organ weights. However, treatment-related decreases in mean numbers of corpora lutea and implantations with subsequent increased preimplantation loss and decreased numbers of total and live embryos were observed at ovarian and uterine examination of females treated at 500 mg/kg/day. These findings were not reported at doses up to 75 mg/kg/day (5-fold human exposure). In addition, there was an increase in oestrous cycle length at ≥75 mg/kg/day.

Embryofoetal development toxicity studies were conducted in rats and rabbits. In maternal animals, expected pharmacological decreases in body weight and body weight gain were observed. In rats, these effects were non-dose dependent and consisted in decreases in body weight gain up to 14-20% compared to controls during treatment duration that were overall not considered as adverse. In rabbits, these effects were more marked and treatment was less tolerated. At the high dose level of 75 mg/kg/day, this led to abortion of three dams on GD 25-27 (each with 9-10 implantation sites detected at necropsy) because of body weight loss and little to no food consumed between GD 7-24/27. Foetal examinations did not reveal treatment-related malformations or embryofoetal lethality in either rat or rabbit at doses up to 500 mg/kg/day and 75 mg/kg/day, respectively, corresponding to 11.5-fold (rat) and 5.8-fold (rabbit) human exposure. The developmental NOAEL was determined at 500 mg/kg/day in rats since no other effect on embryofoetal development was reported. In rabbits, a decrease in foetal body weights was observed at 75 mg/kg/day leading to a developmental NOAEL of 25 mg/kg/day (2.9-fold human exposure).

In the pre- and postnatal development toxicity study, reductions in mean maternal body weight gain (impacting on body weight) and food consumption were observed at ≥75 mg/kg/day and considered non-adverse.  Effects  on  body  weight  of  F1  offspring  were  also  noted  at  500  mg/kg/day  during  the preweaning period. Based on combined sex values, the difference was statistically significant at each testing occasion from PND7. On PND21, the body weight was decreased by 10.5% compared to controls. During the postweaning period at this dose level, there was still a decrease in body weight gain and body weight in both sexes with final body weight decreased by 10.1% (males PND113) and 7.0% (females, PND92) compared to controls. There were decreases in mean body weight at 75 mg/kg/day during the preweaning and postweaning periods that were considered test article related but non-adverse. There were no effects on sexual maturation, neurobehavioral or reproductive function in the F1 generation rats.  Toxicokinetic  parameters  supported  the  excretion  of  paltusotine  in  maternal  milk  with  milk-to- plasma ratio at 4hours post-dose on LD20 ranging from 2.36- to 3.77-fold. Exposure levels measured in rat offspring on that day (Cmax and AUC0-8) were 0.1-0.8% of those measured in dams. Based on the decreased pup weight observed during the development period at 500 mg/kg/day, the preweaning and postweaning developmental NOAELs were established at 75 mg/kg/day corresponding to 6.1-fold human exposure levels considering exposure levels measured at this dose in the rat EFD study.

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

## 4.4.6. Toxicokinetics and exposure margins
