Margins of exposures (animal/human) are based on Cmax and AUC0-24h measured in healthy volunteers, as PK profile in healthy volunteers and in patients were similar, this could be acceptable. However, the resulting animal/human margins of exposure should be taken in consideration with caution, given the large variability of paltusotine bioavailability.

## 4.4.7. Local tolerance

Dedicated local tolerance studies have not been performed with paltusotine. Local tolerance data were part of repeat-dose toxicology studies. GI effects were noticed in dogs (diarrhoea, emesis) and were considered as pharmacologically driven given SST2 receptor was present in GI tractus.

## 4.4.8. Other toxicity studies

The Applicant has justified that no dedicated immunotoxicity study was performed, since no immune adverse  effects  are  expected  from  the  pharmacological  properties.  In  addition,  no  immunotoxicity findings were observed in the non-clinical or clinical program. This was considered acceptable. A low immuno-toxicological potential of paltusotine was predicted.

No metabolites were detected in plasma in humans. Therefore, no need the animal characterization of human metabolites. In human excreta, only glucuronide metabolites were detected and were also detected in rats (plasma or excreta).

Seventy-nine starting materials, intermediates or process impurities were tested according to ICH M7 guideline. Two QSAR models were conducted and accompanied with an expert opinion and when needed, assessments  were  performed  in  miniaturized  and/or  standard  Ames  test.  The  presented  data  was acceptable. In addition, to set the specifications of 808-Imp 3 (2-Pyrrolidone) at 1%, 808-Imp 4 (Chloro Impurity) at 0.3%, 808-Imp 9 (Dimer Impurity) at 0.3%, 808-Imp 10 (Amine Impurity) at 0.5% and 808-Imp-G at 0.3%, two 28-day rat study were conducted with paltusotine with spiked impurities levels. No toxicity  was  observed  and  the  NOEL  was  set  at  the  highest  tested  dose,  which  was  acceptable. Therefore, in study 424-0157-TX, 808-Imp 3 (2-Pyrrolidone) present at 2.3% in the tested batch was considered qualified at 0.037 mg/kg/day (considering the human equivalent dose at the NOAEL), 808Imp  4  (Chloro  Impurity)  present  at  0.47%  in  the  tested  batch  was  considered  qualified  at  0.0075 mg/kg/day  (considering  the  human  equivalent  dose  at  the  NOAEL),  808-Imp  10  (Amine  Impurity) present at 0.54% in the tested batch was considered qualified at 0.0087 mg/kg/day (considering the human equivalent dose at the NOAEL), 808-Imp-G present at 0.49% in the tested batch was considered qualified at 0.0079 mg/kg/day. In study 424-0163-TX, 808-Imp 9 (Dimer Impurity) present at 1% in the tested batch was considered qualified at 0.019 mg/kg/day. Therefore, the proposed specifications were considered acceptable with a maximum daily dose at 60 mg.

The phototoxic potential was assessed according to ICH S10 guideline. Paltusotine absorbs at 238 nm (UV light). Considering that the MEC is above the threshold in UV wavelengths (&gt; 1000 mol-1 cm-1 at wavelengths between 203 nm to 350 nm), an in vitro 3T3 NRU was conducted. The results indicated a phototoxic potential since the photoirritancy factor (PIF&gt; 5) and the mean photoeffect (MPE&gt; 0.15) were above the recommended threshold. To further characterize effects of real administration of paltusotine on eyes and skin, an in vivo study was conducted in pigmented rats. As paltusotine showed bonding to melanin, the used species was considered acceptable. Rats were administered at 0, 10, 75 and 2 groups at 250 mg/kg/day for 3 days at around Tmax (one 250 mg/day/day with UV-vis light exposure and one without).  There  were  no  paltusotine-related  ocular  or  cutaneous  findings  indicative  of  phototoxicity. Initially,  corneal  dystrophy  was  observed  but  no  dose-dependency  was  observed  (2  animals  at  10 mg/kg/dose, bilateral, 1 animal at 75 mg/kg/day, bilateral, and none at 250 mg/kg). This effect is justified as common background and not test-related effect. This effect was further investigated by an extended histopathology analysis (5 additional serial sections of eye of each rat). This effect was not confirmed (incidence of cornea inflammation is similar in each group including group and 1 case of fibrodysplasia is observed one in left cornea and one in right cornea at 10 mg/kg/day). Therefore, this effect  could  indeed  be  considered  as  not  test-related.  Initially,  inferior  focal  retinal  retinopathy  was observed in two animals unilaterally at the high dose 250 mg/kg/day. This effect was not considered as phototoxicity as the product absorbs at 238 nm (in UV spectrum) and the UVs wavelengths would be stopped by cornea and lens and not reach the retina. Additional histopathology analysis (amendment 1) showed minimal degeneration of retina with minimal inflammation in 0/10 eyes in control group, 2/10 eyes at 10 mg/kg/day, in 3/10 eyes at 75 mg/kg/day and 4/10 at 250 mg/kg/day. Although the severity of this effect was not worsened with the dose, the number of affected animals was dose-dependent. The incidence of this effect was higher that the presented historical control values (max 15/120 examined eyes). Therefore, the applicant's conclusion that this effect could not be considered as test-related was not endorsed. The retina distribution was not studied in QWBA distribution study. Exposures measured in this 3-day administration study at the highest dose of 250 mg/kg (at D3, Cmax = 2650-5550) were lower than those observed after 91 days of administration at the highest dose of 500 mg/kg/day in the 26-week study (at D91 Cmax= 5970/7830 ng/ml) and similar than those observed after 182 days of administration (Cmax = 3005 ng/ml). Ophthalmologic examinations and eye histopathology analysis revealed no test-related effect. In the same way, no test-related effect was observed in dogs at higher exposures for a longer time (up to 39-week). Therefore, the link between paltusotine administration and the adverse effect observed in retina in phototoxicity study after 3-day administration was questioned. Currently, investigative ocular monitoring is set up in 4 clinical trials (see clinical part). No additional non-clinical study was requested. The non-clinical findings are reported in the RMP. The relevance of the retina degeneration in human is not known and is currently studied in the on-going clinical trials (see clinical safety part).

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

## 4.4.9. Ecotoxicity/environmental risk assessment

As an ionizable compound, paltusotine Log Kow was experimentally determined according to OECD guideline 107 and was &lt;4.5. Therefore, the definitive PBT/vPvB assessment was not requested.

The initially calculated value of PEC surface water was above the trigger value of 0.01 µg/L, studies for Phase II, Tier A environmental fate and effects testing and analysis following the 2006 EMA Guideline were  submitted  and  during  the  on-going  Phase  II,  Tier  A  studies,  a  further  review  of  the  recent epidemiological data of acromegaly in the EU revealed the erroneous calculations. The newly determined prevalence value of 1.1/1000 was endorsed by the COMP (EMA/OD/0000234998, 23/01/2025). The updated calculation of PECsurface water  was  below  the  threshold  value,  therefore  no  ERA  Phase  II  was needed.

In  Phase  II  no  environmental  risk  was  characterized  for  surface  water,  groundwater  and  microorganisms.  However,  some  properties  of  paltusotine  may  be  of  concern  in  the  long  term  (even  if PBT/vPvB evaluation was not requested), resulting from a poor biodegradation and the formation of non- extractable residues in sediments, putatively bioavailable for sediment dwelling organisms. No questions were raised as the Phase II was not needed based on the updated calculation of PECSW.

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

In addition, the Applicant considered that paltusotine is not an endocrine active substance; however, as highlighted in DART part of this AR, the adverse effects observed at ovarian/uterine examination and oestrous cycle length in high dosed females of the fertility study may be related to the pharmacological activity  of  paltusotine  considering  the  data  obtained  with  somatostatin  analogues.  In  addition,  the literature  reports  e.g.  that  somatostatin,  acting  primarily  through  sstr2,  is  an  inhibitor  of  electrical excitability  of  GnRH  neurons  in  adult  female  and  male  mice,  and  inhibits  the  LH  surge  after intracerebroventricular injection in rats. The influence of GH on reproduction is documented ( Chang et al., 2022 ).

| Calculation Value CAS-number (if available):                     | Calculation Value CAS-number (if available):         | Unit Conclusion 2361216-83-1 (hydrochloride salt) 2172870-89-0 (free base) | Unit Conclusion 2361216-83-1 (hydrochloride salt) 2172870-89-0 (free base) |

chemical class)

As a result of the above considerations, the available data do not allow to conclude definitively on the potential risk of Paltusotine to the environment or the PBT/vPvB assessment.

The Applicant commits to perform the following studies as follow-up measures: the ERA will be updated, and a fish full lifecycle test will be conducted ( OCDE 240 guideline ).

## 4.5. Overall discussion and conclusions on non-clinical aspects

## 4.5.1. Discussion

Paltusotine has demonstrated its efficacy through pharmacology studies. Paltusotine is a biased, highly selective SST2 agonist that decreases levels of GH, IGF-1 and cAMP. Paltusotine was not selective on a panel of 87 assays, no off-targets were identified. Safety studies showed no impact on oral glucose tolerance and CNS. Some events occurred on cardiac and respiratory system (variation of heart rate, blood pressure, interval QT, minute volume and respiratory rate) but at minimal 5-fold higher clinical dose. These events were transient, dose-independent and have been observed in clinical studies.

Paltusotine was demonstrated to be poorly metabolized and circulated mainly in the blood as unchanged, was  mostly  eliminated  in  the  form  of  glucuronide  conjugates  via  the  bile,  had  the  same  metabolic pathways in animals and humans, and accumulated moderately after repeat dose. The small number of animals used each time, and the large intra- and inter-assay variability did not allow for quantitate evaluation of relative bioavailability. Even if many data were presented, they were difficult to compare since the experimental conditions were always different in terms of formulation (presence of ethanol, propylene glycol, vitamin E, HCl,), salt (di HCl, HCl, dimesylate), food regimen (fasted, not fasted, food ad libitum), dose etc.

Exposures reached at highest doses in 26-week rat and 39-week dog studies were 6.9- and 25-fold higher, respectively, than the anticipated steady state clinical AUC exposure at 60 mg. Toxicokinetic analysis in rats and dogs showed peak and total systemic exposures of paltusotine increased in a dose- dependent manner over the dose range. Conversely, AUC values did not show time-dependency: in both 13- and 26-weeks toxicity studies in rats and in 13- and 39-weeks studies in dogs, exposure values decrease  from  day  1  to  the  last  day  of  treatment.  The  non-clinical  safety  program  was  conducted according to ICH guideline M3, which was acceptable. The toxicity profile determined in rats and in dogs revealed no specific organs toxicity. Non-adverse decreases in body weight and body weight gain at all doses tested were observed in rats and in dogs in addition to the clinical observation of emesis and abnormal stool observed in dogs. These findings were considered non-adverse and could be explained by paltusotine pharmacological activity. NOAEL in rats and in dogs were determined at the highest tested dose 500 mg/kg/day in rats and 75 mg/kg/day in dogs which correspond to 6.5- and 25-fold the clinical exposure.  The  multiple  of  exposure  in  rats  measured  in  26-week  study  at  D182  were  lower  than expected. Rat exposures treated at 500 mg/kg/day were not coherent between studies, the apparent variability  could  be  inherent  to  the  physicochemical  properties  of  paltusotine,  characterised  by  low solubility and permeability.  To conclude, animal/human margins of exposure calculated in the whole dossier were taken in consideration with caution, given the large variability of paltusotine bioavailability (see animal PK part of AR).

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

A standard genotoxicity battery was conducted according to ICH guideline S2. The AMES test did not detect a mutagenicity potential. In vitro micronucleus test was considered as equivocal. The in  vivo micronucleus test was negative. Paltusotine was considered to be a non-genotoxic compound.

No  carcinogenicity  potential  was  detected  in  26-week  study  in  transgenic  mice  (up  to  2.8-fold  the exposure at the MHRD) and in 2-year study in rats (up to 33-fold the exposure at the MHRD).

The package of developmental and reproductive toxicity studies in rats and rabbits was in line with the regulatory  requirement  applicable  to  the  current  marketing  authorization.  In  rats,  there  were  no treatment-related effects on fertility indices, male reproductive organs and sperm parameters at up to 500  mg/kg/day  (16.7-fold  human  exposure),  whereas  decreased  numbers  of  corpora  lutea  and implantations with increased preimplantation loss contributed to a decrease in the number of foetuses. These conclusions are reported in SmPC sections 4.6 and 5.3. In embryofoetal development studies no treatment-related malformations or embryofoetal lethality were shown in rats and rabbits at dose levels inducing  exposure  levels  up  to  11.5-fold  and  5.8-fold,  respectively,  human  exposure.  In  rabbits, abortions related to drug-induced body weight loss and markedly decreased food intake during the dose period were seen at the high dose level. The developmental NOAEL was 2.9-fold human exposure based on decreased foetal weight in that species. In the pre- and postnatal development toxicity study, there were no effects on sexual maturation, neurobehavioral or reproductive function in the F1 generation rats. The developmental NOAEL was determined on the basis of a decrease in F1 body weight during the pre-  and  postweaning  periods  at  500  mg/kg/day  (6.1-fold  human  exposure  levels).  Toxicokinetic parameters measured on LD20 showed excretion of paltusotine in maternal milk, as well as systemic exposure of F1 pups (0.1-0.8% of maternal exposure, lowest exposure could be also due to decreased maternal milk intake from PND 14/15 in rats). The absence of any juvenile animal study was acceptable to support marketing of a product which would only be used in adult patients.

The  specified  and  identified  impurities  in  drug  substance  and  drug  product  above  the  qualification thresholds as per ICH Q3A and ICH Q3B, respectively, were qualified in two 28-day repeat-dose toxicity studies in rats. The impurities tested are the following: N-isopropyl, O-isopropyl, 808-Imp 9 (study 4240163-TX) and 808-Imp 4, 808-Imp-G, 808-Imp-I, 808-Imp 3, 808-Imp 5, 808-Imp 6, 808-Imp 10 (study 424-0157-TX).

No phototoxic potential was determined for paltusotine. However, retina degeneration was observed in the in vivo phototoxicity study. Although the link between paltusotine administration and this adverse effect is not clear, the Applicant has set up an ophthalmologic monitoring assessment in clinical trials of

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

6- month duration or longer to assess any potential clinical relevance in humans. No further animal study was requested as clinical experience is available (see clinical safety part).

The  available  data  do  not  allow  to  conclude  definitively  on  the  potential  risk  of  paltusotine  to  the environment. However, the CHMP has agreed that the ERA will be updated, when a fish full lifecycle test will be conducted ( OCDE 240 guideline ) and its results will be submitted in the post-approval setting.

## 4.5.2. Conclusions

From a non-clinical point of view, the application is approvable with the proposed ERA post-approval measure.

## 5. Clinical aspects

## 5.1. Introduction

## 5.1.1. Good Clinical Practice (GCP) aspects

The clinical trials were performed in accordance with GCP as claimed by the applicant

The applicant has provided a statement to the effect that clinical trials conducted outside the community were carried out in accordance with the ethical standards of Directive 2001/20/EC.

A request for GCP inspection has been adopted for the following clinical study CRN00808-09. The inspection covered two investigational sites and the site of the sponsor. The Integrated Inspection Report was provided on 13 November 2025.

Based on the review of clinical data and the above-mentioned reports, CHMP did not identify the need for a further GCP inspection of the clinical trials included in this dossier (see section 2.4.3).

## 5.2. Clinical pharmacology

The clinical pharmacology program for Paltusotine encompasses 17 completed clinical studies, 12 performed in healthy volunteers (see below), 3 Phase 2 in patients with acromegaly ( CRN00808-02 , CRN00808-03 , CRN00808-05 ) and two pivotal Phase 3 studies ( CRN00808-08 and CRN0080809 ):

- One SAD/MAD, DDI, food effect study ( CRN00808-01 )
- One mass balance and metabolism study ( CRN00808-06 )
- Five biopharmaceutical studies investigating rBA, food or pH effect ( CRN00808-01 , CRN00808-04 , CRN00808-07 , CRN00808-13 , CRN00808-20 )
- Four studies investigating intrinsic factors ( CRN00808-10 , CRN00808-18 , PA0001 , PA0002 )
- Two DDI studies ( CRN00808-01 , CRN00808-15 )
- A QT/QTc study ( CRN00808-14 ).

## 5.2.1. Methods

## Bioanalysis

Six validated bioanalytical methods were used during clinical development to quantify paltusotine in human plasma by LC-MS/MS techniques. The methods are considered adequate and comply with the acceptance criteria of the bioanalytical method validation of EMEA/CHMP/EWP/192217/2009 Rev. 1 Corr. 2 or of ICHM10 on bioanalytical method validation and study sample analysis .

## Pharmacokinetic analysis

For single or multiple-dose studies, PK parameters evaluated in plasma include Cmax, Cmin, Tmax, AUCs (AUC0-t, AUC0-24, AUCtau, AUC0∞), CL, CL/F, V, Vd/F and Vz/F, AUC%extra, AR (accumulation ratio), λz, T1/2.

For the mass balance study, additional PK parameters as Aeu, feu, Aef, fef and CLR were calculated.

Standard non-compartmental (model-independent) pharmacokinetic methods were used to calculate PK parameters using Phoenix WinNonlin v8.1 or later.

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

## Pharmacodynamic Methods

In a phase III study conducted outside Japan, subjects took SDD tablets one hour before meals after overnight fasting. In this study, when paltusotine was administered immediately after a meal, it was confirmed  that  the  exposure  was  significantly  reduced  for  both  capsules  and  SDD  tablets,  and  the exposure was reduced even when the capsule was given 2 hours after a meal. Based on the above, the timing of administration of paltusotine was decided to be 1 hour before breakfast and 3 hours after breakfast.

The effects of paltusotine 40 mg QD on  serum IGF-1 concentration were evaluated under 2 dosing conditions: administration of paltusotine at 3 hours after a standard meal vs 1 hour before a standard meal.

Pharmacodynamic blood samples were collected for the determination of IGF-1 in serum at specified timepoints as follows: Day -1 (prior to dosing start), Days 1 to 6 pre-dose and at 12 hours post-dose; and on Day 7 pre-dose and at 12, 24, 96, and 168 hours post-dose.

Paltusotine can reduce blood levels of GH and IGF-1 via selective action on SST2. However, serum GH is difficult to detect in healthy adults because it has a short half-life and is secreted cyclically, primarily at night. There is also a method of temporarily increasing the serum GH concentration and evaluating it, but from ethical considerations, only IGF-1 was to be measured in this study.

## 5.2.2. Pharmacokinetics

## 5.2.2.1. Introduction

Overall, the clinical pharmacokinetics of paltusotine has been described and substantiated adequately with the submitted clinical package.

## 5.2.2.2. Evaluation and qualification of models

## 5.2.2.2.1. Population pharmacokinetics

A population PK analysis was developed (crn00808-pop-pk-rpt) from which predicted exposure metrics were used as input for two exposure-response (ER) analysis (crn00808-er-rpt).

The population dataset was based on paltusotine PK data from 13 clinical studies (rich data from 11 Phase 1 studies in healthy volunteers, and sparse data essentially Ctrough from the 2 pivotal phase 3 studies CRN00808-08 and CRN00808-09 in patients with acromegaly using the proposed SDD tablets) that evaluated the oral solution or commercial SDD tablet formulations of paltusotine. Data from capsule formulations (not of interest, except for ER analysis) and data from doses less than 20 mg or greater than 160 mg (outside the clinically relevant dose range of 20-60 mg) were excluded from the analysis.

Covariates of interest were baseline demographic covariates (age, body weight, gender, race/ethnicity, renal function (eGFR) and UGT1A1 polymorphisms) and extrinsic factors as the formulation, mealtime, meal type and concomitant PPI.

NLME  methodology  was  used  to  develop  the  paltusotine  Pop-PK  model.  The  first-order  conditional estimation (FOCE) method was used as the default. Various disposition (2-, 3-, and 4-compartment) and absorption models (e.g. mixed order absorption) were assessed. PK parameters were assumed to follow a log-normal distribution. A full model approach was implemented by simultaneously incorporating all covariate-parameter relationships of interest. Following construction and successful estimation of the full model, a backward elimination process was performed, where covariates were deleted singly from the full model based on change in OFV. Standard diagnostic plots were generated, and the final model was qualified using visual predictive check, quantitative predictive checks, and bootstrapping.

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

## 5.2.2.2.2. Physiology based pharmacokinetic model

Physiologically based pharmacokinetic (PBPK) modelling was performed to inform on DDI and to evaluate the effect of renal impairment on Paltusotine PK (crn00808-pbpk-rpt-crin-1d).

## 5.2.2.3. Absorption

## Healthy volunteers

Following single dose of paltusotine with the SSD tablet (1h post-dose fasting) from 20 mg to a 60 mg dose, geometric mean Cmax and AUC24h ranged from 80.7 to 235 ng/mL and 651 to 2350 ng.h/mL ( CRN00808-07 ). Tmax was generally achieved 1 to 4h regardless of post-dose duration.

Following multiple doses of paltusotine with the SDD tablet at a 60 mg QD dose geometric mean, Cmax was 290 ng/mL and AUC of 2890 ng.h/mL.

## Patients

Following multiple doses of paltusotine QD (SDD tablet) from 20 mg to 60 mg, geometric mean Ctrough ranged from 51.3 to 79.8 ng/mL ( CRN00808-08 ).

Following multiple doses of paltusotine QD (SDD tablet) at doses of 40 mg or 60 mg, dose normalized concentrations collected 2h post-dose at steady-state was 4.51 ng/mL/mg dose ( CRN00808-05 ).

Based on in vitro investigations, paltusotine is a substrate of P-gp and BCRP.

## Absolute bioavailability

An average absolute bioavailability of paltusotine (oral solution) was estimated at 70% with individual values ranging from 59% to 82%. For the SDD tablet, absolute bioavailability of paltusotine was estimated at 51%.

## BCS classification

Based on in vitro investigations on solubility and permeability, paltusotine (HCl.SSD) can be classified as a BCS class 3 compound (high solubility/low permeability).

## Influence of food

A  food  effect  on  paltusotine  PK  was  demonstrated.  With  a  high  fat-meal  both  Cmax  and  AUC24h decreased by 81% and 85%, respectively ( CRN00808-07 ). With a low fat-meal both paltusotine Cmax and AUCt decreased by approximately 72% and 68%, respectively ( CRN00808-07 ).

## Influence of gastric modifiers

A dose dependent decreases of paltusotine exposure (AUC and Cmax) by 20% and 40% was observed following a 20 mg or a 60 mg dose ( CRN00808-07 ).

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

## 5.2.2.4. Bioequivalence

## Relative bioavailability/Bioequivalence

Several oral formulations of paltusotine were developed and evaluated during the clinical development program:

- -An  aqueous  solution  containing  paltusotine.HCl  which  was  optimized  to  provide  appropriate solubility  ( Form 1 ).  This  solution  contained  65  w/w%  Ora-sweet  flavoring  syrup,  25  w/w% propylene glycol and 10 w/w% Dα -tocopherol polyethylene glycol succinate
- -An IR capsule containing a powder blend of paltusotine.HCl manufactured by hot melt granulation (HMG) ( Form 2 ). The final blend was filled into Swedish orange gelatine capsules and supplied at 5, 10 and 20 mg strengths.
- -An IR uncoated tablet containing paltusotine dimesylate salt (paltusotine.2MSA) manufactured by direct compression ( Form 3 ). One strength was developed at 10 mg.
- -An IR film-coated tablet containing paltusotine.HCl formulated as an amorphous spary-dried dispersion (SDD) with stabilizing polymer copovidone ( Form 4 ). The SDD composition is 15:85 paltusotine:copovidone for a nominal SDD drug load of 15%. Two strengths were developed at 20 mg and 30 mg.
- -A Form 5 was also developed and consisted of Form 4 with a 35% SDD drug load and used solely in the rBA study CRN00808-20 .

Form 4 is  the  proposed commercial formulation and have been used in the Phase 3 pivotal studies ( CRN00808-08/09 ).

Bridging between Form 1 vs Form 2

Between Form 1 (R) and Form 2 (T), Form 2 has a rBA of 0.689 ( CRN00808-01 ).

Bridging between Form 2 vs Form 4

Between Form 2 (R) and Form 4 (T), Form 4 has a rBA of 0.825 ( CRN00808-07 ).

Bridging between Form 4 20 mg vs 30 mg

Both strengths are BE, with GMR (CI90%) for Cmax and AUCt of 100% (84.4-118.5) and 98.8% (84.2115.9), respectively ( CRN00808-13 ).

## 5.2.2.5. Distribution

Paltusotine has a &gt;99% and &gt;98% protein binding on albumin and AAG, respectively, a B/P close to 1 and has a high distribution in tissue with a Vz/F estimated at 406 L.

## 5.2.2.6. Metabolism

Metabolite profiling was performed, and 4 metabolites were identified in total excreta and none in plasma (figure 1).

In plasma pooled sample (AUC48h), the unchanged parent paltusotine accounted for 100% of TRA.

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

In faeces, paltusotine was the main component excreted with 39.7% of the dose, followed by M632/1 (20.4%), M472/1 (7.92%). M648/1 and M676/1 accounted for less than 5% of TRA. 9.5% of the dose excreted in faeces was not identified, thus more than 83% of the dose excreted in faeces was identified.

In urine, unchanged paltusotine was detected at a low level (1.33%). M632/1 accounted for 1.44% of the  dose  and  other  metabolites  less  than  0.5%.  More  than  84%  of  the  dose  excreted  in urine  was identified.

Paltusotine is extensively metabolized mainly through glucuronidation via UGT1A1 and to a lesser extent by 1A9, and oxidation via CYP3A4 mainly and to a lesser extent CYP2D6.

Figure 1 Proposed metabolic scheme for Paltusotine in human subjects

<!-- image -->

Gluc = glucuronic acid

## 5.2.2.7. Elimination

Across the clinical studies in healthy volunteers, after a single dose of paltusotine from 20 to 120 mg geometric mean half-life ranged from 22 to 30 hours. Based on the PPK analysis, CL/F was estimated at 11 L/h.

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

In the ADME study CRN00808-06 following a 20 mg dose of paltusotine as an oral solution, mean T1/2 was 26h, CL/F was 8.4 L/h. Paltusotine is highly metabolized and cleared mainly by metabolism and faecal excretion.

## · Mass balance

The excretion and biotransformation of [ 14 C]-paltusotine was investigated in 6 healthy subjects following a single oral dose of 20 mg paltusotine (solution) incorporating approximately 80 µCi [ 14 C]paltusotine.

The  arithmetic  mean  (±SD)  cumulative  recovery  from  faeces  and  urine  was  94  %  (±  1.1)  of  the administered radioactive dose with recovery ranging from 92 to 95 %.

Faecal excretion was the predominant route of elimination, with arithmetic mean recovery of TRA of 90% (± 2.2) and 3.9 (± 1.2) in urine. Unchanged paltusotine was recovered at 1.33% and 39.7 % in urine and faeces respectively. Most of the administered radioactivity (90%) was recovered in the first 120 h post-dose.

## 5.2.2.8. Dose proportionality and time dependency

Dose proportionality was demonstrated after a single paltusotine dose between 20 to 120 mg in healthy volunteers. Steady-state is expected to be reached by 7 days. At a 60 mg dose the accumulation ratio for Cmax and AUCt was 1.31 and 1.55 respectively.

## 5.2.2.9. Pharmacokinetics in the target population

Five clinical studies investigated paltusotine PK in patients with acromegaly following phase 2 (studies CRN00808-02/03/05 ) or phase 3 studies (studies CRN00808-08/09 ).

In  study CRN00808-08 (pivotal  study,  naïve  or  uncontrolled  participants),  participants  received  a starting dose of paltusotine 20 mg QD (or matching placebo) as tablet ( Form 4, 20 mg strength only ), and their dose could be titrated up to 60 mg QD through Week 24 based on biochemical status (IGF-1 level) and tolerability. Down-titration was allowed based on tolerability.

## Population Pharmacokinetic analysis (Pop-PK)

A  Pop-PK  analysis  (Pop-PK,  CRNT-Proj1-23-0005)  aiming  to  characterize  the  PK  of  paltusotine  and identify / quantify sources of variability was developed. Model-based steady state exposure metrics were predicted at in the target population and used for subsequent E-R analysis for efficacy and safety.

Overall, 5560 observations from 316 subjects were included. All post-dose BLQ (n= 118, 3.2% of total observations &lt;5%) were excluded from the analysis. This was found to be acceptable. Two outliers (|CWRES|&gt;6) were identified and removed later resulting in a total of 315 subjects with 5558 paltusotine concentrations available.

The full model estimated clinically meaningful effects for the tablet formulation, mealtime, concomitant proton pump inhibitor (PPI) use, and sex, with each significantly impacting paltusotine bioavailability. The effects of meal type, race, body weight, UGT1A1 metabolizer status, eGFR, and cholecystectomy status were not clinically or statistically significant.

The final model consisted of a linear 3-compartment disposition model with first-order absorption (Ka), a relative BA for the tablet formulation (Ftab) and separate absorption lag times for oral solution and tablet formulations. IIV was estimated for CL, V2, Ka, tlags, and Ftab, IOV was included on Ftab and Ka and the model used a proportional residual error. Population estimates of paltusotine CL/F and Vss/F were approximately 24.2 L/h (9.41 /0.389 [38.8% being Ftab of the SDD tablet given with a meal at 1 h meal postdose, equivalent to absolute BA of 27.2% (0.389×70% the absolute AB of the oral solution) and 598 L (232.5 L/0.389). Ka was estimated at 0.317 h-1 and tlag was slightly longer for the SDD tablet relative to the oral solution (approximately 12 min vs 8 min). The proportional RUV was reasonable estimated at 21.1%.

## 5.2.2.10. Special populations

## Renal impairment

No formal dedicated PK study was performed to investigate the effect of renal impairment on paltusotine PK given the minor contribution of the renal route for paltusotine elimination (only 3.9% of TRA recovered in urine with 1.33% as unchanged paltusotine). This was further supported by results from the PBPK modelling approach. Relative to the healthy control group, the GMR of predicted steady state AUC0-t and Cmax across all groups of renal impairment and dose levels increased by &lt;16%, indicating a minimal impact of renal impairment.

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

Subjects included in the Pop-PK analysis were characterized with regard to renal status using the eGFR metric  (calculated  using  CKD-EPI  method)  with  a  mean  eGFR  of  99  mL/min/1.73m 2 ,  median  100 mL/min/1.73m 2 , min max 52-144 mL/min/1.73m 2  in the target patients with acromegaly. However, the exact numbers and proportions of subjects in each category of renal impairment was unknown. The effect of renal function was investigated as a continuous covariate within the Pop-PK analysis and was not identified to have a significant effect on the PK of paltusotine.

## Hepatic impairment

A  formal  PK  study  ( CRN00808-10 ),  investigating  the  effect  of  mild,  moderate  and  severe  hepatic impairment on paltusotine PK was performed. Results of this analysis indicated that following a 20 mg single dose, hepatic impairment had no effect on paltusotine PK.

Data of interest  from  mild/moderate and severe hepatic impairment arms (n= 22 subjects, 418 PK samples from Study CRN00808-10) was excluded from the Pop-PK analysis. No conclusion regarding the effect of hepatic impairment on the PK of paltusotine could be drawn from the population approach.

## Gender

No formal dedicated PK study was performed has been performed. This was accepted.

The effect of gender was investigated using the Pop-PK analysis. Even though female participants showed higher paltusotine oral bioavailability, this results in a moderate 22% higher AUCss relative to males, with a 95CI of GMR of [1.18-1.26], indicating no significant clinical impact on paltusotine PK exposures.

## Ethnicity

A  formal  PK  study  ( CRN00808-18 )  investigating  the  PK  of  single  and  multiple  doses  of  40  mg  of paltusotine in Chinese healthy subjects was performed.  After a single oral dose of 40 mg paltusotine in Chinese healthy volunteers, Tmax was reached at 1.5h, with a geometric mean Cmax of 238 ng/mL and AUC24h of 1700 ng.h/mL. At steady state, Tmax was reached at 1.5h, with a geometric mean Cmax of 311 ng/mL and AUC24h of 2950 ng.h/mL. Accumulation ratio for Cmax and AUC were 1.42 and 1.84 respectively.

A formal PK study ( PA0001 ) investigating the PK of single ascending dose of paltusotine (20 to 80 mg) in healthy adult Japanese subjects was performed. At the paltusotine 40 mg dose level, Cmax and AUC0-inf were higher and tmax was shorter in the Japanese participants relative to the Caucasian participants. The GMRs of Cmax, AUC0-last, and AUC0-inf for Caucasian to Japanese participants in the paltusotine 40 mg group were 38.2%, 50.7%, and 51.7%, respectively.

In the Pop-PK analysis, the analysed dataset included a majority of White subjects (n= 195 of 316, 62%), while n= 75 were Asian (23.7%) and n= 45 were neither Asian nor White (14.2%, classified as other race). Race effect was investigated as a categorical covariate (Asian or other race versus White) and did not have a meaningful effect on the exposure of paltusotine.

## Weight

No formal PK study with regard to weight has been performed.

The BW effect was investigated within the Pop-PK analysis. The mean BW of the 119 patients with acromegaly analyzed was 83 kg (median = 81 kg, min = 45 kg, max = 138 kg). Compared to a reference BW of 70 kg, patients with a BW of 55 kg (5th percentile) is expected to have a marginal increase of AUCss by approximately 4% (&lt;5%); and similarly, patients with a BW of 106 (5th percentile) kg is expected to have minimal decrease on AUCss by 4.7%, with 95% CI of GMR at [1.01- 1.08] and [0.920.99], respectively, both within the range of [0.8-1.25] indicating no clinically meaningful impact on PKs of paltusotine.

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

## Age

No formal investigations in elderly have been performed. The effect of age (as a continuous covariate) on the PKs of paltusotine was investigated within the Pop-PK analysis (290 participants aged 18-65 years and 25 elderly participants, including 20 aged 65-74 years, 5 aged 75-84 years and none above 85 years) and was not considered clinically meaningful.

## Paediatric population

No clinical / PK data are available in children below 18 years. The safety and efficacy of paltusotine in the paediatric population have not been established.

## 5.2.2.11. Pharmacokinetic interaction studies

Based on in vitro investigation, a clinically significant interaction of paltusotine mediated by CYP3A4 and CYP2D6 inhibition, P-gp, BCRP inhibition could not be excluded. Although in vitro paltusotine inhibited MATE1/2K inhibition, the DDI study conducted with metformin refuted the in vitro findings.

To assess paltusotine DDIs, clinicals studies were conducted and a PBPK model was used predict the interaction magnitude. No major deficiencies in the conducted clinical DDI studies were observed. The PBPK  model  tends  to  under-predict  Cmax  following  single  doses  of  40  and  60  mg  by  20%  to approximately 40 % (range 19% - 39%) which was consistent with the observed 32% underprediction of  Cmax,ss  following  multiple  administration,  without  major  misperception  of  exposure  (AUC0-inf). Overall, the model predictions of paltusotine PK were considered acceptable, except for predictions of paltusotine interactions with strong CYP3A4 inhibitors. See the clinical pharmacology discussion.

## Paltusotine as victim drug

Clinically relevant interactions are expected to be limited due to the relatively mild contribution of CYP3A4 (fm CYP3A4 14.3%) in the paltusotine metabolism.

The metabolism of paltusotine is mainly mediated by glucuronidation (fm UGTs = 0.38) and mostly by UGT1A1 (fm ~ 0.29).  Based on a worst-case scenario, it is not expected that a paltusotine drug increase following UGT1A1 should significantly affect UGT1A1 drug substrate as these are, mostly, not NTI drugs.

In a clinical study conducted in healthy patients (Study CRN00808-15), paltusotine co-administration with carbamazepine, a strong UGAT1A1, CYP3A4, and P-gp inducer, led to a clinically significant decrease in paltusotine exposure by 70% (90% CI AUC GMR [0.26-0.35]) and a decrease in Cmax by 64% (90% CI Cmax GMR [0.45-0.70]). Therefore, in case of concomitant treatment with UGT1A1 inducer, the paltusotine dose should be increased.

Cyclosporine increased paltusotine exposure by almost two-fold (GMR AUC = 1.89 90% CI AUC GMR [1.65-2.17]) and led to a two-fold increase in Cmax (90% CI Cmax GMR [1.66-2.41]) in healthy subjects (study CRN00808-07 cohort 3). The SmPC was aligned accordingly.

## Paltusotine as perpetrator

The drug-drug interactions study results conducted in healthy subjects (study CRN00808-07 cohort 4) showed paltusotine did not significantly inhibit CYP3A4 with an increase in midazolam exposure by 13% (90% CI in AUC GMR [1.03-1.25]).

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

Paltusotine decreased metformin exposure by 22% (90% CI AUC GMR [0.67-0.90]) and Cmax by 39% (90% CI Cmax GMR [0.54-0.70]) (study CRN00808-07 cohort 2), which was not consistent with paltusotine inhibition of MATE 1/2K observed in vitro .

When co-administered with paltusotine 40 mg in healthy subjects (Study CRN00808-07 cohort 3), the reduced exposure in cyclosporine by 35 % (90% CI AUC GMR [0.56-0.75]) and reduced Cmax by 53% (90% CI Cmax GMR [0.42-0.54]) was also observed with somatostatin analogues.

## 5.2.3. Pharmacodynamics

## 5.2.3.1. Mechanism of action

Similar to the natural hormone somatostatin (SST), paltusotine demonstrates potent suppression of GH and IGF-1 secretion in healthy volunteers and in patients with acromegaly. As an agonist, paltusotine exerts its pharmacological activity via highly selective binding to SST2 and exhibits little or no affinity for other SST receptor subtypes or other receptors.

Paltusotine potently inhibited cyclic adenosine monophosphate (cAMP) accumulation via human SST2 activation with an average drug (agonist) concentration that results in EC50 of 0.25 nM. The paltusotine selectivity over other human SST receptors was &gt;4000-fold, potentially reducing the risk of off-target activity.

Table 9 Selectivity of paltusotine toward SST receptor subtypes

|             | Human SST EC 50 (nM)   | Human SST EC 50 (nM)   | Human SST EC 50 (nM)   | Human SST EC 50 (nM)   | Human SST EC 50 (nM)   |
|-------------|------------------------|------------------------|------------------------|------------------------|------------------------|
| Compound    | SST1                   | SST2                   | SST3                   | SST4                   | SST5                   |
| Paltusotine | >10000                 | 0.25                   | 3300                   | 1100                   | >10000                 |
| SS14        | 0.83                   | 0.14                   | 0.16                   | 0.20                   | 0.065                  |

EC50=Effective concentration resulting in 50% inhibition of cAMP, SS14=native somatostatin-14, SST=somatostatin Paltusotine was evaluated for its ability to cause internalization and desensitization of receptors.

In  studies  with  hemagglutinin-tagged  human  SST2,  paltusotine  was  less  potent  at  internalization (EC50=19 nM) and exhibited a larger bias ratio (approximately 10-fold higher than native somatostatin), which may translate to a low propensity for tachyphylaxis.

For comparison, paltusotine is more specific to SSRT2 than the other SRLs.

The primary PD effect of paltusotine has been evaluated in 2 phase 1 healthy participants in study CRN00808-01 (IGF-1 and GH) and study PA0002 (IGF-1 only) which demonstrated that paltusotine PD activity is consistent with literature that describes the pharmacological activity of SST agonists. Indeed, paltusotine demonstrated suppression of stimulated GH secretion and rapid and sustained decline in serum IGF-1 concentrations, resulting in target engagement for paltusotine.

Proof of concept in healthy participants informed the 3 phase 2 and 2 placebo-controlled phase 3 studies of  paltusotine  in  participants  with  acromegaly.  In  these  studies,  clinically  and  statistically  significant suppression of IGF-1 and GH and a reduction of acromegaly symptoms were demonstrated with oncedaily oral paltusotine doses of 40 mg and 60 mg.

## 5.2.3.2. Primary and secondary pharmacology

## Primary pharmacology

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

1) Study CRN00808-01 was a single and multiple ascending doses study which included a PD objective to determine the effect on serum IGF-1 and growth-hormone releasing hormone (GHRH) analog-stimulated GH levels after single or multiple ascending doses of paltusotine or placebo.

91 participants were enrolled and randomized for the study.

In the single dose cohorts, all randomized subjects who received any amount of study drug (CRN00808 or placebo), who had results from baseline and from ≥ 1 post-baseline PD assessment, were included in the PD population (n=39).

In the multiple dose cohorts, the PD population included 21 (87.5%) subjects in receipt of CRN00808 and 10 (83.3%) placebo subjects.

All subjects in the single-dose and multiple-dose cohorts were male.

The  PD  activities  of  paltusotine  on  IGF-1  and  GH  levels  were  assessed  in  single-dose  cohorts  of paltusotine oral solution at 1.25 mg (Cohort S1), 2.5 mg (Cohort S2), 5 mg (Cohort S3), 10 mg (Cohort S4 Period A), and 20 mg (Cohort S5) vs placebo. There were also single-dose cohorts of paltusotine capsules at 40 mg (S6) and 60 mg (S7) vs placebo.

The PD activities were also assessed in the multiple-dose cohorts of paltusotine capsule administered at 5 mg for 7 days (Cohort M1; IGF-1 and GH) and 10 mg (Cohort M2), 20 mg (Cohort M3), or 30 mg (Cohort M4) for 10 days (IGF-1 only) vs placebo.

The pharmacodynamic activity of paltusotine was assessed in the single-dose cohorts (Cohorts S1 to S5 only) through a GHRH challenge, where GHRH-stimulated GH levels in serum were measured.

Serum GH can be difficult to detect in healthy volunteers because GH has a short half-life and is secreted in a pulsatile fashion. Therefore, a GHRH challenge was performed to cause a reproducible temporary elevation of serum GH and assess the ability of CRN00808 to suppress stimulated GH secretion.

Serum IGF-1 levels as a measure of PD parameters were also assessed in single-dose cohorts (Cohorts S1 to S5 only).

Suppression of GHRH-stimulated GH secretion occurred for all single-dose 1.25 mg to 20 mg paltusotine oral solutions tested. Suppression of GH was observed at all dose levels tested in the single-dose range of  5  mg  to  20  mg  QD  paltusotine  oral  solution.  This  response  was  dose  dependent,  reaching  nearmaximal inhibition of GH suppression at the 10 mg dose (91% suppression of stimulated GH secretion).

Similar effects have been observed with other peptide SST agonists. Minimal changes in GH secretion were observed following GHRH challenge in placebo subjects on both Day-1 and Day 1. Reductions in mean GH AUC (0-2 hours) percent change of baseline levels were significant compared to placebo for all CRN00808 single-dose levels &gt;1.25 mg.

No changes in serum IGF-1 levels were observed following single-dose administration of paltusotine over the assessment period, regardless of dose level. The lack of observed change in serum IGF-1 following single-dosing was expected. In both acromegaly patients and healthy volunteers, sustained suppression of GH results in lowering of serum IGF-1 levels. In contrast to the rapid effects of the GH response, IGF1 levels are known to change more gradually and continuous exposure to somatostatin agonists for several days is generally required to produce an observable reduction.

In the multiple cohorts, the reduction in GH post-dose did not translate to changes in mean GH AUC (024  hours)  percent  change  of  baseline  on  Day  1.  The  median  GH  AUC0-24  change  from  baseline  in participants receiving daily doses of 5 mg paltusotine for 7 days was significantly lower than the placebo at day 7. The other doses of paltusotine were not investigated as regards to the assessment of GH levels.

No changes in serum IGF-1 levels were observed following single-dose administration of paltusotine over the assessment period, regardless of dose level.

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

Administration of CRN00808 in the multiple-dose cohorts did not result in any change from baseline in mean IGF-1 AUC (0-24 hours) on Day 1. However, suppression of IGF-1 was observed with the 10 mg to 30 mg QD paltusotine oral solution dose range, and mean IGF-1 changes from baseline were between -26.8% to -36.4% on 10 days after daily dosing of 10, 20 or 30 mg CRN00808, compared to +10.6% change of baseline levels with placebo. The percentage decrease in IGF-1 AUC (0-24 hours) from baseline at Day 10 was statistically significant in all active cohorts (M2 (10 mg), M3 (20 mg) and M4 (30 mg)) compared to placebo. The differences between CRN00808 and placebo were -41.9 (-64.1; 19.7), -47.0 (-70.4; -23.7), -37.4 (-60.7; -14.0) for the M2, M3 and M4 active cohorts respectively.

As CRN00808 concentrations reached steady state, serum IGF-1 concentrations began to decline. This decline reached steady state in approximately seven days. IGF-1 remained suppressed for approximately 3 to 4 days after the final dose and began to recover as CRN00808 plasma concentrations fell.

The PD effect of paltusotine on GH and IGF-1 in healthy participants is consistent with the literature that describes the pharmacological activity of somatostatin agonists. Significant suppression of IGF-1 was not observed for the dose of paltusotine 5 mg vs placebo at day 7.

This suggests that drug concentrations that result in maximal suppression of IGF-1 in healthy volunteers would  translate  to  meaningful  suppression  of  IGF-1  in  acromegaly  patients.  Of  note,  although  the investigators attempted to enrol both male and female participants, all participants were men, the great majority of whom were under 30 years of age.

2) Study PA0002 was a phase 1, single-centre, open-label, multiple-dose, 2-period crossover study in healthy Japanese adult male participants designed to assess the PK, PD, safety, and food effects of paltusotine in healthy Japanese participants.

Twelve healthy adult Japanese males (22 to 37 years of age) were assigned to 2 groups to receive daily oral dosing of paltusotine 40 (2×20) mg (15% drug load) SDD tablets in 2 study cycles.

Following 7-day dosing of paltusotine 40 mg QD, similar decreases in serum IGF-1 were observed in healthy Japanese adult participants when administered at 3 hours after a meal or 1 hour prior to a meal.

Under both dosing conditions, similar reductions in mean serum IGF-1 concentrations were observed on Day  7.  Serum  IGF-1  concentrations  were  decreased  by  approximately  60  to  66  ng/mL  relative  to baseline.

In conclusion, these 2 phase 1 studies demonstrated a rapid dose-dependent suppression of stimulated GH  and  IGF-1  secretion,  observed  as  early  as  Day  1,  with  sustained  decline  in  serum  IGF-1 concentrations  observed  once  paltusotine  steady  state  was  achieved  at  Day  7.  Maximal  IGF-1 suppression reached its nadir in approximately 7 days. Unlike the rapid suppression of GH, IGF-1 levels decrease gradually and require 4 to 10 days of exposure to SST agonists to produce an observable effect. The gradual decrease of IGF-1 is likely due to its long t1/2 (approximately 15 h), which is due to high affinity IGF-1 binding proteins in plasma.

The degree of IGF-1 suppression observed in study CRN00808-01 compared to placebo (approximately 37-47%) by multiple doses of paltusotine was similar to that observed for peptide somatostatin analogs (octreotide, lanreotide) in previously reported healthy volunteer studies.

The  distinction  however  is  that  paltusotine  achieves  a  steady  state  within  7  days,  whereas  depot injections of other SRLs treatments require several weeks or months to reach steady state and dose optimization with paltusotine is possible in much shorter time. Unlike the rapid suppression of GH, IGF1  levels  decrease  gradually  and  require  4  to  10  days  of  exposure  to  SST  agonists  to  produce  an observable effect. The gradual decrease of IGF-1 is likely due to its long t1/2 (approximately 15 h), which is due to high affinity IGF-1 binding proteins in plasma. After the final paltusotine dose, IGF-1 remained  suppressed  for  several  days  but  began  to  recover  as  paltusotine  plasma  concentrations declined.  Analogous  to  the  observation  that  it  takes  several  days  to  achieve  the  nadir  of  IGF-1 suppression with once-a-day dosing of paltusotine, it takes several days for IGF-1 to recover back to baseline levels upon paltusotine withdrawal.

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

## Secondary pharmacology

According to clinical and non-clinical data, paltusotine normalizes excess hormone secretion of GH by tumour cells to moderate the clinical signs and symptoms associated with acromegaly, through selective binding to SST2. Excess GH levels result in increased secretion of IGF-1 from the liver and other organs.

Paltusotine exerts its agonist activity to moderate excess hormone secretion via highly selective binding to SST2, thereby reducing clinical signs and symptoms with minimal off-target toxicities from the liver and other organs.

Based  on  the  results  of  the  clinical  program,  the  safety  profile  of  paltusotine  includes  ADRs  of gastrointestinal events (diarrhoea, abdominal  pain, nausea, and abdominal  discomfort), sinus bradycardia, and cholelithiasis, consistent with other SRL treatments. These AEs are generally mild or moderate  and  can  be  managed  through  the  appropriate  risk  mitigation  measures  (e.g.  clinical monitoring, safety surveillance).

Indeed, based on the clinical data from the phase 3 studies, and phase 2 studies, there were no safety signals were identified on the basis of the QTcF analysis. The events occurred in participants with and without a history of bradycardia, occurred in the first three months of treatment and there was no clear dose association. At 4.6 times the exposure of the Invented name 60 mg therapeutic dose, clinically significant QTc interval prolongation was not observed.

However, paltusotine may inhibit gallbladder contractility and decrease bile secretion, which may lead to gallbladder stones or sludge.

There was not a higher incidence of cholelithiasis in the paltusotine group compared with the placebo group (2% versus 4%, respectively, in the RC phases of studies CRN00808-08 and CRN00808-09). However, events continued to accrue over time, particularly between 6 and 9 months, and an analysis of gall bladder ultrasound data showed a higher incidence of gallstones in the paltusotine group compared to the placebo group. Cholelithiasis occurred in 4.7% and bile duct stone in 0.4% of all participants exposed to paltusotine in the clinical development program.

In participants who had not received previous treatment with SRL therapies, cholelithiasis was reported in 8.3% (2/24) of participants. No participants discontinued paltusotine due to cholelithiasis. There were 3 serious adverse reactions (SARs) of cholelithiasis.

Gastrointestinal  symptoms  of  diarrhoea,  abdominal  pain,  nausea  and  abdominal  discomfort  were collectively the most frequently reported adverse reactions with paltusotine. The majority of these events were mild, did not require treatment, occurred mostly during the first three months of treatment, and did not lead to discontinuation of paltusotine. Most events of diarrhoea lasted ≤ 3 days.

No hepatic indicative of DILI, metabolic and hormonal disorders (which included thyroid hormones) were reported.

The effects of paltusotine on the developing human foetus are not known; however, the completed nonclinical studies and available clinical data with paltusotine do not indicate teratogenicity.

No human data on the effect of paltusotine on fertility are available. Studies in animals showed no impaired fertility and no effect on mating at human exposure of Paltusotine.

It is unknown whether paltusotine is excreted in human milk. Available toxicology data in animals have shown excretion of paltusotine in milk. A risk to the newborns/infants cannot be excluded.

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

Exposure-response analyses were performed separately for each of the two phase 3 studies CRN0080808 and CRN00808-09 .  The  exposure  -safety  analysis  evaluated  potential  relationships  between paltusotine steady-state exposure and safety measures including AEs, out of range laboratory values, vital signs, gallbladder ultrasounds, and ECGs (Report CRNT-Proj1-23-0006).

No definitive relationships between any AEs, ECGs, or vital measures and paltusotine exposure were identified in either study. HbA1c showed a likely linear relationship with Paltusotine exposure in Study CRN00808-08 but increases in HbA1c from baseline were not considered clinically meaningful (60 mg paltusotine dose was associated with 0.23% increase in HbA1c).

The predicted placebo-corrected LS mean change from baseline in QTcF was less than 5 ms and the upper bound of the 90% CI was less than 10 ms at the geometric mean Cmax of 1341 ng/mL, which was 4.6-fold  of  the  Cmax,ss  at  the  dose  level  of  60  mg  (290  ng/mL).  Collective  review  of  data  as described in the paltusotine TQT Study Waiver Request demonstrated there is low to minimal risk for potential effects on cardiac repolarization with paltusotine.

New incidences of gallbladder sludge appeared elevated in study CRN00808-08 only for the highest quintile of paltusotine exposure where 4 of 10 (40%) participants showed the presence of sludge. There was no relationship between new cases of gallstones and paltusotine exposure.

Exposure-safety analyses of AEs and out-of-range laboratory, gallbladder ultrasound, ECG, and vitals data showed no relationships between paltusotine exposure and safety measures in Study CRN0080809 .

For each potential and identified safety concerns, specific measures were recommended in the SmPC of paltusotine.

## 5.2.3.3. Pharmacodynamic interactions with other medicinal products or substances

No dedicated PD DDI studies were conducted, and this was acceptable. However, paltusotine potential PD interactions with glucose metabolism may lead to DDI with some diabetes treatment. Please refer to the section Safety related drug -drug interactions .

## 5.2.4. Pharmacokinetics/pharmacodynamics (PK/PD)

## Exposure-response analysis

ER analysis for efficacy and safety was performed for the pivotal studies CRN00808-08 and CRN0080809 .

- Efficacy

## CRN00808-08

The ER dataset consisted of 111 subjects who received either paltusotine or a placebo dose.

A total of 409 unique paltusotine PK data (Ctrough) and 645 unique IGF-1 levels (635 observations before rescue therapy) were available from 54 subjects who received the paltusotine SDD tablet. Overall paired PK/PD consisted of 349 matched observations between observed paltusotine concentration and IGF1, and 635 between predicted paltusotine concentrations.

In  contrast,  in  subjects  receiving  placebo  (57  subjects),  a  total  of  405  unique  paltusotine  PK  data (Ctrough) and 662 unique IGF-1 levels (598 observations before rescue therapy) were available. Overall paired PK/PD consisted of 332 matched observations between observed paltusotine concentration and IGF1, and 598 between predicted paltusotine concentrations.

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

The final PK/PD model parameter estimates are presented in Table 10.

## CRN00808-09

The ER dataset consisted of 58 subjects who received either paltusotine or a placebo dose.

A total of 291 unique paltusotine PK data (Ctrough) and 335 unique IGF-1 levels (322 observations before rescue therapy) were available from 30 subjects who received the paltusotine SDD tablet. Overall paired PK/PD consisted of 285 matched observations between observed paltusotine concentration and IGF1, and 320 between predicted paltusotine concentrations.

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

In  contrast,  in  subjects  receiving  placebo  (28  subjects),  a  total  of  241  unique  paltusotine  PK  data (Ctrough) and 318 unique IGF-1 levels (209 observations before rescue therapy) were available. Overall paired PK/PD consisted of 167 matched observations between observed paltusotine concentration and IGF1, and 209 between predicted paltusotine concentrations.

The final E-R model developed for CRN00808-08 was used to predict IGF-1 results for CRN00808-09 . A plot of the predicted E-R curves for the 30 subjects on active therapy is presented in Figure 3.

- Safety

No strong relationship was found between paltusotine Ctrough and any of the safety endpoints.

## 5.2.5. Dose selection and therapeutic window

Oral paltusotine doses were evaluated in 2 phase 1 studies in healthy participants including study CRN00808-01 that evaluated an oral solution (1.25 to 20 mg) and in study CRN00808-07 that evaluated SDD tablets (20 mg to 80 mg). Paltusotine doses (5 mg to 40 mg as capsule) were evaluated in 2 phase 2 studies in acromegaly (studies CRN00808-02 and CRN00808-03 ).

Based on these results, a 40 mg QD dose of the SDD tablet was expected to result in paltusotine systemic exposures similar to those achieved with the 40 mg dose of the capsule formulation used in the phase 2 studies. Combined with E-R modelling, data from the phase 2 studies suggested that a paltusotine dose of 40 mg QD was likely to achieve IGF-1 suppression consistent with that achieved by depot SRLs for most participants with acromegaly.

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

In the phase 3 studies CRN00808-08 and CRN00808-09 , a titration scheme was applied 20 to 60 mg and 40 to 60 mg based on IGF-1 level. The efficacy E-R model showed that the paltusotine median Ctrough at steady-state associated with 20 mg (34 ng/mL), 40 mg (57 ng/mL), and 60 mg (89 ng/mL) were at approximately the IC66, IC76, and IC83 of the E-R curve, respectively. Based on a population maximum IGF-1 reduction of 57%, doses of 20 mg, 40 mg, and 60 mg corresponded to IGF-1 reductions of approximately 37%, 43%, and 47%, respectively. Accordingly, at a population-level, the 20 mg QD dose of paltusotine appeared submaximal, while the 60 mg QD dose approached the asymptotic nadir of the E-R curve.

## 5.2.6. Overall discussion and conclusions on clinical pharmacology

## 5.2.6.1. Discussion

## Pharmacokinetics

## Impaired renal function

The  results  of  Pop-PK  and  observed  data  analyses  stratified  according  to  renal  function  indicate comparable paltusotine exposures between subjects with normal and mild renal impairment, confirming that mild impairment has no clinically relevant effect. However, only a few subjects with moderate renal impairment  (n=2  observed;  n=4  Pop-PK)  were  available,  and  no  data  were  provided  for  severe impairment, preventing formal conclusions for these subgroups. The SmPC section 5.2 was amended to reflect the available PK data in all subgroups.

## Impaired hepatic function

Mean plasma concentrations of paltusotine over time between 0-24 hours post-dose in study CRN0080810 showed a higher exposure in subjects with hepatic impairment, up to approximately 6 h post dose. However, statistical analysis reports that mild hepatic impaired patients had the higher value (133.28 ng/mL), the following is the Cmax value for the severe hepatic impaired patients (110.33 ng/mL), while normal and moderate hepatic impaired patients show similar values (101.35 ng/mL and 100.21 ng/mL respectively). AUC0-24 values were comparable. Overall, it is thought that the exposure differences are not clinically relevant. Cigarette smokers were allowed in this study, but since only one participant was an  on-going  smoker,  smoking  is  considered  unlikely  to  have  influenced  the  summary  statistics  of paltusotine exposure.

## Race / Ethnicity

Based on observed steady-state dose-normalised Ctrough data (N=109), geometric mean ratios (GMRs) relative to White subjects were 0.95, 1.15, and 0.83 for Black/African American, Chinese, and Japanese subjects, respectively, suggesting slightly higher exposure in Chinese (+15%; 95% CI [0.85-1.55]) and lower exposure in Japanese subjects (-17%; 95% CI [0.59-1.17]). These differences were considered modest and not clinically meaningful.

Notably, single-dose NCA data (AUC0-24h and Cmax) indicated higher exposures in Chinese subjects compared with White subjects (+25% and +60%, respectively), broadly consistent with the steady-state trend. In contrast, Japanese subjects showed higher single-dose exposure (AUC0-24h +13%; Cmax +45%), inconsistent with the lower steady-state Ctrough (-17%). This discrepancy was acknowledged but could not be explained by the Applicant. Its regulatory impact was considered limited in view of the greater clinical relevance of steady-state exposure, and therefore not further pursued.

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

The totality of evidence, with emphasis on observed steady-state Ctrough data, does not indicate a clinically relevant impact of ethnicity on paltusotine PK.

## Body weight

Based on Pop-PK analyses (N=315; mean 76 kg, median 73 kg, IQR 65-85 kg, range 45-138 kg), paltusotine exposure varied by less than ±5% across the BW range, indicating no clinically meaningful effect. The SmPC was amended accordingly.

## Elderly

Additional Pop-PK analyses (N=315) included 20 subjects (6.4%) aged 65-74 years, only 5 subjects (1.6%) aged 7584 years, and none aged ≥85 years, indicating that no clinically relevant effect of age was identified. The SmPC was explicitly reflects the limited PK data in subjects aged 75-84 years and the absence of data in subjects aged ≥85 years.

## PK interactions

## Paltusotine as victim

Paltusotine is mainly metabolised by glucuronidation  (fm=0.38)  and  mostly  by  UGT1A1  (fm UGT1A1=0.29).  Based  on  a  worst-case  scenario,  it  is  not  expected  that  paltusotine  drug  increase following UGT1A1 should significantly affect UGT1A1 drug substrate as these are, mostly, not NTI drugs.

## Inducers

Following the administration of 60 mg paltusotine, the clinical study in healthy subjects showed that carbamazepine, a strong inducer of CYP3A4/5, UGT1A1, and P-gp, decreased paltusotine Cmax and AUC by approximately 40% and 70%, respectively. Co-administration of paltusotine with strong inducers may thus decrease the therapeutic response. Therefore, in case such association cannot be avoided, the paltusotine dose should be increased up to three-fold the therapeutic dose, based on IGF-1 levels, and safety monitoring. The dose should not exceed 120 mg due to the absence of safety data, the lack of dose proportionality demonstration beyond this dose and the expected limited gain in efficacy of an increased dose from 120 mg up 180 mg as a 5% gain in IGF-1 inhibition is expected from a 40 to 60 mg increase when paltusotine is administered alone.

Based on the observed 70% decrease in paltusotine exposure following strong inducer (carbamazepine), a smaller decrease in exposure following moderate or weak inducer of CYP3A4/5, UGT1A1, or P-gp, is expected. Co-administration of paltusotine with a moderate (e.g. efavirenz) or weak (prednisone) inducer of CYP3A4/5, UGT1A1, or P-gp may thus decrease the therapeutic response and dose adjustment may be necessary according to the clinical response. In case of minimal induction that may require paltusotine dose increase, it must not exceed the maximal dose of 120 mg as this corresponds to safe exposure limits of the no -effect exposure boundaries.

## Acid reducing agent

Co-administration  of  paltusotine  with  PPIs  demonstrated  a  dose-dependent  decrease  in  paltusotine exposure that may decrease thus decrease therapeutic response and dose adjustment may be necessary according to the clinical response.

## Cyclosporine

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

A clinical study conducted in healthy subjects showed following 200 mg cyclosporine administration, paltusotine exposure was increased by two-fold. Nevertheless, no dose adjustment is considered needed as no clinically significant adverse events is expected.

## Paltusotine as perpetrator

Based on in vitro studies, it cannot be excluded that paltusotine could inhibit P-gp and CYP2D6 drug substrate  respectively.  The  provided  PBPK  model  predictions  of  expected  DDI  mediated  by  P-gp  or CYP2D6 inhibition respectively cannot be used to rule out clinically significant DDI due to insufficient qualification dataset and inconsistent results in the demonstration of the predictive capacity of drug interaction of the compound file of the drug substrates used to perform simulations.

Caution of use with P-gp and CYP2D6 drug substrates have been included in SmPC 4.5 section.

## Exposure-Response safety

Regarding the proposed 80%-200% no effect boundary, the upper boundary of +200% has been justified based on the only safety outcome for which an E-R relationship has been established.

## Dose rationale and justification

The dosing rationale appears sound for subjects switching from standard therapy (octerotide or lanreotide) to paltusotine at the 40 mg dose, as IGF-1 levels were similar to those at baseline during treatment with octreotide LAR or lanreotide injections.

For naïve subjects, it was questioned whether maintaining a paltusotine dose of 60 mg in patients with high levels of IGF-1 at baseline (1.7xULN) would be beneficial given the predicted effects of a 60 mg dose of paltusotine on IGF-1 dynamics (reduction by 47.5%). A plot showing the simulations of posttreatment IGF-1 based on varying levels of baseline IGF-1 for a QD dose of 60 mg was provided, concluding that a dose of 60 mg is expected to provide IGF1 levels ≤1.0xULN across a broad range of baseline IGF-1 levels (&gt;1.7xULN to 4.0xULN). Such conclusion can only be agreed at the more for an IGF-1 at baseline of 2.5x ULN and by considering an IMAX effect of 57%. Based on the provided plot, the applicant was asked to compute for each Baseline IGF-1(xULN) of 2.0, 2.5, …, 5.0, the proportion of subjects who are expected to achieve Post-treatment IGF-1(xULN) of 0.5-&lt;1, 1-&lt;1.5, 1.5-&lt;2, 2above. Results of the simulation were provided in the table below and clearly demonstrate that the probability of achieving IGF1 normalisation (≤ 1xULN) or near -normalisation (≤ 1.3xULN) markedly decreases with increasing baseline IGF-1 levels.

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

In addition, the applicant discussed the results available from the 40 patients on active treatment in Stratum 1 of study CRN00808-08 who had a mean (SD) baseline IGF-1 of 2.2 (0.83) xULN from which efficacy results observed were compared to those predicted by the IMAX model (provided a Ctrough paltusotine was available). The percentage of patients achieving a post treatment IGF1 of ≤ 1xULN, 1 to ≤ 1.5 and&gt; 1.5 were as follows for observed vs predicted: 42.5% vs 45%, 25% vs 22.5%, 32.5% vs 32.5% demonstrating the accuracy of the model predictions, this was agreed.

Of note, the efficacy results for the subjects from study CRN00808-08 were more precisely addressed in section 5.1 of the SmPC. Furthermore, we can observe that baseline IGF-1 decreased no more than 0.8 to 1 xULN. With a baseline IGF-1 &gt;2.5xULN in naïve patients, a normalisation or a near normalisation of IGF-1 cannot be achieved and this was reflected in the SmPC section 4.2.

Finally, it is worth noting that the PK/PD model (given its efficacy predictive performance, see above), and in particular the monitoring of trough concentrations (predictive of efficacy in patients), could be useful in treatment-naïve patients during the gradual dose increase (from 20 to 60 mg). Failure to reach a  certain  concentration  level  could  allow  for  anticipation  of  IGF-1  normalization  and  justify  further measures (reassessment of the B/R).

## Primary pharmacology

The primary pharmacodynamic (PD) effect of paltusotine is a reduction of GH and IGF-1 levels which may  enable  normalisation  of  these  hormone  levels  in  patients  with  acromegaly.  The  PD  effect  of paltusotine on GH and IGF-1 was assessed in healthy volunteers in phase 1 studies (study CRN0080801 (IGF-1 and GH) and study PA0002 (IGF-1 only).

Serum IGF-1 and GH are known biomarkers of acromegaly disease. IGF-1 has been reported to be the most reliable biomarker to monitor the efficacy of SRL-treatments in acromegaly. Most symptoms of acromegaly are non-specific, so a normal IGF-1 level in the presence of persistent symptoms has been considered controlled disease.

Of note, serum GH can be difficult to detect in healthy volunteers because GH has a short half-life and is secreted in a pulsatile fashion. Therefore, a GHRH challenge was performed in study CRN00808-01 to cause a reproducible temporary elevation of serum GH and assess the ability of CRN00808 to suppress stimulated GH secretion.

The 2 phase 1 studies demonstrated a rapid dose-dependent suppression of stimulated GH and IGF-1 secretion, observed as early as Day 1, with sustained decline in serum IGF-1 concentrations observed once paltusotine steady state was achieved at Day 7.

The degree of IGF-1 suppression observed in study CRN00808-01 compared to placebo (approximately 37-47%) by multiple doses of paltusotine was similar to that observed for peptide somatostatin analogs (octreotide, lanreotide) in previously reported healthy volunteer studies ( Tiberg et al, 2015 and Kuhn et al, 1994 ). Indeed, in both acromegaly patients and healthy volunteers, sustained suppression of GH release results in lowering of serum IGF-1 levels.

However, in contrast to the rapid effects of the GH response, IGF-1 levels decrease more gradually and require several days of exposure to SST agonists to produce an observable effect ( Tiberg et al, 2015 and Kuhn et al, 1994 ). Plasma concentrations of somatostatin analogs that result in this level of suppression in  healthy  volunteers  are  comparable  to  the  trough  concentrations  in  acromegaly  patients  on  their highest approved dose ( Tiberg et al, 2015; Kuhn et al, 1994 ).

Indeed, as trough paltusotine concentrations reached steady state, serum IGF-1 concentrations began to  decline.  Maximal  IGF-1  suppression  reached  its  nadir  in  approximately  7  days,  whereas  depot injections of SRL treatments require several weeks or months to reach steady state and dose optimization with  paltusotine  is  possible  in  much  shorter  time.  Unlike  the  rapid  suppression  of  GH,  IGF-1  levels decrease gradually and require 4 to 10 days of exposure to SST agonists to produce an observable effect. The gradual decrease of IGF-1 is likely due to its long t1/2 (approximately 15 h), which is due to high affinity IGF-1 binding proteins in plasma. After the final paltusotine dose, IGF-1 remained suppressed for several days but began to recover as paltusotine plasma concentrations declined. Analogous to the observation that it takes several days to achieve the nadir of IGF-1 suppression with once-a-day dosing of  paltusotine,  it  takes  several  days  for  IGF-1  to  recover  back  to  baseline  levels  upon  paltusotine withdrawal.

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

Proof of principle in healthy participants informed the acromegaly clinical development program but it is difficult to extrapolate these results to patients with acromegaly. Moreover, the forms of paltusotine were different in the single dose cohorts of CRN00808-01 , it was an oral solution and in the multiple-dose cohorts and in the phase 2 studies, it was the capsules and in the phase 3 studies, it was the SDD tablets as in the phase 1 PA002 study. Only men were included in these 2 phase 1 studies.

Given that healthy subjects did not present with the elevated IGF-1 level at baseline typical for untreated patients with acromegaly and that dose-response relationship in healthy adults is relatively flat due to the low IGF-1 levels in this population, the difference in IGF-1 response between the paltusotine doses might not be detectable. Compared to placebo, suppression of IGF-1 levels on the last day of dosing for the 10 mg, 20 mg and 30 mg cohorts was similar indicating that the 10 mg dose achieved a nearmaximal response.

The  results  of  study  CRN00808-01  in  healthy  volunteers  which  was  an  exploratory,  proof-ofpharmacology trial, suggested that paltusotine was well tolerated at doses that 10 mg to 30 mg/day resulted in PD activity, as measured by suppression of GHRH-stimulated GH and IGF-1 secretion. These results were also consistent with literature that describes the pharmacological activity of somatostatin agonists and fulfilled the main objectives of that study.

Then, the doses of paltusotine used in the phase 3 studies were justified with the E-R modelling analysis and the phase 2 studies.

## Secondary pharmacology

According to clinical and non-clinical data support that through selective binding to SST2, paltusotine normalizes excess hormone secretion of GH by tumour cells to moderate the clinical signs and symptoms associated with acromegaly. Excess GH levels result in increased secretion of IGF-1 from the liver and other organs.

Paltusotine exerts its agonist activity to moderate excess hormone secretion via highly selective binding to SST2, thereby reducing clinical signs and symptoms with minimal off-target toxicities from the liver and other organs.

Based on the results of the clinical program, paltusotine was generally well tolerated across all studies in the clinical program with no unexpected safety signals. The safety profile of paltusotine includes ADRs of  gastrointestinal  events  (diarrhoea,  abdominal  pain,  nausea,  and  abdominal  discomfort),  sinus bradycardia, and cholelithiasis, consistent with other SRL treatments. These AEs are generally mild or moderate  and  can  be  managed  through  the  appropriate  risk  mitigation  measures  (e.g.  clinical monitoring, safety surveillance).

Exposure-response analyses were performed separately for each of the two phase 3 studies CRN0080808 and CRN00808-09 .  The  exposure  -safety  analysis  evaluated  potential  relationships  between paltusotine steady-state exposure and safety measures including AEs, out of range laboratory values, vital signs, gallbladder ultrasounds, and ECGs. No definitive relationships between any AEs, ECGs, or vital measures and paltusotine exposure were identified in either study. HbA1c showed a likely linear relationship with paltusotine exposure in Study CRN00808-08 but increases in HbA1c from baseline were not considered clinically meaningful (60 mg paltusotine dose was associated with 0.23% increase in HbA1c).

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

New incidences of gallbladder sludge appeared elevated in study CRN00808-08

only for the highest

quintile of paltusotine exposure, where 4 of 10 (40%) participants showed the presence of sludge. There

was no relationship between new cases of gallstones and paltusotine exposure. However, paltusotine

may inhibit gallbladder contractility and decrease bile secretion, which may lead to gallbladder stones or

sludge. This was reflected in the SmPC section 4.4.

Exposure-safety analyses of AEs and out-of-range laboratory, gallbladder ultrasound, ECG, and vitals

data showed no relationships between paltusotine exposure and safety measures in Study CRN00808-

09.

For  each  potential  and  identified  safety  concern,  there  are  specific  measures  which  are  already

recommended in the SmPC of paltusotine as regards bradycardia, cholelithiasis and pregnancy.

As Paltusotine is a SRLs, although no glycaemic, thyroid or hepatic disorders have been reported during

the clinical trials with Paltusotine, in line with the other SRLs, appropriate recommendations were added

in section 4.4 of the SmPC and in the PL.

For hepatic function, given the reported cases of ALT/AST increases &gt;3×ULN, the consistency with

class-related effects, and the importance of adequately inform prescribers, a statement on mild to

moderate transaminase elevations was included in section 4.8 of the SmPC, in line with other SRL

therapies.

5.2.6.2.

Conclusions

Overall, the PK and PD of paltusotine has been sufficiently characterized.
