## -Description of the participants, baseline characteristics and extent of exposure

To  be  eligible  to  enter  the  Treatment  Period,  participants  were  required  to  have  Screening  IGF-1 measurements in the ranges specified for Group 1 (primary analysis cohort) and Groups 2 through 5 (exploratory analysis cohorts), based on prior SRL and IGF-1 status.

- Participants in Group 1 were partial responders to stable treatment with octreotide LAR or lanreotide depot monotherapy ,  with at least 1 screening IGF-I value greater than 1× ULN and the Visit 2 value ≤ 2.5 × ULN.
-  Participants  in Group  2 were partial  responders to  a  stable  combination  of  octreotide  LAR  or lanreotide depot plus a dopamine agonist (cabergoline), with the same IGF-1 criteria as Group 1.
- Participants in Group 3 were complete responders (mean of screening IGF-1 values &lt;1×ULN) to a stable  combination  of  octreotide  LAR  or  lanreotide  depot plus  a  dopamine  agonist ,  in  all  cases cabergoline.
- Participants in Group 4 were complete responders to a stable dose of pasireotide LAR (mean of Screening IGF-1 values were ≤ ULN).
- Participants in Group 5 were complete responders to  a  stable combination of octreotide LAR or lanreotide depot plus pegvisomant (mean of Screening IGF-1 values were ≤ ULN).

A total of 47 adult subjects were enrolled in the study on stable doses of SRL-based pharmacotherapy for the treatment of acromegaly were planned to be recruited for this study. 42 subjects (89.4%) completed the trial.

A washout (ie, demonstration of increased IGF-1 levels and/or return of symptoms) before switching from parenteral to oral therapy with paltusotine was not required.

Approximately 57% of participants were female, the median age was 51 years (IQR, 43.0 to 61.0 years), the majority of subjects (83%) were &lt;65 years of age; 17% were ≥ 65 years of age.

The median duration since acromegaly diagnosis was 112.50 months (IQR, 59.80 to 143.60 months). The majority of participants had prior surgery for pituitary tumour ( 78.7%).

Pretrial  acromegaly  therapies:  Of  the 25  participants in  Group  1 EAS (efficacy  analysis  set), 12 participants were on lanreotide depot (1 participant on 60 mg/month, 4 participants on 90 mg/month, and  7  participants  on  120  mg/month).  The  remaining 13  participants were  on  octreotide  LAR  (1 participant on 10 mg/month, 9 participants on 30 mg/month, and 3 participants on 40 mg/month).

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

Medically naïve patients were excluded. Subjects who were complete responders (both Screening IGF-1 values ≤ ULN) to octreotide LAR or lanreotide depot monotherapy were excluded.

## -Objectives

- To evaluate the efficacy of paltusotine in acromegaly subjects treated with somatostatin receptor ligand (SRL) based treatment regimens based on IGF-1 levels, GH levels, PRO questionnaires (Investigator Assessed Symptoms of Acromegaly, ASD, AcroQoL, EQ-5D-5L, Patient Global Impression of Severity (PGI-S), and Patient Global Impression of Improvement (PGI-I).
- To evaluate the safety and tolerability of paltusotine in acromegaly subjects.
- To evaluate the pharmacokinetics (PK) of paltusotine in acromegaly subjects.

## -Primary and secondary endpoints

## Primary endpoint

- Change from baseline in IGF-1 level at W13/EoT in Group 1 subjects Efficacy Analysis Set (EAS). The Baseline value was defined as the mean of the IGF-1 prior to first dose of study drug.

## Secondary endpoints

- Proportion of subjects with IGF-1 ≤ ULN in Groups 3, 4, and 5 subjects only at W13/EoT.
- Proportion of subjects with IGF-1 ≤ 1.5 × ULN at W13/EoT in group 1.

## Main Exploratory endpoints:

- . Change from baseline in integrated serum GH levels measured at W13/EoT.
- . Change from baseline in IGF-1 level at W13/EoT in subjects in Groups 2 through 5.
- . Change from baseline in symptoms of acromegaly as measured by total ASD* (Acromegaly Symptom Diary) score at W13/EOT was evaluated as an exploratory endpoint.
- . Change from baseline in EuroQoL-5 dimensions-5 levels (EQ-5D-5L) score at W13/EoT.
- . Change from baseline in acromegaly QoL questionnaire (AcroQoL) at W13/EoT.
- . Patient Global Impression of Improvement (PGI-I) scores at W13/EoT.

*ASD: The ASD is a brief disease-specific symptoms diary developed and validated by the Sponsor in accordance with the FDA Patient-Focused Drug Development Guidance and Patient-Reported Outcome Measures Guidance. The ASD was developed to fill a recognized need in the availability of a patientreported  measure  of  acromegaly.  Other  commonly  used  measures  either  lack  items  specific  to acromegaly or sufficient content related to symptoms. Thus, the ASD was developed as a novel, daily diary to  assess  important  symptoms  of  acromegaly  from  the  patient  perspective .  The  ASD consists of 7 symptoms (headache pain, joint pain, sweating, fatigue, weakness in legs, swelling, and numbness or tingling). Two additional items, difficulty sleeping and difficulty with short-term memory, are also included in the diary. Patients rate the severity of each symptom using a 0 to 10 numeric rating scale; the ASD weekly total score describes the average severity of all 7 symptoms across a single  week.  The  ASD  weekly  total  score  ranges  from  0  to  70;  for  all  ASD  items, a  higher  score indicates greater severity. The proposed preliminary threshold range to characterize a meaningful change from the patients' perspective for the ASD total is a 4- to 6-point change for improvement or worsening out of a total score of 70 ( Martin et al 2023 ).

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

## -Treatments Administered

Paltusotine was supplied as 5 mg and 10 mg solid dose HMG capsules .

The starting dose of 10 mg QD paltusotine was found to be the lowest dose that was maximally effective in suppressing IGF-1 in healthy volunteers at steady-state and this level of suppression mirrored that achieved by either octreotide or lanreotide. The doses can be increased up to 40 mg/day or reduced in case of intolerance.

If  at  any  time,  the  participant  experienced  a  significant  worsening  of  symptoms  associated  with acromegaly, and in the Investigator's opinion would not benefit from continuing in the study, standard acromegaly treatment was to be resumed , and participants were to be discontinued from the study and  an  early  termination  visit  was  to  be  scheduled,  before  resuming  standard  pharmacotherapy whenever possible.

Of note, due to COVID-19 pandemics, some participants were up-titrated during specific study visits without availability of prior IGF-1 data.

In group 1, all patients commenced paltusotine at 10 mg once daily and most patients were titrated to the maximum dose of 40 mg by the EoT period (week 13). By week 13, 18/23 ( 78% ) patients in group 1 who completed the treatment period titrated to the maximal dose of 40 mg/day, 2 ended treatment at 30 mg, 2 at 20 mg, and 1 at 10 mg/day.

Of note, doses for most patients in groups 2 and 3 were also increased to 40 mg/day.

## -Results and outcomes

- Primary Endpoint: ' Change from Baseline in IGF-1 at Week 13/End of Treatment at Week 13/EOT in Group 1 participants of the Efficacy Analysis Set (EAS).'

The median change from baseline in IGF-1 values at W13/EoT in  Group 1 subjects  (EAS)  was  not significant,  at  approximately  zero,  indicating  no  change, -0.03 × ULN (IQR,  -0.107  to  0.107×  ULN; p=0.6285 ).

At baseline, median IGF-I for patients was 1.34× ULN (IQR: 1.08, 1.47× ULN) and remained relatively stable throughout the 13-week paltusotine treatment period.

Of 23 participants who completed the dosing period in Group 1, 20 participants ( 87.0% ) achieved IGF-1 levels at EOT within 20% of Baseline or lower . Overall, the IGF-1 values for participants in Group 1 were found to be maintained.

Table 13 IGF-1 (× ULN) at Baseline and End of Paltusotine Treatment in Group 1 (EAS)

## · Secondary Endpoints

- 1) Proportion of Participants with IGF-1 ≤ ULN at Week 13/End of Treatment (this endpoint was limited to only complete responders at baseline in Groups 3, 4 and 5 who started the study with IGF-1 ≤ 1 × ULN).

At W13/EOT, of the 12 participants in Groups 3, 4, and 5, the percentage of participants who maintained IGF-1 level of ≤ 1.0 × ULN was 33.3% (n=4) and the percentage of participants who did not maintain IGF-1 level of ≤ 1.0 × ULN was 66.7% (n=8), p= 0.8448.

## 2) Proportion of Participants with IGF-1 ≤ 1.5 × ULN at Week 13/End of Treatment

At Baseline, of the 25 participants in Group 1 (EAS), the proportion of participants who had IGF-1 level of ≤ 1.5 × ULN was 80.0% (n=20).

At W13/EOT, the proportion of participants with IGF-1 ≤ 1.5 × ULN remained the same [80.0% (n=20)] and the proportion of participants with IGF-1 &gt;1.5× ULN was 20.0% (n=5).

Overall, at baseline, of the 47 subjects (FAS), the percentage of subjects who had IGF-1 level of ≤ 1.5 × ULN was 83.0% (n=39).

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

At W13/EoT, the percentage of subjects with IGF-1 level of ≤ 1.5 × ULN was 68.1% (n=32, responders) and the percentage of subjects with IGF-1 &gt;1.5× ULN was 31.9% (n=15, non-responders ).

## · Key exploratory endpoints

## 1) Change from Baseline in Serum GH Levels Measured at Week 13/End of Treatment

The median change from baseline in serum GH levels at W13/EoT in Group 1 subjects (EAS) was not significant,  at  approximately  zero,  indicating  no  change, -0.05 ng/mL  (IQR,  -0.29  to  0.20  ng/mL; p=0.6285 ).

At baseline, median integrated GH levels in group 1 were 0.69 ng/mL (IQR: 0.49-1.55 ng/mL) and were unchanged at week 13/EoT.

The median baseline GH for groups 2 and 3 was 1.04 ng/mL (IQR: 0.51-1.61 ng/mL). The median GH increase from baseline to week 13/EoT was 0.68 ng/mL (IQR: 0.10-1.58; P =.0067 ). Four weeks after withdrawing paltusotine, the integrated GH had significantly increased from week 13/EoT by a median of 15.0% (IQR: 1.8%-134.4%; P=.0419 ).

## 2) Change from Baseline in IGF-1 level at W13/EoT in subjects in Groups 2 through 5

Median IGF-I levels were 1.21× ULN (IQR: 0.93-1.50× ULN) at baseline.

After discontinuation of cabergoline and conversion from depot SRL to paltusotine, the median change from baseline in IGF-1 levels at W13/EoT in Groups 2 and 3 subjects was 0.28× ULN (IQR, 0.149 to 0.985× ULN; p=0.0020 ) suggesting a contribution to baseline IGF-1 control from the pre-trial dopamine agonist.

Patients who had received octreotide LAR or lanreotide depot plus cabergoline before the trial (n =15) showed  a  small but  statistically  significant  increase  in  median  IGF-I  concentrations when switched  from  combination  therapy  to  paltusotine  monotherapy,  followed  by  a  significant  and  more robust increase in IGF-I during the 4 weeks after discontinuation of paltusotine of 22%.

## 3) Change from Baseline in Symptoms of Acromegaly as Measured by Total ASD Score at Week 13/End of Treatment

For the total enrolled population ( n=47 ), out of a maximally symptomatic total score of 70, median total ASD scores were low at Baseline [median = 12.00 (IQR: 4.21, 23.93) n=20 ], suggesting low symptom burden at Baseline in this study population.

These low scores were maintained or numerically lower as of the end of the paltusotine treatment period [median (IQR) = 7.86 (3.43, 18.38) n=32 ]. Scores showed no significant change from baseline to week 13/EoT (median [IQR] = -0.43 [-5.0 to 2.4], n=18 ).

The median change from baseline in total ASD score at W13/EOT in Group 1 participants (EAS) was 0.21 (IQR: -2.71 to 2.43), p = 0.8657.

## 4) Plasma Octreotide and Lanreotide Concentrations

In the overall study population, 24 subjects switched from octreotide LAR and 19 switched from lanreotide depot.

In the case of octreotide, median residual concentrations fell to the LLOQ of the octreotide assay by W7 (12 weeks after last depot injection). In the case of lanreotide, residual concentrations had fallen by a median of 50.1% by W7 and by 77.3% at W13 .

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

To evaluate the potential impact of residual octreotide and lanreotide on the change from baseline in IGF-1 and GH values, sensitivity analyses evaluating the octreotide vs. lanreotide subsets were performed in Group 1 subjects.

The median change from baseline in IGF 1 values at W13/EoT in subjects (n=12) on pre-trial lanreotide depot [ -0.07 × ULN (IQR, -0.16 to 0.01× ULN); 0.0640 ] was similar to that in subjects (n=13) on pretrial octreotide LAR [ 0.01 × ULN (IQR, -0.10 to 0.18× ULN); p=0.3757 ].

The median change from baseline in GH values at W13/EoT in subjects on pre-trial lanreotide depot [0.03 ng/mL (IQR, -0.24 to 0.17 ng/mL); p= 0.7334 ] was similar to that in subjects on pre-trial octreotide LAR [-0.11 ng/mL (IQR, -0.30 to 0.22 ng/mL); p= 0.8394 ].

## 5) Patient Global Impression of Improvement (PGI-I) scores at W13/EoT .

At W13/EoT, 11 ( 23.4% ) subjects had 'very much improved' or 'much improved', whereas 26 ( 55.3% ) subjects had 'minimally improved' or reported 'no change' compared to before starting the study, while treated with depot SRLs. No patients reported any degree of worsening while treated with paltusotine.

## Phase 2 Study CRN00808-02 'Acrobat EVOLVE'

## -Study design

Study CRN00808-02 was  a  placebo-controlled,  double-blind, randomized  withdrawal  study designed to evaluate the safety, efficacy, and PK of 4 different doses of paltusotine in a sample of participants, with a confirmed acromegaly diagnosis that was controlled (mean of IGF-1 ≤ ULN) values,  as  confirmed  by  central  laboratory  on  two  occasions  measured  1-2  weeks  apart  during  the Screening Period) on a stable approved dose of octreotide LAR or lanreotide depot (stable defined as no change in dose for the last 3 months). This study was conducted at 45 clinical sites in 13 countries. Enrolment in this study was halted due to business reasons/considerations as of 31 March 2020, with 13 participants receiving paltusotine.

First patient enrolled: 11 March 2019

Last patient completed: 31 March 2020

Figure 5 Study design

<!-- image -->

Abbreviations: IGF-1=insulin-like growth factor-1; V=visit; W=week

All subjects were switched to 10 mg of paltusotine once daily, four weeks after receiving the last injection of depot SRL during the screening period. At predetermined timepoints, IGF-1 levels and tolerability were assessed, and doses were increased in 10 mg increments as dictated by the protocol up to a maximum of 30 mg. After this dose titration phase, participants were randomized to receive paltusotine or placebo for four additional weeks if their week eight IGF-1 was &lt;1.0×ULN.

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

A total of 58 participants were screened for eligibility, only 13 subjects enrolled in the study with confirmed diagnoses of acromegaly whose levels of IGF-1 were biochemically controlled (IGF-1 ≤ ULN) on standard-of-care SRL depot injections completed participation in the study.

Of the 13 treated participants, 11 participants were evaluated for eligibility to randomize at the Week 10 visit (1 participant discontinued early from the study, and 1 participant was not eligible to randomize due to a dose dispensing error prior to Week 10). Of the 11 participants evaluated for eligibility to randomize, 7 participants (63.6%) were randomized into the RWP, and 4 participants (36.4%) did not enter the RWP due to IGF-1&gt;ULN at Week 8.

## A washout (ie, demonstration of increased IGF-1 levels and/or return of symptoms) before switching from parenteral to oral therapy was not required.

## -Description of the participants and baseline characteristics

The median (range) age of subjects was 50 years (29 - 75 years) for the 13 subjects included in the ITT analysis set. The median (range) duration since diagnosis of acromegaly was 145.3 months (17.2 - 253.1 months) for the 13 subjects included in the ITT analysis.

## -Treatments Administered and extent of exposure

Paltusotine was administered as a 5 mg or 10 mg HMG capsule . During the titration period (up to 13 weeks), participants received an oral once-daily dose of either 5 mg, 10 mg, 20 mg, or 30 mg paltusotine based on the titration schedule.

During the RWP (4 weeks), participants received either matching placebo or 5 mg, 10 mg, 20 mg, or 30 mg  paltusotine  based  on  their  previously  optimized  dose.  At  any  point  during  the  titration  period, participants who were treated with 10 mg or 20 mg QD and were judged by the Investigator as intolerant to the current dose of the study drug were to undergo a one-time dose reduction (from 10 mg to 5 mg QD or from 20 mg to 10 mg QD).

## -Study Objectives and Efficacy Endpoints

The objectives of study CRN00808-02 study were to evaluate the efficacy, safety, tolerability, and PK of paltusotine in acromegaly participants.

The primary endpoint was the proportion of randomized participants who met responder criteria (based on the mean of two consecutive IGF-1 measurements ≤ 1 × ULN) at Week 13.

Key secondary endpoints were:

Change in IGF-1 levels between Week 10 and Week 13

Change in GH levels between Week 8 and Week 13

Change in symptoms of acromegaly as measured by total ASD Score between Week 10 and Week 13.

## -Results and outcomes

- Primary Efficacy Endpoint: ' proportion of randomized participants who met responder criteria (based on the mean of two consecutive IGF-1 measurements ≤ 1 × ULN) at Week 13.

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

Seven (7) of the 11 participants (64%) who completed the titration period per protocol maintained normal IGF-1 at Week 8, allowing entry into the RWP.

## · Secondary Endpoints

As expected with the small sample size (n=7), no significant differences (p&gt;0.05) between paltusotine and placebo were seen in the secondary endpoints of IGF-1, GH, or total ASD score during the RWP.

The median treatment difference between paltusotine and placebo in IGF-1 change from Week 10 to Week 13 was -0.27×ULN (95% CI: -1.2 to -0.052×ULN) and the median treatment difference between paltusotine and placebo in IGF-1 percent change from Week 8 to Week 13 was -37.2% (95% CI: 104.7% to -1.2%).

The median treatment difference in GH change from Week 10 to Week 13 was 0.11 ng/mL (95% CI, 2.6 to 0.60 ng/mL) and the median treatment difference in GH percent change from Week 10 to Week 13 was 40.6% (95% CI, -361.6% to 106.5%).

The median treatment difference in total ASD score change from baseline was 1.1 (95% CI, -4.3 to 1.9).

## · Exploratory efficacy analyses

- Changes in IGF-1 and changes in GH levels: not relevant
- Plasma Concentrations of Paltusotine, Octreotide, and Lanreotide

At  the  end  of  the  titration  period  (Week  10/Visit  11  pre-dose),  median  (n;  range)  paltusotine concentrations appeared dose-proportional in the limited number of subjects at each dose: 10 mg (6.56 ng/mL [n=4; 4.27 - 11.5 ng/mL]); 20 mg (13.1 ng/mL [n=6; 7.14 - 91.4 ng/mL]); and 30 mg (38.6 ng/mL [n=2; 28.8 - 48.4 ng/mL]). All paltusotine concentrations had washed out at the end of the 4 weeks follow-up.

Residual octreotide plasma concentrations were below the lower limit of quantitation of 100 pg/ml at Week 10 (Visit 11) and Week 13 (Visit 14). Residual lanreotide was still detectable at Week 13 (Visit 14). The median (range) percent reduction in residual lanreotide plasma concentrations from Screening Visit 2 to Week 13 (Visit 14) was -72.6% (-83.4%, -71.7%).

## Phase 2 study CRN00808-05 'Acrobat ADVANCE'

First patient enrolled:

26 February 2020.

On-going: the  data  cut-off  date  for  the  interim  analysis  study  was 21 May 2025 ,  providing  up  to approximately 5.5 years of long-term data for paltusotine.

## -Study design

Eligible participants from studies CRN00808-03 and CRN00808-02 were invited to participate in the on-going OLE Study CRN00808-05 , which provides long-term safety, tolerability, and efficacy data for paltusotine. The starting daily dose for the tablet formulation was 40 mg (2×20 mg tablets) QD for oral self-administration.

Study CRN00808-05 is an on-going phase 2, 6-year, OLE study designed to evaluate the long-term safety and efficacy of paltusotine in adult participants with acromegaly who completed either of the parent studies CRN00808-02 or CRN00808-03 .

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

Two groups of participants were enrolled: (1) Group 1 participants who were not currently enrolled in study CRN00808-02 or  study  CR N00808-03 ,  but  had  completed  study CRN00808-02 or  study CRN00808-03 and had resumed standard acromegaly medications (Delayed Rollover), and (2) Group 2a  participants  who  were  completing  study CRN00808-02 or  study CRN00808-03 and  had  not resumed standard acromegaly medication (Direct Rollover).

Eligible participants entered a Treatment Period of up to 312 weeks , followed by a 28-day Follow-up Period.

Figure 6 Study design

<!-- image -->

## - Study Objectives and Endpoints

The objective of this study is to evaluate the long-term safety and efficacy of paltusotine in acromegaly.

The primary endpoint is the incidence of treatment-emergent adverse events (TEAEs) throughout the study.

The secondary endpoints are the change from the Week 16 visit (15 weeks of study treatment) in IGF-1 levels and the change from Week 16 in GH levels.

* Change from Week 16 was utilized for these analyses as all participants were expected to have been up-titrated to the maximum dose by that time-point.

## Exploratory efficacy endpoints included:

Proportion of participants with IGF-1 measurements ≤ ULN at each post-baseline visit

Proportion of participants who achieve serum GH &lt;1.0 ng/mL

Number of consecutive visits with IGF-1 ≤ ULN

Change from baseline in AcroQoL total score

Change from baseline in ASD total score Additional exploratory analysis were evaluated at the interim analysis:

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

Participants treatment preference, as assessed by the Treatment Preference Questionnaire (TPQ) at W52

Participants  medication  assessment  as  measured  by  the  treatment  satisfaction  questionnaire  for medication (TSQM-9) at, W312/V32 or early termination (ET)

## -Disposition and baseline characteristics

A total of 45 participants were screened, of which 43 participants were enrolled and included in the Safety Population.

11 participants enrolled from parent Study CRN00808-02 , of whom 7 were delayed rollover and 4 were direct rollover. 32 participants enrolled from parent Study CRN00808-03 , of whom 17 were delayed rollover and 15 were direct rollover.

No participants had completed the Treatment Period (Week 207) or the Follow-up Period (Week 211) as of the data cut-off date.

Eight participants (18.6%) discontinued from the study early, two (25%) of which were due to AE, and two (25%) of which were due to inability to fulfil study requirements and procedures, four (50%) were due to other reasons.

Participant demographics and baseline characteristics were generally consistent between the two parent studies. The median age was 52 years (range, 30 to 76), with 35 participants (81.4%) falling in the ≥ 18 to &lt;65 age group, and 55.8% of participants were female.

Study participants had a median duration since acromegaly diagnosis of 123.7 months, ranging from 25 to 369 months. Most participants ( 86% ) had prior pituitary surgery.

Pre-trial  therapies  consisted  of  octreotide  LAR  ( 48.8% of  participants;  mean  dose  30  mg/month), lanreotide  depot (44.2%; mean  dose  108.9  mg/month),  cabergoline  ( 30.2% ;  mean  dose  3.4 mg/week), pasireotide LAR ( 14.0%; mean dose 46.7 mg/month), and pegvisomant ( 2.3% [n=1]; 20 mg/week).

## - Treatments Administered and extent of exposure

Paltusotine capsules or tablets were taken once a day in the morning after an overnight fast (at least 6 hours). No food was allowed for at least 2 hours after capsule administration and 1 hour after tablet administration including time for study medication administration.

## The recommended initiation dose of paltusotine for all participants was 10 mg per day (QD) at the Week 1/Visit 3 .

Participants received an oral once daily dose of either 10, 20, 30, or 40 mg paltusotine capsules based on titration. If tolerated, the dose of study medication was to be increased to 20 mg QD at the Week 4 Visit if the IGF-1 value measured was &gt;ULN range provided by the central laboratory.

At the Week 8 Visit , the dose of study medication was to be increased to a maximum of 40 mg QD if IGF-1 remained &gt;ULN and the participant was tolerating the study medication.

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

Doses could be decreased from doses of 20 mg QD or above, to improve tolerability, as necessary, at any time during the study.

Switching from capsules to tablets allowed participants to shorten the post-dose fasting to 1 hour because of the lessened food effect, and tablets were chosen as commercial formulation.

Thirty-six participants ( 83.7% ) were switched from paltusotine capsules to tablets, which reflected all active participants as of the data cut-off date. The participants who were not switched from capsules to tablets terminated Study CRN00808-05 prior to the introduction of tablets.

After the switch, dose levels for tablets could be increased from 40 mg (2×20 mg tablets) to 60 mg (3×20 mg tablets) QD based on IGF-1 response; down-titration was allowed based on tolerability.

Table 14 Tablet Dose Titration Options for Study CRN00808-05

| Tablet Dose Titration/Adjustment   | IGF-1 Criteria                                               | Tolerability Criteria                                             |
|------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------|
| 40 mg → 60 mg                      | Participant's most recent IGF-1 results on 40 mg is >1.0×ULN | Increase allowed due to acceptable tolerability on 40 mg          |
| 40 mg → 20 mg                      | --                                                           | Dose reduction required due to unacceptable tolerability on 40 mg |
| 20 mg → 40 mg                      | Participant's most recent IGF-1 results on 20 mg is >1.0×ULN | Tolerability is acceptable on 20 mg                               |
| 60 mg → 40 mg                      | --                                                           | Dose reduction required due to unacceptable tolerability on 60 mg |

IGF-1=insulin-like growth factor-1, mg=milligram, ULN=upper limit of normal

Adjunctive acromegaly treatments (ie, dopamine agonists and/or pegvisomant) were permitted to be initiated in combination with paltusotine if IGF-1 levels were still elevated after the participant had been taking  paltusotine  40  mg  QD  (or  maximally  tolerated  lower  dose) for  2  to  4  weeks ,  if  clinically appropriate: this would generally have occurred by the Week 12 visit.

If  it  was  determined that adjunctive medication was required, then use of a dopamine agonist (e.g. cabergoline,  bromocriptine)  was  the  expected  first-line  adjunct,  as  clinically  appropriate.  If  after  an adequate period of assessment, study medication combined with a dopamine agonist did not achieve therapeutic targets, then pegvisomant could be added as a second-line adjunct, if clinically appropriate. No participants received cabergoline-adjunctive therapy prior to Week 15 . The highest frequency of participants ( n=18 ) receiving cabergoline occurred at Week 64 and beyond.

Only one participant from parent study CRN00808-02 received cabergoline, which was one dose of 1.8 mg/week given at Week 47. All remaining cabergoline doses were taken by participants from parent study CRN00808-03 .

Two participants (4.7%) took adjunctive pegvisomant, both of whom were enrolled in parent Study CRN00808-03.

Of the 43 participants enrolled, 34 participants were continuing in the study at the data snapshot date of 31 August 2025. No participants have completed the Treatment Period (Week 311) or the Follow-up Period (Week 315).

Most participants ( 65.1%) were being treated with paltusotine 60 mg QD, and 27.9% were being treated  with  40  mg  QD.  Approximately  half  of  participants  from  parent  Study  CRN00808-02  (n=6,

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

54.5%) were receiving a paltusotine dose of 40 mg QD, whereas the majority of participants from parent Study CRN00808-03 (n=24, 75.0%) were receiving paltusotine 60 mg QD, which was expected as  most  of  the  latter  participants  were  sub-optimally  controlled  on  monotherapy  with  octreotide  or lanreotide prior to enrolment in the parent study.

The number of paltusotine who required paltusotine down titration due to treatment-emergent adverse events in the OLE phase was: 1/43 participants (2.3%) had reduced the dose of paltusotine from 60 mg to 40 mg and 1/43 (2.3%) had reduced the dose of paltusotine from 40 mg to 20 mg.

## -Results and outcomes as regards efficacy endpoints

## 1) 'Proportion of Participants with IGF-1 Measurements ≤ ULN at each Post-baseline Visit' (interim analysis at the data snapshot date of 31 August 2025)

At Week 3, the overall proportion of participants of study CRN00808-05 with IGF-1 ≤ 1.0 x ULN was 34.9%  (15/43) ,  with 81.8% (9/11)  from  study  CRN00808-02  and 18.8% (6/32)  from  study CRN00808-03.

Among participants on paltusotine only, the overall proportion of participants of study CRN00808-05 with IGF-1 ≤ 1.0 x ULN was 50% (8/16) with 77.8% (7/9) from study CRN00808-02 and 14.3% (1/7) from study CRN00808-03.

At Week 15, 31.0% (13/43) of participants in the overall CRN00808-05 population achieved IGF-1 level ≤1.0xULN. As expected, based on baseline characteristics and clinical status of participants in the parent studies, the proportion of participants who achieved IGF-1 level ≤1.0xULN at Week 15 was higher in participants  from  parent  study  CN00808-02  ( 63.6%: 7/11) than  in  participants  from  parent  study CRN00808-03 ( 19.4%: 6/32), though participants were not fully up-titrated at the Week 15 timepoint.

Among 62.5% (10/16) participants with 'paltusotine only' in the overall CRN00808-05 population who achieved IGF-1 level ≤ 1.0xULN, 77.8% (7/9) were from study CRN00808-02 and 42.9% (3/7) from study CRN00808-03.

The proportion  of  IGF-1  responders  (IGF1  level  ≤1.0xULN)  increased  over  time  and  was  generally maintained through Week 272 in the overall study population and within the subgroups by parent study.

For participants from study CRN00808-02, the IGF-1 response rate increased to 90.0% (9/10) by Week 23, with IGF-1 response rates at subsequent visits ranging from 62.5% (5/8) to 100% (4/4) through Week 259.

For participants from study CRN00808-03, the IGF-1 response rate increased to 30.0% (9/30) by Week 31, with IGF-1 response rates at subsequent visits ranging from 24.1% (7/29) to 69.2% (18/26) through Week 27.

The median IGF-1 level (×ULN) at Week 3 was similar to the baseline median IGF-1 level (×ULN) on SRL-based therapy (ie, long-acting somatostatin receptor ligand [LA SRL] Baseline) from the parent studies and was &lt;1.5 (×ULN) from Week 3 through Week 272.

The median IGF-1 level (xULN) after Week 15 was decreased at each visit from Week 23 through Week 272 (22 consecutive visits).

In participants from parent study CRN00808-02, median (range) IGF-1 level (×ULN) at Week 15 was 0.91 (0.2 to 1.4). In these participants (who were previously controlled on SRLs), median IGF-1 levels were similar to their baseline levels on SRL based therapy and stayed below 1.0×ULN for most of the study.

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

In participants from parent study CRN00808-03, median (range) IGF-1 level (xULN) at Week 15 was 1.37 (0.8 to 2.8). Small decreases in median IGF-1 level (×ULN) were observed after Week 23 through Week 272.

As  described  previously,  most  participants  from  study  CRN00808-03  required  pre-trial  combination therapy and were taking adjunctive medication (cabergoline and/or pegvisomant as per protocol) during study CRN00808-05. Paltusotine monotherapy was able to maintain IGF-1 levels similar to previous SRLbased therapy, and for participants who were treated with paltusotine and adjunctive therapy, additional treatment benefits were observed, as evidenced by the continued improvement in IGF-1 levels during the study.

Figure 7 Median (+/- IQR) Overall IGF-1 x ULN by Visit CRN00808-05 Safety population

<!-- image -->

IGF-1=insulin-like growth factor-1; IQR=interquartile range; LA-SRL=long-acting somatostatin receptor ligand; ULN=upper limit of normal

Notes: Safety Population is participants who received at least 1 dose of study drug. ULN is determined based on the participant's age and sex. Error bars represent 25 th  and 75 th  percentiles.

LA-SRL Baseline is the baseline from the parent study (Study CRN00808-02 or Study CRN00808-03). Untreated Baseline is the last non-missing assessment in the parent study when in the untreated withdrawal phase. Data snapshot date: 31 August 2025

## 2) Proportion of Participants who Achieve GH &lt;1.0 ng/mL (31 August 2025)

At Week 15, 53.5% of  participants  in  the  overall  CRN00808-05  population  achieved GH level &lt;1.0 ng/mL ( 63.6% in participants from parent Study CN00808-02 and 50.0% in participants from parent Study CRN00808-03 group).

The proportion of participants with GH level &lt;1.0 ng/mL ranged from 50.0% to 100.0% from Week 23 to Week 272 in the overall study population.

For  participants  from  Study  CRN00808-02, the  proportion  of  participants  with  GH  level  &lt;1.0 ng/mL ranged from 50.0% to 87.5% from Week 23 to Week 259. For participants from Study CRN00808-03, the proportion of participants with GH level &lt;1.0 ng/mL ranged from 50.0% to 100.0% from Week 23 to Week 272 (the Week 272 timepoint represents only 3 participants).

The overall median (range) GH level at Week 15 was 0.7990 (0.045 to 28.960) ng/mL with overall median GH levels remaining &lt;1.0 ng/mL after Week 23 up to Week 272.

## Treatment Preference Questionnaire (TPQ) at W51

At the cut-off date of 5 December 2023, data were available on 36 participants at Week 51 , of whom 91.7% indicated a preference for a once daily oral medication compared to a preference for previous injections among 2.8% or no preference ( 5.6%). For Week 51 and Early Termination combined, the preference was once daily oral medication among 87.2% of participants.

## S ummary for AcroQoL, EQ-5D-5L, PGI-S, PGI-I at W51

AcroQoL, and EQ-5D-5L were generally stable throughout the study.

## Tumour volume

There were no clinically significant tumour changes as assessed by the Investigator.

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

## 5.3.2. Main studies

2 phase 3 studies have been conducted by the applicant. Studies CRN00808-08 and CRN00808-09 were similar with respect to the duration of washout of existing SRLs, dose titration, and treatment with stable doses of paltusotine in the RC Phases.

## 5.3.2.1. Study CRN00808-08

## 5.3.2.1.1. Study title

## PATHFNDR-2: Paltusotine Acromegaly Therapy Featuring a Non-Invasive Daily Regimen

## 5.3.2.1.2. Study design

This was a phase 3, multicenter, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled study where participants with non-pharmacologically treated acromegaly including participants with no prior medical therapy, participants who last received medical therapy at least 4 months prior to screening, and participants who were controlled on medical therapy for at least 3 months but agreed to wash out prior to beginning study treatment, were randomly allocated to receive either paltusotine or placebo.

Figure 12 Study schema

<!-- image -->

D=day, EoR=End of Randomized Controlled Phase, R=randomization, RC=randomized controlled, W=week a During the Screening Period of Study CRN00808-08, a washout period of up to 12 to 16 weeks was included for the group of participants who were controlled on medical therapy but agreed to washout of long-acting octreotide or lanreotide prior to study enrolment.

## RC Phase + OLE Phase

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

<!-- image -->

EOR=End of RC Phase, EOS=End of Study, IGF-1=insulin-like growth factor-1, OLE=Open-label Extension, RC=Randomized Controlled

EOR was defined as the end of the RC Phase at Week 24.

EOS is defined as the day final study data are collected and should occur 4 weeks after the last dose of study drug. The end of Week 228/EOS Visit (Follow-up Visit for the OLE Phase) is the last planned visit of the study, but the EOS Visit could occur at Week 28 (Follow-up Visit for the RC Phase) in participants who did not enter the OLE Phase.

After the screening period, all eligible participants were randomized in a 1:1 ratio to receive paltusotine or  matching  placebo, stratified  by  prior  treatment (medically  naïve  or  previously  treated  versus washout). The RC phase treatment period was 24 weeks .

After randomization in the RC phase, all participants had a dose-titration period (based on IGF-1 levels and tolerability) of 12 weeks.

This  study  was  designed  to  provide  an adequate  washout  duration of  long-acting  octreotide  or lanreotide of up to approximately 24-weeks ( at least 12 weeks in the screening period plus the 12-week dose titration period in the RC Phase ).

After the first 12 weeks of study treatment including dose titration in the RC phase, no further dose titration was permitted for the remaining 12 weeks of study treatment in the RC phase. This allowed for an additional 12 weeks on a stable dose of study treatment to adequately assess the effectiveness of paltusotine.

Participants who completed the 24-week RC phase or who met rescue criteria and completed the 24week RC phase with rescue medication could continue participation in the OLE phase.

At the completion of study target enrolment for the RC phase, the remaining participants in the washout group who had entered screening and met eligibility criteria were eligible to directly enrol into the OLE for up to 200 weeks plus a 4-week follow-up .

The  RC  phase  was  completed,  and  the  OLE  phase  is  on-going  with 113 participants treated  with paltusotine for up to 60 weeks , as of the data cut-off date of 20 January 2024 .

This  study  was  conducted  at 56 sites in 15 countries :  Argentina,  Brazil,  Bulgaria,  China,  France, Germany, Greece, Hungary, India, Israel, Italy, Poland, Spain, United Kingdom (UK), and United States (US).

## Treatment

Participants received paltusotine or matching placebo during the RC phase and paltusotine during the OLE phase.

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

Paltusotine was available as immediate-release 20 mg tablets . Matching placebo tablets were identical in appearance to paltusotine tablets and were administered orally according to the randomization scheme, on an identical schedule to those receiving paltusotine.

During the RC and OLE phases, study drug was to be taken in the morning, with water, after an overnight fast of at least 6 hours. No food or drink (except for water) was allowed for at least 1 hour after drug administration, starting from the time study drug was administered.

In the RC phase , the starting dose was 20 mg QD (1×20 mg tablet of active paltusotine or matching placebo) for oral self-administration. A dose of 20 mg was selected to minimize potential gastrointestinal side effects in participants washed out from medical therapy or naïve to medical therapy. After confirming tolerability with the participant, the dose was increased to 40 mg QD (2×20 mg tablets of active paltusotine or matching placebo) at the Week 2 visit .

Dose down-titration was allowed at any time during the RC phase of the study if the dose was not tolerated in the judgement of the investigator.

Beginning at Week 6 , the dose could be increased to 60 mg QD (3×20 mg tablets of active paltusotine or matching placebo) if the 40 mg dose was acceptably tolerated and based on biochemical control and the Week 4 IGF-1 was &gt;0.9 × ULN . If the Week 4 IGF-1 was ≤ 0.9 × ULN, the dose was maintained at 40 mg QD until the end of the RC phase, unless an unscheduled measurement obtained at any time from Week 4 to prior to or at Week 12 showed IGF-1 &gt;0.9×ULN, in which case the dose was increased from 40 mg to 60 mg QD if the 40 mg dose was acceptably tolerated. It was anticipated that the study drug dose would be stable, and titration completed prior to or at Week 12, with no dose titrations/adjustments after  Week  12.  Participants  who  required  dose  up-titration  after  Week  12  were  considered  nonresponders.

Table 15 Dose Titration Options

| Dose Titration/Adjustment   | IGF-1 Criteria                                                        | Tolerability Criteria                                                 |
|-----------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------|
| 20 mg → 40 mg               | --                                                                    | Increase required at Week 2 visit if acceptable tolerability on 20 mg |
| 40 mg → 60 mg               | Week 4 IGF-1 >0.9×ULN or at any time prior to or at the Week 12 visit | Acceptable tolerability on 40 mg                                      |
| 60 mg → 40 mg               | --                                                                    | Dose reduction required due to unacceptable tolerability on 60 mg     |
| 40 mg → 20 mg               | --                                                                    | Dose reduction required due to unacceptable tolerability on 40 mg     |

If the investigator determined that a dose titration/adjustment after Week 12 was required, the investigator had to contact the Medical Monitor to ensure that a consistent approach to dose titration/adjustment was applied across the study.

In the OLE phase , the starting dose regimen for participants is paltusotine 20 mg QD at the week 24/EOR visit , and if tolerated, the dose can be increased to 40 mg QD at the Week 26 visit .

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

Dose escalation in the OLE phase generally occurred once the first IGF-1 result was available. At that time,  the  investigator  could  determine  whether  the  dose  could  be  up-titrated  to  60  mg  (3×20  mg tablets). The dose could be increased to 60 mg QD if the 40 mg dose was acceptably tolerated and based on biochemical control. Dose down-titration was allowed if the dose was not tolerated in the judgement of the investigator.

Study medication could be held for up to a total of 14 days, but no more than 7 consecutive days per year in the OLE.

## Concomitant medications

## Prohibited Medications

Prohibited medications during the study included the following:

- Proton pump inhibitors
- Use of famotidine in the evenings
-  Oral  estrogen,  except  for  monophasic  estrogen-progestin  oral  contraception  or  stable  estrogen replacement
- Strong inducers of the drug metabolizing enzyme CYP3A4, including but not limited to: apalutamide, carbamazepine,  enzalutamide,  mitotane,  phenytoin,  rifampin,  and  St.  John's  wort  (because  these medicines could decrease the concentration of paltusotine in the systemic circulation)
- Any standard acromegaly drug that was not used as a rescue medication during the RC Phase (or an allowed adjunctive medication in the OLE Phase
- Octreotide (any form), lanreotide, or pasireotide during the OLE
- Any other investigational drug, unless approved by the Medical Monitor (eg, COVID-19 therapies)

## Rescue therapies

During  the  RC  Phase,  rescue  criteria  were  in  place  to  ensure  safety  for  participants  who  required resumption of standard acromegaly treatment (ie, 2 consecutive IGF-1 levels ≥ 1.5 × ULN* at the highest dose of study treatment [60 mg/day] and new or worsening of acromegaly symptoms as assessed by the investigator for at least 2 weeks).

*Of note, in a large study of naïve acromegaly patients, octreotide LAR monotherapy achieved mean IGF-1 of 1.5× ULN ( Colao A 2014 ) at 3 months of treatment through 12 months.

Symptoms had to be attributed to uncontrolled acromegaly in the opinion of the investigator and could include but were not limited to the following: headache, arthralgia, fatigue, hyperhidrosis, or soft tissue swelling.

Significant worsening was defined as symptoms requiring a substantial increase in level of clinical care (e.g. significant intervention needed to avert hospitalization or a clinically notable increase in frequency or intensity of patient contact required) or substantial clinical deterioration (e.g. worsening from a mild to severe AE or the onset of an SAE would meet these criteria).

Octreotide  LAR  or  lanreotide  depot  monotherapy  (approved  SRLs)  were  the  only  rescue  therapies permitted during the RC phase of the study.

Participants who met rescue criteria in the RC phase were to be discontinued from study drug (paltusotine or placebo). These participants were considered non-responders, and they were encouraged to continue in the study on rescue therapy for assessment and observation in the remainder of the 24-week RC phase.

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

All participants treated with paltusotine or placebo who completed the RC phase, including those who received rescue therapy, could participate in the OLE phase if they met all eligibility requirements.

## OLE Phase Adjunctive Treatments

If there was evidence of paltusotine efficacy at 6 months but participants had not reached therapeutic target goals, adjunctive treatment could be initiated if clinically appropriate beginning at Week 48 . If these criteria were met, non-SRL acromegaly treatments, such as cabergoline or GH receptor antagonist (ie, pegvisomant), were permitted and could be initiated as an adjunctive treatment when a participant experienced at least 2 consecutive elevated IGF-1 ( ≥ 1.3 × ULN) measurements at the highest paltusotine dose level (60 mg) or was otherwise not at the participant's therapeutic target in the judgement of the investigator.

If  adjunctive  medications  were  not  appropriate  or  not  accessible  under  local  regulations,  and  the participant was not under acceptable biochemical control while treated with Paltusotine monotherapy at the maximal tolerated dose in the opinion of the investigator, the participant was discontinued from the OLE portion of the study.

## Randomisation

Randomization was performed using a fixed-block randomization scheme. The randomization scheme was generated prior to the initiation of the study by an independent statistician/programmer who was not a member of the study team. Randomization was stratified by prior treatment (medically naïve or previously treated [Stratum 1] versus washout [Stratum 2]).

## Blinding

The Sponsor, contract research organizations (CROs), investigators, study site personnel, and study participants were blinded to treatment assignment during the RC Phase. The randomization schedule was kept strictly confidential and accessible only to authorized persons. The randomization schedule was made  available  for  unblinding  and  analysis  only  after  the  RC  phase  was  completed,  the  protocol deviations were determined, and the study database was locked.

The IRT system was programmed with blind-breaking instructions in case of an emergency for participant safety and for expedited reporting of SAEs to regulatory agencies, when required.

## Patient population

## -Main inclusion criteria

Three groups of participants were enrolled:

- Participants  with  no  prior  medical  therapy  (Group  1/Stratum  1):  'medically  naïve  group'  who  at screening had IGF-1 ≥ 1.3 × ULN. Group 1 subjects must have had at least 1 pituitary surgery 3 months or more prior to screening
- Participants who last received medical therapy at least 4 months prior to screening (Group 2/Stratum 1) who had IGF-1 ≥ 1.3 × ULN at screening

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

- Participants  who  were  controlled  on  stable  dose  of  medical  therapy  with  octreotide  or  lanreotide monotherapy for at least 3 months before screening but agreed to washout prior to beginning study treatment (Group 3/Stratum 2) who had IGF-1 ≤ 1.0 × ULN at screening . After informed consent was provided, the participant was not to receive, further pretrial acromegaly medication. IGF-1 had to rise from the first screening visit by at least 30% and be ≥ 1.1 × ULN (the lower limit for eligibility is IGF-1 of 1.05 when rounded to 2 decimal places).

Of note, during the screening period, a washout period of up to 12 to 16 weeks was included for the Group 3 participants who were controlled on medical therapy but agreed to washout of long-acting octreotide  or  lanreotide  prior  to  study  enrolment.  Participants  who  were  controlled  on  short-acting octreotide or lanreotide had a washout period of approximately 8 weeks .

The screening period for the medically naïve group and the previously treated group (no treatment within previous 4 months) was approximately 4 weeks in duration and consisted of one screening visit.

## Other inclusion criteria

1. Previous  diagnosis  of  acromegaly  confirmed  by  the  Investigator  and  approved  by  the  Medical Monitor. This requires evaluable documentation of a pituitary adenoma.
2. Females who engage in heterosexual intercourse must be of non-childbearing potential, defined as either surgically sterile (ie, hysterectomy, bilateral salpingectomy, or bilateral oophorectomy), OR be postmenopausal with at least 1 year of amenorrhea, OR must agree to use either a highly effective or a clinically acceptable method of contraception from the beginning of screening until at least 30 days after the last dose of study drug.
3. If the subject is male, the subject should agree to use a condom when sexually active with a female partner of childbearing potential from screening until at least 30 days after the last dose of study drug or remain abstinent. Male subjects should also agree to not donate sperm for the duration of the study and until at least 30 days after the last dose of study drug.
4. -Main exclusion criteria
- History of ineffectiveness or significant intolerance of octreotide or lanreotide treatment
- History of pituitary radiation therapy within 3 years of screening
- Subjects with adrenal insufficiency, diabetes insipidus, or central hypogonadism who are not receiving adequate hormone replacement therapy at the time of screening
- High-risk pituitary tumour (compression of the optic chiasm or invasion of adjacent brain structures, history of tumour growth within 1 year after surgery or radiation)
- Diabetes mellitus treated with insulin for less than 6 weeks prior to the study entry, or with change in total daily insulin dose by &gt;15% within 6 weeks prior to screening.
- Use of prohibited medications such as
11. . Lanreotide depot or octreotide LAR ( within 16 weeks before screening ) (Group 2only)
12. . Pasireotide LAR ( within 24 weeks prior to screening )
13. . Pegvisomant ( within 16 weeks before screening )
14. . Dopamine agonists ( within 16 weeks before screening )
15. . Current use of oral estrogen replacement therapy for &lt;12 weeks prior to screening

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

- .  Current  use  of  medications  that  are  strong  inducers  of  CYP3A4 within  2  weeks prior  to screening
- Poorly  controlled  diabetes  mellitus  defined  as  having  a  haemoglobin  A1c  (HbA1c) ≥ 8.5%  ( ≥ 69 mmol/mol),  or  estimated  HbA1c  based  on  fructosamine  if  HbA1c  is  not  evaluable  (eg,  due  to hemoglobinopathies).
- Female subjects who are pregnant or lactating. Subjects must have a negative pregnancy test during screening and prior to the first dose of study drug.

## 5.3.2.1.3. Objectives and estimands

## Primary objective

The primary objective of the RC phase was to evaluate the efficacy of paltusotine versus placebo on IGF-1  response  in  participants  with  non-pharmacologically  treated  acromegaly  who  were  randomly allocated to receive either paltusotine or placebo.

## The  primary  efficacy  endpoint was:  ' Proportion  of  participants  who  achieved  biochemical response in IGF-1 ( ≤ 1.0 × ULN) at EOR'.

IGF-1 response was defined as IGF-1 level ≤ 1.0 × ULN based on the average of the last 2 measurements prior to EOR (Weeks 22 and 24), rounded to 2 significant figures. If only 1 measurement was available at Weeks 22 and 24, this 1 measurement was used to define response.

Non-response was defined as IGF-1 level &gt;1.0×ULN based on the average of the last 2 measurements or with IGF-1 missing at both Week 22 and Week 24.

Participants  who  discontinued  treatment  for  any  reason  prior  to  EOR,  received  prohibited  standard acromegaly treatment, received rescue therapy with criteria met, or titrated up in dose after Week 12 were also considered non-responders.

## Estimand for the primary objective

Table 16 Estimand for primary objective

| Population               | Patients with acromegaly (medically naïve, previously treated, or post-washout) who would encounter the intercurrent event of discontinuation, rescue medication, prohibited medication, or dose up-titration after Week 12 if assigned to either paltusotine or placebo.   |
|--------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Treatment conditions     | Assignment to paltusotine, regardless of discontinuation and use of rescue medication, compared to assignment to placebo, regardless of discontinuation and use of rescue medication.                                                                                       |
| Endpoint (variable)      | Proportion of participants achieving IGF- 1 ≤ 1.0×ULN at end of randomized treatment, based on the average of Weeks 22 and 24. If only one of the two values was available, that single result (rounded to two significant figures) was used.                               |
| Population-level summary | Proportion of responders in each treatment group; comparison via odds ratio using exact logistic regression.                                                                                                                                                                |

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

| Intercurrent events and strategy to handle them   | Intercurrent events and strategy to handle them   |
|---------------------------------------------------|---------------------------------------------------|
| Discontinuation of treatment                      | Composite: to reflect treatment failure           |
| Rescue medication use                             | Composite: to reflect treatment failure           |
| Use of prohibited medication                      | Composite: to reflect treatment failure           |
| Up-titration after Week 12                        | Composite: to reflect treatment failure           |

The  primary  estimand  assesses  the  effect  of  paltusotine  versus  placebo  on  biochemical  control  of acromegaly, defined as achieving IGF-1 ≤ 1.0 × ULN at Week 24 (based on the average of Weeks 22 and 24, or a single value if only one is available). Participants who discontinued treatment, received rescue or prohibited medication, or had dose up-titration after Week 12 were considered non-responders. These intercurrent events were addressed using a composite strategy, reflecting clinical failure.

## Statistical methods for estimation and sensitivity analysis on primary estimand

The primary efficacy analysis was based on the Full Analysis Set (FAS). The estimand targeted the proportion of participants with IGF-1 ≤ 1.0 × ULN at End of Randomisation (EOR), defined as the average of Weeks 22 and 24 (rounded to two significant figures). Participants with missing IGF-1 values at both visits  or  with  intercurrent  events  (treatment  discontinuation,  rescue/prohibited  medications,  or  dose titration after Week 12) were considered non-responders.

The primary analysis used an exact logistic regression model adjusting for prior treatment strata; Firth logistic regression was prespecified as a fallback. Multiplicity was controlled through a hierarchical testing procedure.

Sensitivity  analyses  explored  variations  in  the  estimand,  including  per-protocol,  completers,  and alternate handling of intercurrent events (e.g. up-titration not considered treatment failure).

## Secondary objectives

Secondary objectives were to evaluate the effects of paltusotine versus placebo on IGF-1 level, IGF1 response ( ≤ 1.3 × ULN), GH response (&lt; 1 ng/mL), and acromegaly symptoms in the RC phase. The objective of the OLE Phase is to evaluate the long-term safety and efficacy of paltusotine.

## The key secondary efficacy endpoint for the RC Phase was as follows:

- -Change from Baseline in IGF-1, in units of ULN, to EOR

## Additional secondary efficacy endpoints were:

- -Proportion of participants who achieved IGF-1 &lt;1.3×ULN at EOR
- -Proportion of participants with GH &lt;1 ng/mL at Week 22
- -Change from Baseline in Total Acromegaly Symptoms Diary (ASD) score to EOR

## Table 17 Estimand for the secondary objectives

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

| Population                                      | Patients with acromegaly (medically naïve, previously treated, or post-washout) who would encounter the intercurrent event of discontinuation, rescue medication, prohibited medication, or dose up-titration after Week 12 if assigned to either paltusotine or placebo.   |
|-------------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Treatment conditions                            | Assignment to paltusotine, regardless of discontinuation and use of rescue medication, compared to assignment to placebo, regardless of discontinuation and use of rescue medication.                                                                                       |
| Endpoint (variable)                             | Change from baseline to EOR in IGF-1, expressed in ULN units. EOR defined as average of Weeks 22 and 24, or last available IGF-1 prior to rescue or discontinuation.                                                                                                        |
| Population-level summary                        | Treatment group difference in ranks based on an ANCOVA (fixed effects for treatment group and baseline IGF-1 level as covariates), with associated 95% confidence interval.                                                                                                 |
| Intercurrent events and strategy to handle them | Intercurrent events and strategy to handle them                                                                                                                                                                                                                             |
| Rescue medication use                           | While-on-treatment: IGF-1 value prior to rescue is used (LOCF).                                                                                                                                                                                                             |

## Statistical methods for estimation and sensitivity analysis on the secondary estimand

The key secondary endpoint was the change from baseline in IGF-1 (×ULN) at EOR, analysed in the FAS using an ANCOVA model with treatment group, prior treatment strata, and baseline IGF-1 as covariates. The EOR value was defined as the average of IGF-1 at Weeks 22 and 24, or the last prerescue or available value if data were missing.

A sensitivity analysis was conducted using multiple imputation based on on-treatment intermediate placebo values to handle missing post-baseline data and assessments following rescue therapy.

## Exploratory objectives

Exploratory objectives were to evaluate the effect of paltusotine versus placebo on GH level, need for rescue therapy, time to need for rescue treatment, GH response (&lt;2.5 ng/mL), and tumour volume. The pharmacokinetic objective was to evaluate the pharmacokinetic parameters of paltusotine in participants with acromegaly.

## Exploratory Efficacy Endpoints

The exploratory efficacy endpoints for the RC Phase were as follows:

- -Change from Baseline to Week 22 in GH
- Proportion of participants who receive rescue therapy by EOR
- Time from randomization to first dose of rescue treatment
- Time to initial response of IGF-1 ≤ 1.0×ULN
- Proportion of participants with GH &lt;2.5 ng/mL at Baseline and Week 22
-  Change from Baseline in residual pituitary tumour volume at EOR

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

-  Change  from  Baseline  to  EOR  in  EuroQol-5  Dimensions  5-Level  (EQ-5D-5L)  health  index,  Visual Analogue Scale (VAS), and dimension scores
-  Change from Baseline to EOR in Patient Global Impression of Severity (PGI-S)
- Patient Global Impression of Improvement (PGI-I) at EOR
- Treatment preference questionnaire at EOR
- Change from Baseline to EOR in Acromegaly Quality of Life (AcroQOL) total score
- Proportion of participants who achieved IGF-1 ≤ 1.0×ULN and GH&lt;1.0 ng/mL at EOR
- Proportion of participants who achieved IGF-1 ≤ 1.0×ULN and GH&lt;2.5 ng/mL at EOR
- Proportion of participants who achieved IGF-1&lt;1.3×ULN and GH&lt;1.0 ng/mL at EOR
- Proportion of participants who achieved IGF-1&lt;1.3×ULN and GH&lt;2.5 ng/mL at EOR

## 5.3.2.1.4. Results

## Participant flow and numbers analysed

Study start: 17 Dec 2021

Study initiation: 23 Aug 2022 (first participant randomized)

Last Participant Visit: 20 Jan 2024 (RC Phase)

Data Cut-off Date: 20 Jan 2024 (RC Phase)

Interim analysis: 31 August 2025 (OLE Phase)

A total of 111 participants were randomized ( 54 participants in the paltusotine group and 57 participants in the placebo group) and included in the FAS .

Of  the  total  participants  randomized, 52  participants (96.3%)  in  the  paltusotine  group  and 54 participants (94.7%) in the placebo group completed the RC Phase of the study.

In the paltusotine group, 4 of 54 participants ( 7.4% ) discontinued study treatment in the RC Phase.

The primary reasons for treatment discontinuation in the paltusotine group were treatment-emergent adverse  events  (TEAEs)  for  2  participants,  meeting  rescue  criteria  therapy  for  1  participant,  and withdrawal of consent for 1 participant.

In the placebo group, 16 of 57 participants ( 28.1% ) discontinued study treatment in the RC Phase. The primary reason for treatment discontinuation for most participants (13 of 16) in the placebo group was meeting rescue therapy criteria. Two participants (3.5%) withdrew consent, and 1 participant (1.8%) discontinued treatment due to a TEAE.

All 13 participants in the placebo group who received rescue therapy met the protocol-defined criteria for rescue therapy.

Of the 111 participants randomized in this study, 103 participants ( 92.8 %) entered the OLE Phase.

Figure 13 Participant Disposition for RC Phase of Study CRN00808-08

IRT = Interactive Response Technology.

Percentages are calculated based on the number of participants in the Full Analysis Set.

1  DE007-08001 underwent randomization in the IRT system by mistake before completing the screening procedures and being assessed for study eligibility. After completing the screening period, this participant was assessed as a screening failure and was never dosed. This randomization error is considered a protocol deviation. This participant is categorized under screening failures and is not included in the randomized set.

## OLE Phase

A total of 114 participants enrolled in the OLE Phase of Study CRN00808-08. 51 participants were from the prior paltusotine group; 52 participants were from the prior placebo group.

In addition, 11 participants directly entered the OLE Phase of the study. These participants were in the washout group at completion of study target enrolment for the RC Phase and had entered screening and met eligibility criteria.

A total of 44 participants from the medically naïve group in the RC Phase continued in the OLE Phase ( 21 in the prior paltusotine group and 23 in the prior placebo group).

A total of 34 participants from the previously treated group in the RC Phase continued in the OLE Phase ( 16 in the prior paltusotine group and 18 in the prior placebo group).

A  total  of 36  participants comprised  the  washout  group  in  the  OLE  Phase,  which  consists  of  14 participants  from  the  prior  paltusotine  group,  11  participants  from  the  prior  placebo  group,  and  11 participants who enrolled directly into the OLE Phase.

At the data snapshot date of 31 August 2025, 22 participants overall ( 19.3%) discontinued the OLE Phase of the study.

Page 2 of 2

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

A summary of participants who discontinued up to the 31 August 2025 data snapshot is provided below:

- 12 participants  in  the  prior  paltusotine  group  discontinued  the  study,  the  majority  from  the medically naïve group and did not demonstrate adequate clinical or biochemical benefit with paltusotine: 9 due to investigator decision, 1 due to withdrawal by subject, 1 due to pregnancy, and 1 due to other [for planned neurosurgery].
- 9 participants in the prior placebo group discontinued the study, the majority from the medically naïve group and did not demonstrate adequate clinical or biochemical benefit with paltusotine due  to  investigator  decision,  2  due  to  adverse  events,  and  1  due  to  'other'  [investigator decision]).
- 1 participant in the directly enrolled group discontinued due to prohibited medication.

No important or major protocol deviations were reported during the OLE Phase.

## Deviations from study plan

Important  protocol  deviations  were  reported  for  10  participants  (9.0%)  in  the  FAS,  most  of  which pertained to ASD questionnaires not being completed during Screening or prior to Week 24.

## Baseline data

## RC PHASE

Baseline characteristics were consistent between treatment groups. The median age for all participants was 46.7 years , ranging from 18 to 80 years. Most participants were &lt;65 years of age ( 91.0% ).

Median BMI was 29.01 kg/m 2 , ranging from 19.0 to 47.9 kg/m 2 .

A  slightly  higher  percentage  of  participants  in  the  paltusotine  group  were male compared  with  the placebo group ( 51.9% versus 42.1% ).

IRT = Interactive Response Technology. Percentages are calculated based on the number of participants in the Full Analysis Set. a=Subject US031-08001 is in strata of washout, but was mistakenly randomized in strata of Medically naïve or Previously treated in IRT. The Screening 1 IGF-1 was 0.88 x ULN and the Screening 2 IGF-1 was 1.16 x ULN.

## Disease Characteristics

Disease characteristics  were  generally  consistent  between  the  paltusotine  and  placebo  groups.  Most participants had a screening IGF-1 &gt;1.3×ULN ( 83.8% ).

Baseline  mean (SD) IGF-1×ULN levels ( 1.985 [0.8086]  and 2.207 [1.1049]  in  the  paltusotine  and placebo groups respectively.

Although Baseline mean (SD) GH (ng/mL) levels were different between the paltusotine and placebo groups  ( 3.026 [2.9035]  and 9.448 [24.1484]),  Baseline  median  values  were  similar  between  the paltusotine and placebo groups ( 2.138 versus 2.300 ).

Study participants overall had a long-standing disease history, with a median of 72.2 months , ranging from 5 to 388 months. However, participants in the paltusotine group had a longer median time since diagnosis ( 77.5 months versus 51.3 months ), and a longer median time since pituitary surgery ( 66.5 months versus 22.0 months ).

The majority of participants ( 89.2% ) had documented prior pituitary surgery; all participants in the medically naïve group had documented prior pituitary surgery.

In the majority of participants, pituitary tumours prior to first surgery were macroadenomas (&gt;10 mm), with 72% in the paltusotine group and 83% in the placebo group meeting this criterion.

Five  participants  (2  paltusotine  and  3  placebo)  received  prior  radiotherapy;  the  mean  time  from radiotherapy was 21.8 years in the paltusotine group and 10.8 years in the placebo group.

Baseline IGF-1 value is defined as the average of the measurements taken on Day 1 on or prior to first dose of study drug and the last IGF-1 value measured just prior to this day. Baseline GH is defined as the average from the fasting integrated GH samples at Screening Visit.

## Medical history

No medical history conditions were reported at a higher incidence (a difference of at least 10%) in the paltusotine group than in the placebo group, either on a SOC or PT basis.

## Rescue Therapy

A total of 15 participants ( 2 in the paltusotine group and 13 in the placebo group )  received pituitary and hypothalamic hormones and analogues as rescue medications, including octreotide and lanreotide.

In the paltusotine group, 1 participant received octreotide as rescue therapy during the RC Phase of the study. This participant completed the RC Phase and continued to the OLE Phase of the study. The other participant received lanreotide as rescue therapy after discontinuing paltusotine treatment due to an adverse event; this participant completed the RC phase but declined entering the OLE Phase of the study. All 13 participants in the placebo group ( 22.8% ) received somatostatin analogues (octreotide or lanreotide) as rescue therapy during the RC Phase.

## Extent of Exposure

## RC PHASE

Of the 54 participants randomized in the paltusotine group, 3 participants ( 5.6% ) received paltusotine 20 mg QD, 15 participants ( 27.8% )  received  paltusotine  40  mg QD, and 36 participants  ( 66.7% ) received paltusotine 60 mg QD as their final dose.

A total of 22 medically naïve participants were randomized to paltusotine in the RC Phase of the study and completed 24 weeks of treatment; 14% were receiving paltusotine 40 mg QD and 86% were receiving paltusotine 60 mg QD at the end of the RC Phase.

A total of 18 previously treated participants were randomized to paltusotine in the RC and completed the 24 weeks of treatment, 1 ( 5.5% ) received 20 mg, 8 ( 44.4% ) received 40 mg and 9 ( 50% ) received 60 mg. at the end of RC phase A total of 14 Washout participants were randomized in the RC phase and completed the 24 weeks of treatment, 1 ( 7.14 %) received 20 mg, 4 ( 28.57 %) received 40 mg and 9 ( 64.28 %) received 60 mg at the end of the RC phase.

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

In the paltusotine group, 3 participants had dose reductions to paltusotine 20 mg QD due to TEAEs during the RC Phase.

## OLE Phase

Of the 113 participants in  the  total  OLE  population  (included  in  the  OLE  Safety  Analysis  Set), 50 participants received paltusotine and 52 participants received placebo in the RC Phase. 11 washout participants entered the OLE Phase directly.

At the data snapshot date of 31 August 2025, the median duration (range) of exposure to paltusotine in the OLE Phase was 94.9 weeks (0.71 to 128.6 weeks) for the total population ( 94.0 weeks [11.6 to 128.6 weeks] for the prior paltusotine group, 93.9 weeks [3.6 to 124.6 weeks] for the prior placebo group, and 99.6 weeks [0.71 to 105.6] for the directly enrolled group).

- Medically naïve group:

A total of 46 medically naïve participants were treated with paltusotine (n=22) or placebo (n=24) in the Randomized Controlled (RC) Phase of Study CNR00808-08;

44  participants (21  in  the  prior  paltusotine  group  and  23  in  the  prior  placebo  group)  continued treatment in the Open-label Extension (OLE) Phase.

- During the OLE Phase (data snapshot 21 May 2025),

After starting at paltusotine 20 mg QD at Week 24, the majority of participants in the prior paltusotine group (81.0%, 17 of 21) and the prior placebo group (61.1%, 11 of 18) up-titrated to paltusotine 60 mg QD by Week 48. The majority of participants continued to receive paltusotine 60 mg QD at subsequent timepoints in the OLE Phase.

- Previously treated group

36 participants previously treated were randomized in the RC (18 in the paltusotine group and 18 in the placebo group);

- During the OLE Phase (data snapshot 21 May 2025),

After starting at paltusotine 20 mg QD at Week 24, the majority of participants ( 66.7%, 10 of 15) in the prior paltusotine group up-titrated to paltusotine 40 mg QD by Week 36. The majority of participants continued to receive paltusotine 40 mg QD at subsequent timepoints in the OLE Phase. One participant was on paltusotine 20 mg QD at Week 96.

In the prior placebo group, after starting at paltusotine 20 mg QD at Week 24, the majority of participants (58.8%, 10 of 17) up-titrated to 60 mg QD by Week 60. The majority of participants in the prior placebo group continued to receive paltusotine 60 mg QD at subsequent timepoints in the OLE Phase. Two participants  in  the  prior  placebo  group  were  receiving  paltusotine  20  mg  QD  at  Week  108  and  1 participant at Week 120.

- Washout group

29 washout participants were randomized in the RC (14 in the paltusotine group and 15 in the placebo group).

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

- During the OLE Phase (data snapshot 21 May 2025),

After starting at paltusotine 20 mg QD at Week 24, the majority of participants (84.6%, 11 of 13) in the prior paltusotine group up titrated to paltusotine 40 mg QD by Week 36. The majority of participants in the prior paltusotine group continued to receive paltusotine 40 mg QD at subsequent timepoints in the OLE Phase. One participant in the prior paltusotine group was on paltusotine 20 mg QD at Week 96.

In the prior placebo group, after starting at paltusotine 20 mg QD at Week 24, the majority of participants (90.9%, 10 of 11) up-titrated to 40 mg QD by Week 36. The majority of participants in the prior placebo group continued to receive paltusotine 40 mg QD at subsequent timepoints in the OLE Phase.

## Outcomes and estimation

## Primary Endpoint - RC Phase

Study CRN00808-08 met  statistical  significance  on  the  primary  endpoint  ( p&lt;0.0001 )  favouring paltusotine over placebo.

Table 29 presents results for the primary efficacy endpoint of the proportion of participants who achieved a biochemical response in IGF-1 ( ≤ 1.0 × ULN) at EOR.

The  proportion  of  participants  who  achieved  IGF-1  level ≤ 1.0 × ULN  was 55.6% (30  of  54)  in  the paltusotine group compared with 5.3% (3 of 57) in the placebo group, providing evidence of significant efficacy.

At EOR, 3 patients (5.6%) were treated with paltusotine 20 mg , 15 (27.7%) patients were treated with paltusotine 40 mg and 36 patients (66.7%) were treated with paltusotine 60 mg .

Among these  patients,  1/3  ( 33.3% )  treated  with  paltusotine  20  mg,  13/15  ( 86.7% )  treated  with paltusotine 40 mg and 16/36 ( 44.4%) treated with paltusotine 60 mg were responders.

CI=confidence interval; EOR = end of randomized controlled phase; IGF-1=insulin-like growth factor-1; IRT = Interactive Response Technology, ULN= upper limit of normal.

IGF-1 at EOR is based on the average of the last 2 measurements of IGF-1 collected at Weeks 22 and 24. When one of the two last IGF-1 measurements is missing a single value is used. ULN is determined based on the participant's age and sex.

Percentages are calculated based on the number of participants in the Full Analysis Set. P-value, odds ratio, and corresponding 95% CI are from an exact logistic regression model with treatment group as a factor and a covariate for prior treatment strata per IRT (medically naïve or previously treated versus washout). For the odds ratio, placebo participants are the reference group.

## Key Secondary Efficacy Endpoint - RC Phase

## Change from Baseline to EOR in IGF-1×ULN

The  LS  mean  (±SE)  changes  from  Baseline  to  EOT  in  IGF-1×ULN  were -0.819 (±0.0789)  for  the paltusotine group and 0.087 (±0.0751) for the placebo group, with a treatment difference of -0.906 (95% CI: -1.106, -0.706). This difference was statistically significant ( p&lt;0.0001 ).

Table 23 Secondary Efficacy Endpoint: Change from Baseline to EOT in IGF-1 (×ULN) in RC Phase of Study CRN00808-08 (Full Analysis Set)

|                                         | Paltusotine (N=54)   | Placebo (N=57)   | Treatment Difference (95% CI)   |         | P-value    |
|-----------------------------------------|----------------------|------------------|---------------------------------|---------|------------|
| IGF-1xULN Change from Baseline to EOT - | Primary Analysis     |                  |                                 |         |            |
| LS Mean                                 | -0.819               | 0.087            | -0.906 (-1.106,                 | -0.706) | <0.0001    |
| LS Standard Error                       | 0.0789               | 0.0751           |                                 |         |            |
| IGF-1xULN Change from Baseline to EOT - | Multiple             | Imputation -     | Control-based                   | Pattern | Imputation |
| LS Mean                                 | -0.827               | 0.124            | -0.950 (-1.166,                 | -0.735) | <0.0001    |
