## 5.3.6. Healthcare professional engagement

Input  has  been  received  from  a  healthcare  professionals'  organisation, European  Society  of Endocrinology (ESE) relating to paltusotine.

The ESE has highlighted that current medication is painful to administer, and it requires an HCP to give the injection. Patients are unable to administer the injection by themselves, and they complain about the hardened injection spot.

The wear-off effect is considered a big problem, several patients struggling until the next injection. A new medicine that mitigates this wear-off effect would be welcomed by most patients. The wear-off phase will be noticed by increased tiredness and increased joint problems and mostly occurs in the last week before the new injection.

Keeping the current medication cooled is inconvenient when travelling. Also, the need to find a HCP for giving the injection during a stay abroad is considered difficult.

The current medication can cause serious side effects, such as type 2 diabetes and, in some patients, the gallbladder needs to be removed.

## 5.3.7. Overall discussion and conclusions on clinical efficacy

## 5.3.7.1. Discussion

The efficacy of paltusotine has been evaluated based mainly in the 2 pivotal phase 3 studies 'CRN00808-08 and CRN08008-09' and also on 2 phase 2 dose studies ' CRN00808-03 and CRN00808-02' with a long-term extension of these studies 'CRN00808-05 '.

The dose selection was mainly based on 2 phase 2 dose studies ' CRN00808-03 and CRN0080802' with a long-term extension of these studies 'CRN00808-05 '.

## Phase 2 studies

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

1) Phase 2 study CRN00808-03 ' ACROBAT EDGE' was an open-label, dose blinded , exploratory study designed to evaluate the efficacy, safety, and PK of 5 doses (5 mg, 10mg, 20 mg, 30 mg and 40 mg) of paltusotine in capsules in participants with acromegaly treated with SRL-based treatment regimens. In this study, subjects were to be switched from their current monthly depot regimen to a once-daily regimen of oral paltusotine without a washout to loss of IGF-1 control.

The starting dose of 10 mg QD paltusotine was found to be the lowest dose that was maximally effective in suppressing IGF-1 in healthy volunteers at steady-state and this level of suppression mirrored that achieved by either octreotide or lanreotide.

The results showed a maintenance of the effects of the prior treatments with other SRLs but there are a lot of uncertainties in this study :

- these results of this study are mainly driven by the results of group 1 (n= 25/47) (partial responders previously treated with monotherapy of octreotide or lanreotide).
- most of the patients in group 1 were treated with paltusotine 40 mg. According to the applicant, these data can be consistent with lower doses of paltusotine (10 and 20 mg) being less suppressive of IGF-1 than the upper end of the dose range. Likewise, doses for most subjects in Groups 2 to 5 were titrated to 40 mg/day. However, most subjects were titrated to the 40 mg per dose of paltusotine, consistent with the upper end of the dose range used in this study (ie, 40 mg/day) being the effective dose for most subjects.
- the population is limited, and the interpretation of the results is very complicated all the more than due to COVID pandemic, some participants were up-titrated during specific study visits without availability of prior IGF-1 data.
- as there was not any washout between the previous SRLs treatment and the start of paltusotine, the possible carry-over effect of previous SRLs treatment (lanreotide) cannot be ruled-out due to the short duration  of  the  study  (13  weeks).  However,  Sensitivity  analyses  indicate that  the  wash  out of  both octreotide  and  lanreotide  were  adequate  to  measure  IGF-1  and  GH  suppression  associated  with paltusotine  treatment.  Indeed,  median  residual  concentrations  of  octreotide  fell  to  the  LLOQ  of  the octreotide assay by W7 (12 weeks after last depot injection). The residual concentrations of lanreotide had fallen by a median of 50.1% by W7 and by 77.3% at W13.
- the results of study show a maintenance of the effects of the prior treatments with other SRLs.

2)  Phase  2  study CRN00808-02 ' ACROBAT  EVOLVE '  was  a  placebo-controlled,  double-blind, randomized withdrawal study designed to evaluate the safety, efficacy, and PK of 4 different doses of paltusotine in capsules (10 mg, 20 mg, 30 mg and 40 mg) in a sample of participants, with a confirmed acromegaly diagnosis that was controlled (mean of IGF1≤ULN) values, on a stable approved dose of octreotide  LAR  or  lanreotide  depot. Enrolment  in  this  study  was  halted  due  to  business reasons/considerations as of 31 March 2020, with 13 participants receiving paltusotine.

The  participants  with  acromegaly  were  responders  to  octreotide  LAR  or  lanreotide  depot.  Of  the randomized subjects (n=7), the percentage who met the responder criteria (IGF1≤ULN) at Week 13 (Visit 13 and Visit 14) was greater in the paltusotine arm, (2 subjects [66.7%]) compared to placebo, (1 subject [25.0%]) with a treatment difference of 41.7% (95% CI: -43.0%, 91.6%). The reduced sample size did not allow for meaningful statistical comparisons between treatments in the RWP.

However, no conclusion can be drawn from this study as this study was halted early and therefore the clinical data are too limited. No washout has been planned between the SRLs and the oral paltusotine. Thus, the carry-over of the effect of SRLs cannot be ruled out given the short duration of 13 weeks. The comparison with patients on placebo cannot be taken into consideration. Moreover, in this study, as in the CRN00808-03 study, the capsules of paltusotine were used while in the phase 3 studies, the SDD tablets are used. This change can have an impact on the dose and on the clinical data.

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

3) Eligible participants from studies CRN00808-03 and CRN00808-02 were invited to participate in the on-going OLE study CRN00808-05 'ACROBAT ADVANCE' ,  which provides long-term safety, tolerability, and efficacy data for 3 doses (10 mg, 20 mg and 30 mg) of paltusotine in tablets (patients were switched from capsules to tablets). Participants are permitted to remain on paltusotine treatment up  to  312  weeks (6  years)  as  long  as  withdrawal  criteria  are  not  met.  In  summary,  long-term paltusotine administration was evaluated up to 272 weeks as of the data cut-off (31 August 2025).

In this on-going study, the starting dose was 10 mg/day. If tolerated, the dose of study medication was to be increased to 20 mg QD at the Week 4 Visit if  the IGF-1 value measured was &gt;ULN range provided  by  the  central  laboratory. At  the  Week  8 Visit,  the  dose  of  study  medication  was  to  be increased to a maximum of 40 mg QD if IGF-1 remained &gt;ULN and the participant was tolerating the study medication. After the switch from the capsules to the tablets, it was also possible to up-titrate the dose to 60 mg/day if IGF-1 &gt; ULN. Doses could be decreased from doses of 20 mg QD or above, to improve tolerability, as necessary, at any time during the study.

Adjunctive acromegaly treatments (ie, dopamine agonists and/or pegvisomant) were permitted to be initiated in combination with paltusotine if IGF-1 levels were still elevated after the participant had been taking  paltusotine  40  mg  QD  (or  maximally  tolerated  lower  dose)  for  2  to  4  weeks,  if  clinically appropriate.

The median duration (range) of exposure was 256.6 (15.0 to 287.7) weeks. Most participants (65.1%) 28/43 were being treated with paltusotine 60 mg QD, and 27.9% 12/43 were being treated with 40 mg QD with (6/11) 54.5% of participants from parent study CRN00808-02 and (24/32, 75%) of participants from parent study CRN00808-03 were receiving paltusotine 60 mg QD,

Most participants who received adjunctive therapy during the study were on a more intensive regimen (adjunctive therapy with cabergoline or pegvisomant; or on pasireotide) prior to entering the parent study CRN00808-03.

The primary endpoint is the incidence of treatment-emergent adverse events (TEAEs) throughout the study.

At the first cut-off date of 5 December 2023, the overall proportion of participants with IGF1 ≤ 1.0 x ULN at Week 3 was 34.9% (95% CI 21.01, 50.93), with highest rates of 42.5% at Week 23 (95% CI 27.04, 59.11) and 54.1% at Weeks 64 and W77 (95% CI 36.92, 70.51).

The main observations of the phase 2 studies are:

-  the  proportion  of  responders  is  higher  in  study CRN00808-02 than  in  study CRN00808-03 and especially in the group of patients treated with paltusotine alone in study CRN00808-03.
-  they have only investigated the efficacy of paltusotine in patients already treated with other SRLs (octreotide, lanreotide, pasireotide) and for study CRN00808-03 , the patients have also been treated with dopamine agonists (cabergoline or bromocriptine), and pegvisomant. The study duration of these studies  is  only  13 weeks, and  this  is  too  short  to  provide  substantial  evidence  of  the  safety  and effectiveness of paltusotine in the treatment of acromegaly due to the potential carry-over of the effects of the other SRLs treatments as no washout has been planned in both studies. In both studies, there were no medically naïve patients, thus the choice of the starting dose for these patients in the phase 3 studies is not based on the results of these studies.

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

- the duration of the effect 'IGF1 levels ≤ ULN' is longer among patients from study CRN00808-02 than in study CRN00808-03.
-  at  the  data  snapshot  date  of  31  August  2025,  at  Week  15, 31.0% of  participants  in  the  overall CRN00808-05 population achieved IGF1 level ≤1.0xULN with a higher proportion of participants from parent study CN00808-02 ( 63.6%) than in participants from parent study CRN00808-03 ( 19.4%) .
- the proportion of IGF-1 responders (IGF1 level ≤1.0xULN) increased over time and was generally maintained through Week 272 in the overall study population and within the subgroups by parent study.
- the proportion of participants with GH level &lt;1.0 ng/mL ranged from 50.0% to 100.0% from Week 23 to Week 272 in the overall study population.
- overall, based on the long-term results of study CRN00808-05, paltusotine in monotherapy was able to maintain IGF-1 levels similar to previous SRL-based therapy, and for participants who were treated with paltusotine and adjunctive therapy, additional treatment benefits were observed, as evidenced by the continued improvement in IGF-1 levels during the study.

## Phase 3 studies

## Study design

1) CRN00808-08 'PATHFNDR-2: Paltusotine Acromegaly Therapy Featuring a Non-Invasive Daily  Regimen' was  a  multicentre,  randomized,  double-blind,  placebo-controlled  study  where participants with non-pharmacologically treated acromegaly including participants with no prior medical therapy,  participants  who  last  received  medical  therapy  at  least  4  months  prior  to  screening,  and participants who were controlled on medical therapy for at least 3 months but agreed to wash out prior to beginning study treatment, were randomly allocated to receive either paltusotine or placebo up to 24 weeks .

After the Screening Period, all eligible participants were randomized in a 1:1 ratio to receive paltusotine or placebo, stratified by prior treatment (naïve or previously treated).

After the first 12 weeks of study treatment including dose titration in the RC phase, no further dose titration was permitted for the remaining 12 weeks of study treatment in the RC phase. This allowed for an additional 12 weeks on a stable dose of study treatment to adequately assess the effectiveness of paltusotine.

Participants who completed the 24-week RC phase or who met rescue criteria and completed the 24week RC phase with rescue medication could continue participation in the OLE phase for up to 200 weeks . This OLE phase is still on-going. At the completion of study target enrolment for the RC phase, remaining participants in the washout group who had entered screening and met eligibility criteria were eligible to directly enrol into the OLE.

2) Study  CRN00808-09 'PATHFNDR-1 ' was a  randomized,  placebo-controlled,  multicentre  study designed  to  evaluate  the  efficacy  and  safety  of  paltusotine  in  participants with  biochemically controlled acromegaly (IGF1 level ≤1.0×ULN) who were switched from monotherapy with injected long-acting octreotide or lanreotide. In the Randomized Controlled (RC) phase, eligible participants were randomized (1:1 ratio) to daily oral administration of paltusotine or placebo when their next dose of octreotide/lanreotide was due to begin, for a Treatment Period of up to 36 weeks . There were stratified by mean Screening IGF1 level (&lt;0.86×ULN, ≥0.86×ULN) and prior treatment (lanreotide, octreotide).

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

The study consisted of 3 periods. The screening period consisted of 2 to 3 visits over 4 to 12 weeks , treatment period ( randomized controlled phase ) of up to 36 weeks with a possible up-titration of paltusotine through week 24 and a fixed dose of paltusotine during the last 12 weeks.

Participants who completed the RC phase could be eligible to enrol and receive paltusotine in the Openlabel Extension (OLE) phase for up to 200 weeks . The RC phase is completed. This report describes results from RC phase. The OLE phase is on-going.

The design of the phase 3 studies has been discussed with EMA and FDA. This design is in line with the line with the Guidance for Acromegaly: Developing Drugs for Treatment (FDA 2023) . The use of placebo was discouraged during the EMA SA, where the use of an active comparator as control arm in the setting of an open label study was suggested, in particular for the population that was already in treatment before enrolment: this, mainly, to prevent an overestimation of efficacy due to possible rebound effects after  suspension  of  the  previous  treatment  in  patients  enrolled  in  the  placebo  arm.  However,  the applicant has chosen placebo-controlled studies, since paltusotine is administered orally and no other oral drugs were approved at the time of the clinical trials; this was considered acceptable also since the main endpoint used was a biochemical measure (IGF-1 level) which is quite a robust marker. A doubledummy long-acting SRL active comparator was also not feasible with injectable forms of somatostatine or lanreotide, in particular patients are accustomed to injections of a viscous SRL solution and would be able to distinguish between an approved SRL injection and a placebo injection, thus breaking the blind; moreover, SRL injections require large-bore needles that can be painful and would place an undue burden on study participants.

## Target population ('inclusion and exclusion criteria)

## 1) Study CRN00808-08

## Main inclusion criteria :

≥18 years of age falling into 1 of the following 3 groups:

- Medically naïve group (Group 1), patients not previously treated with acromegaly medications (including LA-SRLs), with at screening IGF1 ≥1.3×ULN (the lower limit for eligibility is IGF -1 of 1.25×ULN when rounded to 2 decimal places). This group subjects must have had at least 1 pituitary surgery at least 3 months prior to screening.
- Previously Treated group (Group 2) with other SRLs, but no treatment within previous 4 months, with IGF1 ≥1.3×ULN.
- Washout group (Group 3), subjects with stable treatment at screening (no change in dose for 3 months) with octreotide or lanreotide monotherapy, with IGF1 ≤1.0×ULN. Any form of pretrial octreotide or lanreotide monotherapy (long- or short-acting [SC, IM, or oral]) should be washed out and will determine the duration of the Screening Period.

Main exclusion criteria : History of ineffectiveness or intolerance of octreotide or lanreotide treatment. History  of  pituitary  radiation  therapy  within  3  years  of  Screening.  Adrenal  insufficiency,  diabetes insipidus, or central hypogonadism not treated. High-risk pituitary tumour pattern. Several other medical conditions, particularly uncontrolled. Use of certain medications (acromegaly medication use, Group 1 only; Lanreotide depot or octreotide LAR within 16 weeks before Screening, Group 2 only; Pasireotide LAR, within 24 weeks prior to Screening; Pegvisomant within 16 weeks before Screening; Dopamine agonists within 16 weeks before Screening; Any combination of acromegaly medications at Screening; Proton pump inhibitors throughout the study).

## 2) Study CRN00808-09

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

## Main inclusion criteria

Adults &gt;=18 years of age with medically stable, confirmed-active acromegaly and on an approved, stable monotherapy dose of long-acting octreotide or lanreotide, for at least 12 weeks prior to Screening. Previous diagnosis of acromegaly confirmed. For subjects who have had pituitary surgery, there must be documentation of IGF-1 concentration &gt;=1.3×ULN at least 12 weeks after last pituitary surgery. Screening average IGF-1 levels of &lt;=1.0×ULN.

## Main exclusion criteria

Treatment-naïve or treatment-withdrawn acromegaly subjects. History of pituitary radiation therapy. Use of the following medications (Pasireotide LAR, within 24 weeks prior to Screening; Pegvisomant, within 12 weeks before Screening; Dopamine agonists, within 12 weeks before Screening; Short acting somatostatin  analogues,  SA-SSAs,  within  last  12  weeks  before  the  first  dose  of  study  drug.  Other conditions (similar to those for study 08).

The  main  inclusion/exclusion  criteria  of  both  phase  3  studies  were  considered  acceptable  and  were adequate to select a population apt to isolate the effect of paltusotine in acromegaly in both new patients and already controlled patients.

## Dose titration

## Study CRN00808-08

The starting dose of paltusotine in the RC phase was 20 mg QD (1×20 mg tablet of active paltusotine or matching placebo) for oral self-administration. A dose of 20 mg was selected to minimize potential gastrointestinal side effects in participants washed out from medical therapy or naïve to medical therapy. After confirming tolerability with the participant, the dose was increased to 40 mg QD (2×20 mg tablets of active paltusotine or matching placebo) at the Week 2 visit .

Dose down-titration was allowed at any time during the RC phase of the study if the dose was not tolerated in the judgement of the investigator.

At Week 6 , the dose could be increased to 60 mg QD (3×20 mg tablets of active paltusotine or matching placebo) if the 40 mg dose was acceptably tolerated and based on biochemical control and the Week 4 IGF-1 was &gt;0.9×ULN.

In the OLE phase, the starting dose regimen for participants in the OLE phase is paltusotine 20 mg QD at the week 24/EOR visit, and if tolerated, can be increased to 40 mg QD at the Week 26 visit. The dose could be increased to 60 mg QD if the 40 mg dose was acceptably tolerated and based on biochemical control.

The dosing regimen recommended in the RC and in the OLE phase of study CRN00808-08 was not in line with the regimen recommended in the SmPC of paltusotine as in the SmPC, the starting dose is 40 mg/day and increased up to 60 mg/day after at least one week based on IGF-1 levels or clinical signs and symptoms. The applicant agreed to amend the section 4.2 to recommend a dosing regimen for initiation of paltusotine in the medically naïve and uncontrolled patients in line with the dosing regimen investigated in the pivotal study CRN00808-08.

Study CRN00808-09, the starting dose was paltusotine 40 mg QD (or matching placebo), and titrated (down to 20 mg or up to 60 mg QD) based on biochemical status and tolerability.

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

In  the  RC  phase, participants  were  switched  from  a  stable  long-acting  regimen  of  octreotide  or lanreotide to paltusotine (or placebo). The dose titration design of this study was aimed at maintaining participants  in  their  baseline  biochemical  status  of  IGF1  (≤1×ULN) and o ptimizing their  dose  while minimizing the period in which their IGF-1 levels were above baseline.

The starting dose (paltusotine or placebo) was 40 mg QD for oral self-administration. The dose could be increased  from 40  mg  to  60  mg QD  through  Week  24  based  on  IGF-1  response  and  tolerability. Participants who required dose up-titration after Week 24 were considered non-responders. Dose levels could be decreased based on tolerability at any time during the study.

Prior to enrolling in this study, participants had controlled disease on stable doses of existing SRLs for an extended period and would have been acclimated to the safety profile of these treatments. Therefore, a 20 mg starting dose was deemed not necessary in this study, and this is acceptable.

Rescue  criteria  were  provided  for  participants  who  required  resumption  of  standard  acromegaly treatment during the RC phase.

In the OLE phase, the starting paltusotine dose during the OLE phase was 40 mg QD for oral selfadministration.  The  paltusotine  dose  could  be  increased  from  40  mg  to  60  mg  QD  based  on  IGF-1 response, generally once the first IGF-1 result was available.

Rescue  therapy :  IGF-1  levels  and  acromegaly-related  symptoms  were  monitored  throughout  the studies. Criteria for rescue therapy were provided to ensure that participants who experienced worsening of symptoms associated with acromegaly and had increased IGF1 levels (≥1.5×ULN for study 08 and ≥1.3×ULN for study 09) at the highest dose (paltusotine 60 mg QD or placebo) received rescue injections of standard acromegaly therapy and could safely complete the study.

The patients with rescue therapy in the RC were also included in the OLE phase.

## Primary and secondary endpoints

## Primary endpoint

In the 2 phase 3 studies, the primary endpoint was the proportion of participants with IGF1 levels ≤ ULN at EOR. IGF-1 has been reported to be the most reliable biomarker to monitor the efficacy of SRLtreatments in acromegaly. Although serum IGF-1 and GH are known biomarkers of acromegaly disease, given that the secretion of GH is pulsatile, random serum GH concentrations is uncertain. Most symptoms of acromegaly are non-specific, so a normal IGF-1 level in the presence of persistent symptoms has been considered controlled disease.

Key secondary endpoint in study CRN00808 -08: the change from Baseline in IGF-1, in units of ULN, to EOR.

## Main secondary endpoints :

In study CRN00808-08: proportion of participants who achieved IGF-1 &lt;1.3×ULN at EOR; proportion of participants with GH &lt;1 ng/mL at Week 22; change from Baseline in Total Acromegaly Symptoms Diary (ASD) score to EOR.

In study CRN00808-09: change from Baseline in IGF-1, in units of ULN, to EOR; proportion of participants with GH&lt;1.0 ng/mL at Week 34, out of those who had GH &lt;1.0 ng/mL at Baseline; change from Baseline in total ASD*(acromegaly symptoms diary) score to EOR.

The main secondary endpoints are acceptable: the IFG-1 levels are used in clinical practice to assess the effectiveness of acromegaly treatment and to adjust therapy dosage, together with GH levels and clinical symptoms.

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

The other secondary endpoints are similar in phase 2 and phase 3 studies, 'changes from baseline in serum GH levels at EOT (end of trial)', 'change from baseline in IGF-1 levels at EOT' and 'change from baseline in ASD (Acromegaly symptom diary) at EOT' (only in the phase 2 study CRN00808-02). Of note, in the pivotal phase 3 CRN00808-08 , the key secondary endpoint is 'Change from Baseline in IGF-1'.

The ASD is a brief disease-specific symptoms diary developed and validated by the Sponsor in accordance with the FDA Patient-Focused Drug Development Guidance and Patient-Reported Outcome Measures Guidance .  Of  note,  the  dossier  of  validation  of  ASD  is  still  under  review  by  the  European  Medicines Agency (EMA).

The ASD was developed to fill a recognized need in the availability of a patient-reported measure of acromegaly.    The  ASD  was  developed  as  a  novel,  daily  diary to  assess  important  symptoms  of acromegaly from the patient perspective . The ASD consists of 7 symptoms (headache pain, joint pain, sweating, fatigue, weakness in legs, swelling, and numbness or tingling). Two additional items, difficulty sleeping and difficulty with short-term memory, are also included in the diary. The ASD weekly total score ranges from 0 to 70; for all ASD items, a higher score indicates greater severity. The proposed preliminary threshold range to characterize a meaningful change from the patients' perspective for the ASD total is a 4- to 6-point change for improvement or worsening out of a total score of 70 (Martin et al 2023).

The other PROs in the phase 3 studies are exploratory efficacy endpoints such as 'Change from baseline in AcroQoL total score', 'Change from baseline in EuroQoL-5 dimensions-5 levels (EQ-5D-5L)', 'Change from baseline in acromegaly QoL questionnaire (AcroQoL)', 'Patient Global Impression of Improvement (PGI-I) scores' or 'Change from Baseline to EOR in Patient Global Impression of Severity (PGI-S)' and also 'treatment preference questionnaire'.

Other exploratory endpoints are assessed in the phase 3 studies such as 'Change from Baseline in residual pituitary tumour volume', 'Time to initial response of IGF1 ≤1.0×ULN'.

## Statistical considerations

In  both  pivotal  studies CRN00808-08 and CRN00808-09 ,  the  estimand  framework  was  formally applied for the primary endpoint, and the primary estimands are considered acceptable. In both cases, a composite strategy was used to handle intercurrent events such as treatment discontinuation, rescue therapy, prohibited medication, and dose titration. These intercurrent events were incorporated into the definition  of  response,  reflecting  treatment  failure.  The  primary  analysis  method  (exact  logistic regression with fallback to Firth regression) is appropriate given the binary nature of the endpoint and the sample size. The prespecified covariates (including baseline IGF-1 strata and prior treatment strata) are consistently included in both trials.

The statistical methods were well reported in the protocols and the SAPs and can be considered, overall, appropriate to support the testing of the hypotheses for both studies. For the key secondary endpoint, the primary analysis used an ANCOVA model adjusted for treatment, prior treatment strata, and baseline IGF-1.  Intercurrent  events  were  handled  using  a  LOCF  approach,  reflecting  a  while-on-treatment strategy.  A  sensitivity  analysis  based  on  multiple  imputation  using  placebo  on-treatment  data  was performed to assess robustness.

For  secondary  endpoints,  the  Applicant  provided  detailed  estimand  definitions,  including  population, variable, intercurrent event handling, summary measure, and analysis model, which are now considered aligned  with  the  intended  clinical  questions.  The  approach  applied  for  continuous  and  categorical endpoints was consistent with the SAP and was found to be acceptable.

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

The  randomization  scheme  and  blinding/unblinding  procedures  were  well  explained  and  considered adequate. The sample size calculations were comprehensible and reproducible. For study CRN00808-08, the calculation was based on the assumption of equal number of subjects in each stratum and 10% dropout, while in study CRN00808-09 these assumptions were not applied as estimates for the two stratification criteria were not available.

In  study CRN00808-09 ,  an  interim  futility  analysis  was  planned  but  was  never  conducted  due  to accelerated recruitment.

For both studies, the strategy adopted to control the type I error at a two-sided alpha level of 0.05 through the primary, key secondary and secondary endpoints was based on a fixed sequential method with  pre-specified  ordering  by  clinical  relevance.  Among  secondary  endpoints,  the  Applicant  gave particular importance to the change in Total ASD Score, whose validation involved pooled phase II and phase III trials.

Power justifications for the key secondary endpoints were provided and are considered adequate to detect clinically meaningful differences (Question 167).

The conduct of both CRN00808-08 and CRN00808-09 is  considered  adequate.  Both  studies  were randomised, double-blind, and placebo-controlled, with appropriate measures in place to ensure blinding of participants and investigators. No concerns were identified regarding unblinding or bias in treatment administration.  The  stratification  and  randomisation  processes  were  prespecified  and  appropriately implemented.

The  Full  Analysis  Set  (FAS),  consisting  of  all  randomized  participants  (excluding  screen  failures), analysed according to randomization, was used for efficacy analyses in line with the ITT principle. The Per Protocol Set (PPS), including all participants who completed the RC phase without major deviations and with ≥75% treatment compliance, was used for sensitivity analyses. No major protocol deviations affecting safety, confidentiality, or data integrity were reported, and no subjects were excluded from the FAS. Subgroup analyses were well defined in the protocol and are endorsed.

Changes made during the studies were prespecified in SAP amendments. They are not expected to compromise the integrity of the blind or the internal validity of the studies.  Overall, the conduct of both studies was adequate, and no major deviations were identified that would impact the reliability of the results. The methodological framework is generally acceptable, with all major statistical issues resolved.

## Results of all the pivotal phase 3 studies

## Baseline and disposition

In study CRN00808-08 a total of 209 participants were screened, of which 111 participants were randomized (54 participants in the total paltusotine group and 57 participants in the placebo) to the RC phase and included in the FAS.

In study CRN00808-09 a  total  of 115 participants were screened, of which 58 participants were randomized  (30  participants  in  the  total  paltusotine  group  and  28  participants  in  the  placebo)  and included in the FAS.

## OLE Phase disposition .

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

In study CRN00808-08, of the 114 total participants in the OLE phase, 51 participants were from the paltusotine group, and 52 participants were from the placebo group in the RC phase of the study. Eleven participants previously entered the OLE phase directly. These participants were in the washout group at completion of study target enrolment for the RC phase and had entered screening and met eligibility criteria.

. 44 participants from the medically naïve group in the RC phase continued in the OLE phase (21 in the prior paltusotine group and 23 in the prior placebo group).

- . 34 participants from the previously treated group in the RC phase continued in the OLE phase (16 in the prior paltusotine group and 18 in the prior placebo group).

. 36 participants comprised the washout group in the OLE phase, which consists of 14 participants from the  prior  paltusotine  group,  11  participants  from  the  prior  placebo  group,  and  11  participants  who enrolled directly into the OLE phase.

13 participants from the medically naïve group discontinued the study (8 in the prior paltusotine group and 5 in the prior placebo group), 5 participants from the previously treated group discontinued the study (3 in the prior paltusotine group and 2 in the prior placebo group), 2 participants from  the washout group discontinued the study (2 in the prior paltusotine group).

In study CRN00808-09, of the 58 participants , 53 (91.4%) entered the OLE phase: 27 participants were from the paltusotine group and 26 were from the placebo group in the previous RC phase of the study. Two participants (both in the prior placebo group) had dose reductions due to a TEAE.

## Results of the RC phase of study CRN00808-08 (cut-off date: 20 January 2024)

The primary endpoint was met based on the proportion of participants in the paltusotine group (30 of 54 [ 55.6% ]) who achieved biochemical response (IGF1 level ≤ 1.0×ULN) at EOR compared to those in the placebo group (3 of 57 [ 5.3% ]) ( p&lt;0.0001 ).

However, the proportion of responders is mainly driven by the response of group 3 'washout patients'. This was expected as the controlled sub-population of group 3 'washout patients' in study CRN0080808 enriches  the  study  with  the  potential  responders  and  the  efficacy  of  paltusotine  must  be overestimated. Indeed, the patients of the group 3 'washout patients' were already well-controlled by other SRLs and are more susceptible to respond to paltusotine.

Indeed,  the  sensitivity  analysis  of  the  primary  endpoint  by  stratum  showed  a  higher  proportion  of responders among the washout patients compared to the proportions of responders in the other groups: 92.3% (13/14)  of  patients  treated  with  paltusotine  versus 13.3% (2/15)  in  the  placebo  group ( p&lt;0.001) . In the previously treated patients 66.7% (12/18) in the paltusotine group versus 0% in the placebo group ( p&lt;0.001) .  In  the  medically naïve patients, 22.7% (5/12) in the placebo group versus 4.2% (1/24) ( p = 0.1509).

The  sensitivity  results  within  the  stratum  1,  the  effect  of  paltusotine  on  the  primary  endpoint  in 'medically naïve' patients, 22.7% (5/22)) versus 4.2% (1/24) in the group of placebo (p = 0.1509) highlights the lower IGF-1 response observed in this subgroup compared to previously treated patients 66.7% (12/18) (group 2 'already treated patients') versus 0% in the group of placebo and 92.9% (13/14) (group 3 in stratum 2 'washout patients') versus 13.3% (2/15) in the group of placebo. This finding is considered credible given the biological and mechanistic plausibility of a reduced treatment effect in this population.  Since this important subgroup is included in the proposed indication and given that it was the only subgroup with very low effect (and not statistically significant), the Applicant was asked to substantiate the benefit/risk balance of paltusotine in medically naïve patients, and, thus, the inclusion of this subgroup in the indication.

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

In the RC phase, the proportion of medically naïve patients who normalized their IGF-1 levels was consistent with the literature with approximately 17% to 35% ( Katznelson 2014 ) in both drug-naïve and post-operative patients and with 23.6% of medically naïve patients treated with depot injectable octreotide ( Colao et al 2014 ).

The applicant provided arguments to justify the lower efficacy of paltusotine in medically naïve patients, and this was agreed.

In Study CRN00808-08 , participants in the medically naïve group had younger age, higher baseline IGF-1 and GH levels, and larger pituitary tumour size/volume compared to participants in the previously treated and washout groups. However, these clinical features in the medically naïve group are consistent with an unfavourable prognosis based on literature ( Marques-Pamies 2024 ) suggesting that the medically naïve group would be much more challenging to treat than the previously treated and washout groups in this study. Additionally, in the medically naïve group, the mean time since surgery was 34.2 months at baseline, which is much longer than the 3-month recommendation to start medical treatment following pituitary  surgery  depending  on  IGF-1  levels  according  to  acromegaly  consensus  guidelines  ( Melmed 2025 ).

Moreover, in the 13 th  consensus on the multidisciplinary management of acromegaly ( Giustina 2020 ), although the absolute cut-off for defining biochemical control is the upper limit of normal, values slightly higher  than  this  cut-off  (e.g.  within  1.2  to  1.3×ULN)  could  be  considered  as  a  target  of  treatment depending on the clinical scenario. In study CRN00808-08, the proportion of participants with IGF-1 &lt;1.3×ULN at EOR was a secondary efficacy endpoint. The proportion of participants in the medically naïve group who achieved IGF-1 level &lt;1.3×ULN at EOR was 36.4% (8 of 22) in the paltusotine group compared with 8.3% (2 of 24) in the placebo group ( p=0.0495 ).

Other arguments of the applicant to justify the benefit risk of paltusotine in the medically naïve patients are provided in the OLE part.

At EOR, 54 patients were treated with paltusotine: 3 patients ( 5.6% ) were treated with paltusotine 20 mg (1 medically-naïve patient, 1 previously treated with other SRLs and 1 washout patient), 15 patients ( 27.7% ) (3 naïve, 8 uncontrolled and 4 washout) were treated with paltusotine 40 mg and 36 patient s ( 66.7% ) (18 naïve, 9 uncontrolled and 9 washout) were treated with paltusotine 60 mg. Among the medically naive patients, 81.8% are treated with paltusotine 60 mg at the end of the RC phase.

Among responders (30 patients), 1/30 ( 33.33% ) were treated with paltusotine 20 mg (1 washout), 13/30 ( 43.3% ) (3 naïve, 6 uncontrolled and 4 washout) were treated with paltusotine 40 mg and 16/30 ( 53.3% ) (2 naïve, 6 uncontrolled and 8 washout) were treated with paltusotine 60 mg.

It is highlighted that it is unlikely that this issue could be addressed by an increased maintenance dose. Indeed, the long-term efficacy results of the OLE phase of study CRN00808-08 and E-R analyses support paltusotine  60  mg  QD  as  the  maximum  therapeutic  dose.  Indeed,  in  the  E-R  analysis,  based  on  a population maximum IGF-1 level reductions of 57%, paltusotine doses of 20 mg, 40 mg, and 60 mg corresponded to IGF-1 level reductions of approximately 37%, 43%, and 47%, respectively, while 80 mg would result in 49% reduction in IGF-1. These analyses also showed that additional dose increases beyond 60 mg QD would provide only marginal decreases in IGF-1 levels. However, an issue remains for patients with a high level of IGF1 at baseline (≥2.5xULN).

To support the effect at the maintenance dose of 60mg QD, the Applicant has provided an ExposureResponse Analyses conducted in stratum 1 (medically naïve patients, and previously treated patients).

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

Indeed, it was questioned (as part of OC efficacy) whether maintaining a paltusotine dose of 60 mg in patients with high levels of IGF-1 at baseline (1.7xULN) would be beneficial given the predicted effects of a 60 mg dose of paltusotine on IGF-1 dynamics (reduction by 47.5%). Based on the provided plot showing simulations of post-treatment IGF-1 level, for QD 60 mg dosing, by varying levels of baseline IGF-, the Applicant concluded that the maximal dose of paltusotine 60 mg is expected to provide IGF-1 lev els ≤1.0xULN across a broad range of baseline IGF -1 levels (&gt;1.7xULN to 4.0xULN). However, such conclusion can only be agreed for IGF-1 levels at baseline &lt; 2.5x ULN and by considering an IMAX effect of 57%. Therefore, the applicant was asked to compute for each Baseline IGF-1(xULN) of 2.0, 2.5, …, 5.0, the proportion of subjects who are expected to achieve Post-treatment IGF-1(xULN) of 0.5-&lt;1, 1&lt;1.5, 1.5-&lt;2, 2-above, and to thoroughly discuss the results.

The results  of  the  simulation  provided  by  the  applicant  clearly  demonstrated  that  the  probability  of achieving IGF1 normalisation (≤ 1xULN) or near -normalisation (≤ 1.3xULN) markedly decreases with elevated baseline IGF-1 levels.

For baseline IGF-1 of 2.5xULN, 12.8% are predicted to achieve normalisation and less than 40.1% near-normalisation. For baseline IGF-1 of 3xULN, ~6% are predicted to achieve normalisation and less than 23.2% near-normalisation.

Based  on  observed  (vs  predicted)  values  from  the  40  medically  naïve  patients  enrolled  in  study CRN00808-08 with a mean IGF-1 of 2.2xULN at baseline, 42.5% (vs 45%) achieved a normalisation, less than 25% (vs 22.5%) a near-normalisation (IGF-1 levels between 1xULN and 1.5xULN). 32.5% of the medically naïve participants (vs 32.5%) had an IGF-1 over &gt; 1.5 xULN.

These results highlighted that the achievement of IGF-1 normalisation or near normalisation is dependent of IGF-1 baseline and that a 60 mg QD dose cannot achieve IGF-1 normalisation (or near-normalisation) across a broad range of baseline IGF-1 values.

This was consistent with the E-R analysis which showed that paltusotine doses of 20 mg, 40 mg, and 60 mg corresponded to IGF-1 level reductions of approximately 37%, 43%, and 47% , respectively.

In study CRN00808-08, the baseline IGF-1 decreased from 0.8 to 1xULN up to 24 weeks. However, in medically naïve patients with a baseline IGF-1 &gt;2.5xULN, a normalisation or a near normalisation of IGF1 cannot be achieved (suggesting additional measures) and this has been reflected in the SmPC (4.2).

The benefit-risk of paltusotine was considered positive in medically naïve patients in study CRN0080808 at the dose regimen proposed in this study but given that some uncertainties remained as regards the efficacy of paltusotine in patients with high IGF1 (e.g ≥ 2.5) at baseline, section 4.2 of the SmPC was amended accordingly.

Statistically significant differences in favour of paltusotine were observed for the key secondary endpoint and other secondary efficacy endpoints. Results of sensitivity analyses to assess the robustness of the key secondary and secondary efficacy analysis were consistent with the results of secondary efficacy endpoints.

Regarding the key secondary endpoint, the LS means (±SE) change from Baseline to EOT in IGF-1×ULN were -0.819 (±0.0789) for the paltusotine group and 0.087 (±0.0751) for the placebo group, with a treatment difference of -0.906 (95% CI: -1.106, -0.706) ( p&lt;0.0001 ). This result was also confirmed in the different stratum.

Of note, in the medically-naïve patients, the mean IGF-1 levels were about 2.5x ULN at baseline and decrease over time but stayed between 1xULN and 1.5xULN but never decreased under 1xULN while the mean of IGF-1 levels in previously treated patients with o ther SRLs decreased from 2xULN to ≤ 1xULN (from W2 to W24) and for the washout patients, the IGF-1 levels decrease from 1.5x ULN to under

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

1xULN (from W2 to W24). The applicant was asked to discuss this difference between the subgroups of patients.

The applicant assessed the impact of baseline IGF-1 levels and duration of treatment and response with paltusotine, participants were evaluated based on mean baseline IGF 1 levels of &gt;2.5 ×ULN and ≤2.5 ×ULN in the medically naïve group, &gt;2.0 and ≤2.0 ×ULN in the previously treated group, and &gt;1.5 ×ULN and ≤1.5 ×ULN in the washout group, with a data snapshot date of 21 May 2025.

Among the medically naïve patients with IGF-1 levels &gt; 2.5x ULN, although there was a substantial reduction  of  IGF-1  levels  during  the  first  two  weeks  of  treatment,  mean  IGF-1  levels  continued  to decrease with paltusotine treatment during the RC Phase and the OLE phase after the dose titration reaching near normal mean IGF-1 levels at Week 72 but stayed above 1xULN .  This  supports  the recommendation of a warning in section 4.2 for patients with high IGF-1 levels at baseline (e.g &gt; 2.5x ULN).

At EOR, the proportion of participants who achieved biochemical response IGF-1 level &lt;1.3×ULN was 66.7% (36  of  54)  in  the  paltusotine  group  compared  with 14.0% (8  of  57)  in  the  placebo  group ( p&lt;0.0001 ).

This result was also confirmed in the different stratum. Of note, based on the 13th consensus on the multidisciplinary management of acromegaly ( Giustina 2020 ),  values slightly higher than this cut-off (e.g. within 1.2 to 1.3×ULN) could be considered as a target of treatment.

At EOR, the proportion of participants who achieved GH level &lt;1.0 ng/mL was 57.4% (31 of 54) in the paltusotine group compared with 17.5% (10 of 57) in the placebo group ( p&lt;0.0001 ). This result was confirmed by sensitivity analyses.

The LS means (±SE) change from Baseline to EOT in total ASD score were -2.669 (±1.4221) for the paltusotine group and 2.754 (±1.3641) for the placebo group with a treatment difference of -5.423 (95% CI: -9.070, -1.776), indicating an improvement and stabilization of symptoms for the paltusotine group, and worsening of symptoms for the placebo group. This difference was statistically significant ( p=0.0039 ).

There was a better improvement of acromegaly symptoms in the group of washout patients compared to medically naïve patients and compared to the group of previously treated patients. However, the LS means (±SE) change from Baseline to EOT in most bothersome symptoms were -0.553 (±0.4219) in the paltusotine group and 0.357 (±0.3770) in the placebo group, with a treatment difference of -0.910 (95% CI: -1.976, 0.157) in favour of paltusotine but not significant (nominal p=0.0935 ).

Results of multiple exploratory endpoint analyses also favoured paltusotine 'change from baseline to W22 in GH level', 'proportion of participants who achieved GH level &lt;2.5 ng/mL' were statistically significant  for  the  group  paltusotine  compared  to  placebo,  the  Kaplan-Meier  median  time  to  initial response (IGF1≤1.0×ULN for 2 consecutive visits) was 44 days (15.0 days, not estimable [NE]) for the paltusotine group and was not reached (NE, NE) for the placebo group. Paltusotine was associated with stable or reduced pituitary tumour size as measured by MRI.

Regarding the exploratory PROs endpoints, results from the Treatment Preference Questionnaire at Week 24 indicated that 77.8% (42 of 54) of participants in the paltusotine group and 63.2% (36 of 57) of participants in the placebo group preferred oral medication over previous injections for acromegaly. The differences of change from baseline to EOR (EOT at W24) in the EQ-5D-5L health index score and in the PGI-I were significant in favour of paltusotine vs placebo.

However, the difference of the LS means (±SE) change from Baseline to EOT in PGI-S and in AcroQoL composites and standardized scores were not significant between paltusotine and placebo.

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

Clinically significant tumour volume reduction (i.e. &gt;20% reduction in tumour volume) was observed in 4 (11.8%) participants in the paltusotine group and in 0 participants in the placebo group. Four (4) placebo participants had ≥20% increase in tumour volume compared to zero (0) paltusotine participants.

Of note, a minority ( 3.7% , 2 of 54) of acromegaly participants who received paltusotine required rescue medication during 24-week treatment period to maintain disease control as opposed to 22.8% (13 of 57) of those participants who received placebo (nominal p=0.0059 ). This showed that paltusotine is effective in acromegaly.

## Results of the OLE phase of study CRN00808-08 (data snapshot dates: 21 May 2025 and 31 August 2025)

A total of 114 participants enrolled in the OLE phase of study CRN00808-08. Fifty-one participants were  from  the  prior  paltusotine  group,  52  participants  were  from  the  prior  placebo  group,  and  11 participants were previously screened but not randomized and entered the OLE phase directly.

At the data snapshot date of 21 May 2025:

- 31 participants from the medically naïve group were continuing in the study. Indeed, a total of 13 participants from the medically naïve group discontinued the study during the OLE phase. IGF-1 levels were elevated in all these 13 participants.

- 29 participants from the previously treated group were continuing in the study (data on file). A total of 5 participants from the previously treated group discontinued the study during the OLE phase. IGF1 levels were elevated in all these 5 participants.

- 38 participants in the washout group are continuing in the study (data on file). Durning the OLE phase, 2  participants discontinued  the  study  with  elevated  IGF-1  levels.  11  additional  washout participants were directly included in the OLE phase.

Overall, there were 10 participants in the medically naïve group, 4 participants in the previously treated group and 1 in the washout group who were withdrawn following physician decision. All had high levels of IGF-1 and most of them on 60 mg/day of paltusotine and none had received adjunctive therapy during the OLE phase. The applicant was asked to clarify the withdrawal of 10 medically naïve participants. 7 participants were in the prior paltusotine group and 3 were in the prior placebo group.

None of these participants achieved normal IGF-1 levels at any time during the study. The applicant justified that the withdrawal of 10 participants in the OLE phase was consistent with the protocol. The reasons for not achieving a normalization of the IGF-1 levels were that they were younger in age, with higher  baseline  IGF-1  and  GH  levels,  and  larger  pituitary  tumor  size/volume  compared  with  the previously  treated  and  washout  groups.  These  parameters  are  known  to  be  associated  with  an unfavorable prognosis.

Although the withdrawal was higher among the medically naïve participants, there was a majority of participants in the OLE phase of study CRN00808-08, with approximately 80% of participants overall and 68% of medically naïve participants with durable and sustained clinical benefits of paltusotine.

This was consistent with the results of other SRL treatments of acromegaly indicated in medically naïve patients. Indeed, the rates of discontinuation due to treatment failure in the literature were 17.9% for oral octreotide ( Samson 2020 ).

After being treated with placebo for 24 weeks in the RC phase, the time to normalization (IGF-1 &lt;1×ULN) with paltusotine in the OLE is similar as for participants who were originally randomized to paltusotine in the RC phase.

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

IGF-1 levels for the prior placebo group, and the directly enrolled group showed a rapid decrease over the first two weeks in the OLE phase and continued maintenance and durability of mean IGF-1 levels ≤1.0×ULN were observed in participants in the prior paltusotine group for up to 120 weeks , while a decrease in mean IGF-1 level was observed over the initial 2 weeks in the prior placebo group.

The starting paltusotine dose for the OLE was 20 mg resulting in a temporary down titration for prior paltusotine participants from final dose in the RC phase for most participants and a subsequent increase in IGF-1 levels at Week 26. The starting dose of paltusotine in the OLE phase was 20 mg QD, and most participants who were receiving 40 mg QD or 60 mg QD at the end of the RC phase were anticipated to up-titrate to 40 mg QD or 60 mg QD during the OLE phase.

Most participants who were responders in the RC phase continued to be responders in the OLE phase.

No reduced effectiveness of paltusotine overtime was observed in the OLE phase of study CRN0080808 .

At the data snapshot date of 31 August 2025, the proportion of participants who achieved IGF-1 level ≤1.0×ULN ranged from 51.3% to 100% from Week 36 to Week 144 for the overall population (51.0% to 100% for the prior paltusotine group and 37.0% to 100% for the prior placebo group) and ranged from 50.0% to 80.0% for the directly enrolled group from Week 36 to Week 120.

The proportion of participants who achieved IGF-1 level &lt;1.3×ULN ranged from 69.9% to 100% from Week 36 to Week 144 for the overall population ( 68.6% to 100% for the prior paltusotine group and 65.4% to 100% for  the  prior  placebo group) and was 100% from Week 36 to Week 120 for the directly enrolled group.

These  proportions  of  participants  with  IGF-1  level ≤ 1.0 × ULN  with  paltusotine  monotherapy  was consistent and even better than the acromegaly consensus guidelines which stated that the long-term IGF-1 normalization rate with existing SRLs is approximately 30% to 55 % based on results of multiple studies ( Giustina 2020 ).

These proportions were also consistent or improved when compared with the 56% response rate observed for participants in the paltusotine group at the end of the RC Phase of the study.

Overall, the results of the OLE phase of study CRN00808-08 suggest that all the participants experience over time a durable and/or additional reduction in IGF-1 level and stable/reduced tumour volume for an extended period of time in the OLE phase. The proportion of responders among the medically naïve patients increased overtime in the OLE phase showing that these patients may need a longer duration of treatment to normalize their IGF-1 levels especially if their IGF-1 levels were elevated at baseline (≥2. 5 xULN) and this was also consistent with the literature ( Marques-Pamies 2024 ).  This  has  been reflected in the SmPC for the prescribers of this new medicine (section 4.2).

The results of the OLE are difficult to interpret as this phase is open and there is no control arm and due to smaller groups of patients especially after W84.

Another point is the need of adjunctive therapies in the OLE phase of study CRN00808-08 for some patients. In case of evidence of efficacy of paltusotine at the maximal dose of 60 mg/day after 6 months but patients have not achieved the normalization of IGF-1 levels after 6 months, in the OLE phase of study  CRN00808-08,  there  was  a  possibility  to  use  adjunctive  therapies  such  as  cabergoline  or pegvisomant as proposed from W48.

At the data snapshot date of 21 May 2025, in study CRN00808-08 , most of the participants were still on  monotherapy  of  paltusotine.  Indeed, 15.8  % (18/114)  participants  (12  medically  naïve  and  6 previously treated) used adjunctive therapies from W48. The use of adjunctive therapies was observed more  frequently  in  participants  receiving  prior  placebo  (23.1%;  12  of  52  participants)  than  in participants receiving prior paltusotine (11.8%; 6 of 51 participants). Thus, it is difficult to interpret the long-term results, and in which extent the increase of the proportion of medically naïve patients with IGF-1 levels ≤ ULN over the OLE phase is only related to the effect of paltusotine as monotherapy.

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

Thus, the use of adjunctive therapies did not lead to a significant increase of the proportion of patients with IGF-1 levels ≤ ULN nor a significant increase of the proportion of patients with IGF-1 levels &lt; 1.3x ULN. Only one participant who used adjunctive therapy achieved IGF-1 levels ≤ ULN and 2 participants who used adjunctive therapies achieved IGF-1 levels &lt; 1.3xULN.

This demonstrates that the addition of adjunctive therapy did not have at this stage an impact on the long-term efficacy results of paltusotine.

At the data snapshot date of 21 May 2025, the applicant provided a post-hoc analysis in excluding the 18 participants who received adjunctive therapies in study CRN00808-08.

When  these  participants  were  excluded  from  the  response  analyses,  the  proportion  of  participants without  adjunctive  therapy  who  had  IGF-1 ≤ 1.0  x  ULN  were  quite  similar  to  the  proportion  of participants  with  IGF-1 ≤ 1.0  x  ULN  in  the  overall  group (62.0% without adjunctive vs 54.7% overall at Week 60 ;  74.3% without adjunctive vs 63.6% overall at Week 72 ;  73.8% without adjunctive vs 65.4% overall at Week 84 ).  Similar  trends  continued  up  through  Week  120.  The proportion of participants with IGF-1 ≤ 1.3 x ULN were also similar between the 2 groups at these visits.

The use of adjunctive therapies is usually proposed in the therapeutically strategy of treatment of the acromegaly patients but there are currently no clinical data with a high level of evidence that can support the use of these therapies in association to paltusotine. The clinical data were collected in the OLE phase of the study 08 which has an open, non-comparative design and enrolled a low number of patients. Thus, it was not possible to recommend adjunctive therapies in the SmPC.

## Subgroup analysis

In study CRN00808-08 , dividing subject based upon IGF1 values at baseline, the group with IGF1 &gt; 1.78xULN had lower responder rate than IGF1 &lt; 1.78xULN in the paltusotine group (32% vs 76%); this can be expected based on the assumption that more severe disease can be more difficult to treat. Analyses  for  subgroups  based  on  other  common  baseline  characteristics  (e.g.  sex,  weight)  did  not highlight differences.

## Post-hoc analysis

A post-hoc analysis requested by FDA was performed to determine the efficacy of paltusotine among a group of medically naïve patients and not responders to other acromegaly treatments or unknown status and among a group of patient responders to other acromegaly treatments (remotely or recently).

The response is higher in the group of patients already controlled with any previous treatment versus group of patients not controlled with any treatment ( 75% vs 57%). The response to paltusotine is lower  in  the  group  of  medically  naïve  patients,  uncontrolled  patients  with  other  SRLs  and  status unknown: 34.4%. The difference between treatment and placebo are all significant whatever the group or subgroup of patients.

This post-hoc analysis also shows that the response is lower in the group of uncontrolled patients mainly driven  by  the  medically  naïve  patients  (22.7%)  who  have  a  lower  response  to  paltusotine than  the uncontrolled patients and patients with unknown status

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

## Results of the RC phase of study CRN00808-09 (cut-off date: 5 December 2023)

The study met statistical significance ( p&lt;0.0001 ) on the primary endpoint, based on the proportion of participants in the paltusotine group (25 of 30 [ 83.3%]) who maintained biochemical response (IGF-1 level ≤ 1.0×ULN) at EOR compared to those in the placebo group (1 of 28 [ 3.6%]).

At EOR, there were 14 patients in the paltusotine 40 mg group and 16 patients in the paltusotine 60 mg group.  Among  the 25/30  patients who maintained  IGF1  levels  ≤  ULN,  14/25  ( 56% )  in  the paltusotine 40 mg group and 11/16 ( 44% ) in the paltusotine 60 mg group.

Thus, the dose of 40 mg/day with a maximal dose of 60 mg/day is more optimal for maintenance of treatment  in  well-controlled  patients  than  initiation  of  treatment  in  medically  naïve  patients  and uncontrolled patients. However, at the end of the RC phase, 53% of patients are treated with paltusotine 60 mg. Therefore, the maintenance of the efficacy of paltusotine was also questionable in the long-term in well-controlled patients with other SRLs at baseline.

The sensibility analysis to assess the robustness of the primary endpoints showed similar significant results ( p &lt; 0.0001 ) in the completers, PP analysis and in the group of dose titration during the fixed dose period (no patient).

Significant  differences  in  favour  of  paltusotine  were  observed  for  all  secondary  efficacy endpoints. Results  of  pre-specified  sensitivity  analyses  to  assess  the  robustness  of  the  secondary efficacy analyses were consistent with the secondary efficacy endpoint results.

- The LS means (±SE) for the change from Baseline to EOT in IGF-1×ULN were 0.042 (±0.093) for the paltusotine group and 0.833 (±0.096) for the placebo group, with a treatment difference of -0.791 (95% CI: -1.057 to -0.525) ( p&lt;0.0001 ).
- The proportion of participants who maintained GH level &lt;1.0 ng/mL , out of those who had GH level &lt;1.0 ng/mL at Baseline at Week 34, was 87.0% (20 of 23) in the paltusotine group compared with 27.8% (5 of 18) in the placebo group (p=0.0003).
- The LS means (±SE) for the change from Baseline to EOT in total ASD score were -0.606 (±1.504) for  the  paltusotine  group  and 4.588  (±1.593) for  the  placebo  group,  indicating  stabilization  of symptoms for the paltusotine group and worsening of symptoms for the placebo group, with a treatment difference of -5.164 (95% CI: -9.536 to -0.792) (p=0.0216).
-  The  proportion  of  participants  who  maintained  IGF-1  level  &lt;1.3×ULN:  93.3%  (28  of  30)  in  the paltusotine group compared with 28.6% (8 of 28) in the placebo group (nominal p&lt;0.0001).

## Results of multiple exploratory endpoint analyses also favoured paltusotine.

- The LS means (±SE) for the change from Baseline to Week 34 in GH level (ng/mL) were -0.239 (±0.300)  for  the  paltusotine  group  and 1.721 (±0.309)  for  the  placebo  group,  with  a  treatment difference of -1.960 (95% CI: -2.817 to -1.103) (nominal p&lt;0.0001 ).
- The proportion of participants who maintained IGF1 level ≤1.0×ULN and GH level &lt;1.0 ng/mL from Baseline to EOR was 73.9% (17 of 23) in the paltusotine group compared with 5.6% (1 of 18) in the placebo group (nominal p&lt;0.0001 ).
- The Kaplan-Meier median time to loss of biochemical control (first IGF-1&gt;1.0×ULN for 2 consecutive visits), where participants who discontinued treatment prior to reaching IGF-1&gt;1.0×ULN were censored at the time of the last assessment prior to treatment discontinuation, was not reached (IQR: 141.0 days, NE) for the paltusotine group and was 84.0 days (IQR: 58.0, 85.0) for the placebo group. Similar results were observed for the median time to loss of biochemical control using IGF-1&gt;1.3×ULN for 2 consecutive visits.

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

- The proportion of participants who received rescue therapy was 6.7% (2 of 30) in the paltusotine group and 60.7% (17 of 28) in the placebo group (nominal p&lt;0.0001 ), affirming the treatment effect of paltusotine versus placebo.

- Results from the 'treatment Preference questionnaire' (TPQ) at Week 36 indicated that the majority of participants  (approximately  60%  in  each  treatment  group)  preferred  oral  medication  over  previous injections for acromegaly.

-  The  treatment  difference  for Acro-QoL,  EQ-5D-5L  health  index  and  PGI-I  at  EOR between paltusotine and placebo were all not significant.

## OLE Phase of study CRN00808-09 (data snapshot dates: 21 May 2025 and 31 October 2025)

At the data snapshot date of 31 October 2025), a total 53 total participants (27 from the prior paltusotine group and 26 from the prior placebo group) were enrolled in the OLE Phase of Study CRN00808-09.

Eight participants ( 15.1%) overall discontinued the OLE Phase of the study (3 from the prior paltusotine group and 5 from the prior placebo group).

Only 1 participant received adjunctive therapy in the OLE Phase from W60.

The starting dose of paltusotine in the OLE Phase was 40 mg QD day, and most participants who were receiving 60 mg/day at the end of the RC Phase were anticipated to up-titrate to 60 mg/day during the OLE Phase.

The proportion of participants who achieved IGF-1 level ≤ 1.0 × ULN ranged from 74.5% to 93.3% from Week 48 to Week 180 for the overall population ( 50.0% to 96.0% for the prior paltusotine group and 77.3% to 100% for the prior placebo group).

The proportion of participants who achieved IGF-1 level &lt;1.3×ULN ranged from 95.7% to 100% from Week 48 to Week 180 for the overall population ( 92.0% to 100% for the prior paltusotine group and 92.9% to 100% for the prior placebo group).

Continued maintenance and durability of mean IGF-1 levels ≤ 1.0 × ULN were observed in participants in the prior paltusotine group up to Week 180, while a decrease in mean IGF-1 level was observed over the initial 2 weeks in the prior placebo group that was maintained to Week 180.

Mean GH levels in the  OLE  Phase  showed long-term  maintenance  and  durability  from  OLE  Baseline through Week 180.

Mean ASD Total Scores (symptom burden) generally remained stable throughout the OLE Phase.

The effects of paltusotine are maintained over time as regards to the maintenance of the normalization of IGF-1 levels and GH levels but the acromegaly symptoms slightly increased over time and no clinically significant changes in the tumour volume was highlighted from OLE baseline until W84. At the end of the RC phase, most patients were treated with paltusotine 60 mg/day. The use of adjunctive therapies was permitted from W60 in case of lower efficacy and only one participant used adjunctive therapies at the data snapshot date of 31 August 2025.  The results of the OLE phase of study CRN00808-09 are still on-going and the applicant provided the interim results at the data snapshot date of 31 August 2025 (up to W192) .

Updated efficacy results of the OLE phase of study CRN00808-09 (as of the data snapshot date of 31 August  2025)  show  that  paltusotine  continues  to  provide  long-term  biochemical  disease  control  of acromegaly, with 75% to 93% of  participants overall maintaining IGF-1 normalization (IGF 1 level ≤1.0×ULN) up to Week 180, which represents up to 3.5 years of treatment for participants treated with paltusotine in the RC phase and continuing with paltusotine in the OLE phase and up to approximately 2.75  years  of  paltusotine  treatment  for  participants  previously  treated  with  placebo  who  received paltusotine in the OLE phase. These results showing the long-term efficacy of paltusotine are consistent with and potentially better than the long-term IGF-1 normalization rates reported with existing SRL therapies, which are approximately 30% to 55% based on results of multiple studies ( Giustina 2020 ). Paltusotine also provided long-term stabilization in GH level and symptom burden as measured by the ASD Total Score. The overall efficacy profile of paltusotine remains unchanged based on evaluation of the additional data and demonstrates sustained clinical treatment benefits with paltusotine.

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

## Subgroup analysis

No notable difference was observed for any subgroup, and the paltusotine effect was consistent across subgroups.

## Post-Hoc analysis

Overall, a post-hoc analysis was performed to evaluate the data for participants without prior surgery in the  paltusotine  clinical  studies.  The  majority  of  participants  enrolled  had  undergone  prior  surgical intervention  for  their  pituitary  tumours.  However,  a  small  subset  of  participants  (N=20)  across  the acromegaly studies were surgically naïve but had received pharmacological intervention prior to study enrolment. Of note, all the medically naïve patients have undergone pituitary surgery. The effects of paltusotine in terms of lowering IGF1 levels or maintaining normal IGF1 levels were confirmed also in this subgroup of patients but no definitive conclusion can be drawn given the low number of patients. Therefore, this post-hoc analysis cannot have an impact on the wording of the indication as regards the place of paltusotine before or after surgery or if surgery is not an option.

## 5.3.7.2. Conclusions on the clinical efficacy

An  advantage  of  paltusotine  is  its  oral  administration,  that  could  improve  patient's  compliance  to treatment and reduce discomfort due to long-term treatment with injections.

Based on the results  of  the  pivotal  studies  and  their  OLE  phases  as  well  as  supportive  studies,  on literature  and  based  on  PK  clinical  data,  the  benefit-risk  of  paltusotine  was  considered  positive  in medically  naïve  patients  in  study  CRN00808-08  at  the  dose  regimen  proposed  in  this  study.  But uncertainties remain on the possibility of patients with high IGF1 levels at baseline (i.e ≥ 2.5xULN) including the medically naïve patients to normalize their IGF-1 levels, even at the 60 mg QD dose in monotherapy. Some of them will need longer duration of treatment and/or will need adjunctive therapies to achieve the normalization of their IGF-1 levels and some of them will never normalize their IGF-1 levels. This has been appropriately reflected for the prescribers in section 4.2 of the SmPC.

## 5.4. Clinical safety

Please refer to the table of studies in section 5.3.2.

For the purpose of this document, the following definitions apply:

'Adverse event - AE' means any untoward medical occurrence in a subject to whom a medicinal product is administered and which does not necessarily have a causal relationship with this treatment.

'Serious adverse event - SAE' means any untoward medical occurrence that at any dose requires inpatient hospitalisation or prolongation of existing hospitalisation, results in persistent or significant disability or incapacity, results in a congenital anomaly or birth defect, is life-threatening, or results in death. The definition (in line with ICH E2A) includes important medical events that may not be immediately life-threatening or result in death or hospitalisation but may jeopardise the patient or may require intervention to prevent one of the other outcomes listed in the definition above.

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

'Adverse drug reaction - ADR' means any untoward and unintended response to a medicinal product related to any dose administered, for which, after a thorough assessment, a causal relationship between the medicinal product and the adverse event is at least a reasonable possibility, based for example, on their comparative incidence in clinical trials, or findings from epidemiological studies and/or on an evaluation of causality from individual case reports.

## 5.4.1. Safety data collection

The applicant pooled safety data of paltusotine from 5 acromegaly studies into 2 analysis populations: Primary group and Supportive group.

The  primary  group  included  data  from 84  patients included  in  the  two  RC  trials:  CRN00808-08 (treatment of acromegaly) and CRN00808-09 (maintenance therapy of acromegaly). In these 2 studies the to-be marketed formulation (15% drug load SDD tablet) was used, at doses ranging from 20 mg to 60 mg QD in study CRN00808-08 and 40 mg to 60 mg QD in study CRN00808-09.

The supportive group included data from 233 patients included 5 acromegaly studies (completed studies CRN00808-02  and  CRN00808-03,  on-going  study  CRN00808-05,  completed  RC  phases  of  studies CRN00808-08 and CRN00808-09, and on-going OLE phases of studies CRN00808-08 and CRN0080809), exposed for a maximum duration of 4 years. The doses ranged from 10 mg QD to 60 mg QD and included both the capsule and to-be marketed tablet formulation.

## 5.4.2. Patient exposure

The safety profile of paltusotine was based primarily on pooled data from the RC phases of the pivotal phase  3  studies  (CRN00808-08  and  CRN00808-09),  which  included  84  participants  exposed  to paltusotine for up to 36 weeks at doses ranging from 20 mg to 60 mg QD. A pooled analysis of safety data  from  the  2  phase  3  studies  (including  data  from  RC  and  OLE  phases)  and  3  phase  2  studies (CRN00808-03, CRN00808-02, and CRN00808-05) was also performed and included 233 participants exposed to paltusotine with a maximum duration of 4 years. Additional support for safety is provided by safety data from 297 participants exposed to paltusotine in non-acromegaly studies (9 phase 1 studies in healthy participants, excluding the Japan studies, and 1 phase 1 study in participants with hepatic impairment [at single or repeat doses of paltusotine ranging from 1.25 mg to 240 mg]. A total of 36 participants with carcinoid syndrome were treated with paltusotine at repeat doses of up to 120 mg QD in  a  phase  2  study.  Overall,  583  participants  have  been  exposed  to  paltusotine  across  the  clinical program. This approach was acceptable.

## Overall Extent of Exposure in Participants with Acromegaly in the Primary Group

In study CRN00808-08 , during the RC phase, all randomized participants (111 participants, including 54 in the paltusotine group and 57 in the placebo group) received at least 1 dose of study treatment. Of the 54 participants randomized in the total paltusotine group, 3 participants received paltusotine 20 mg QD, 15 participants received paltusotine 40 mg QD and 36 participants received paltusotine 60 mg QD as their final dose. The median duration (range) of exposure was 24.0 weeks in both paltusotine (range: 7.6 to 24.7 weeks) and placebo (range: 1.1 to 25.0 weeks) groups. A total of 52 participants (96.3%) in the paltusotine group and 50 participants (87.7%) in the placebo group received ≥ 3 months of study treatment. Three of 54 participants in the paltusotine group and 15 of 57 participants in the placebo group discontinued study treatment due to TEAEs; of these, 2 and 13 received rescue therapy. Treatment compliance was 97.6% in the paltusotine group and 99.6% in the placebo group.

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

In study CRN00808-09 , during the RC phase, all 58 randomized participants received at least 1 dose of  study  treatment  and  were  included  in  the  Safety  Analysis  Set.  Of  the 30  participants  in  the paltusotine  group ,  14  participants  received  paltusotine  40  mg  QD  and  16  participants  received paltusotine 60 mg QD as their final dose. The median duration (range) of exposure was 36.0 weeks (16.1 to 36.3 weeks) in the paltusotine group and 22.1 weeks (8.0 to 36.9 weeks) in the placebo group. A total of 28 participants (93.3%) in the paltusotine group and 14 participants (50.0%) in the placebo group received ≥ 6 to ≤ 9 months of study treatment. Two of 30 participants (6.7%) in the paltusotine group and 17 of 28 participants in the placebo group discontinued study treatment due to TEAEs and received rescue therapy. Treatment compliance was 97.9% in the paltusotine group and 99.6% in the placebo group.

## Overall Extent of Exposure in the Participants with Acromegaly in Supportive Group

In study CRN00808-03 , a total of 47 participants were exposed to paltusotine (25 in Group 1, 10 in Group 2, 5 in Group 3, 4 in Group 4, and 3 in Group 5) and were included in the Safety Analysis Set. Of these, 41 (87.2%) completed 13 weeks of treatment. Median treatment compliance was 100% based on study drug dosing diaries and study drug accountability. Treatment compliance ranged from 67 to 114% based on study drug accountability and 44 to 100% based on study drug dosing diaries.

In study CRN00808-02 , a total of 13 participants were exposed to paltusotine and comprised the Safety Analysis Set. Due to the early discontinuation of the study, most participants did not reach the highest possible dose based on the titration algorithm. Overall, during the study (including during the randomized withdrawal period), 3 participants up-titrated to the highest allowable dose of 30 mg, 9 participants up-titrated to 20 mg, and 4 remained at the initial dose of 10 mg. Twelve of 13 (92.3%) participants  completed  9  weeks  of  dosing  with  paltusotine.  Seven  of  11  participants  entered  the Randomized Withdrawal Period; of these, 3 (23.1%) participants received paltusotine for 4 additional weeks, and 4 (30.8%) received placebo. Only 1 participant discontinued study treatment early, at Week 3. All but 1 participant (who was incorrectly up-titrated from 10 mg to 20 mg and had a compliance of 80.9%) had a compliance of at least 90% regardless of calculation methods.

In Study CRN00808-05 , a total of 43 participants were exposed to at least 1 dose of paltusotine in this study and comprised the Safety Population (ie, the Safety Analysis Set). Overall, the median time from the end of parent study to the start of study CRN00808-05 was 103 days (range: 1 to 889 days).

Of the 43 participants who received paltusotine, the majority received either 60 mg (24 participants, 55.8%) or 40 mg (15 participants, 37.2%) as their final dose; 1 participant each (2.3%) received 10 mg, 20 mg, and 30 mg paltusotine as their final dose.

In the OLE phase of study CRN00808-08 , a total of 114 participants were exposed to at least 1 dose of  paltusotine  and  were  included  in  the  Safety  Analysis  Set.  Of  the  113  participants  in  the  total population, 50 participants received paltusotine and 52 participants received placebo in the RC phase, and 11 participants enrolled directly into the OLE.

In the OLE phase of study CRN00808-09 , a total of 53 participants were exposed to at least 1 dose of paltusotine in this study and were included in the Safety Analysis Set. Of the 53 participants in the total population, 27 participants received paltusotine and 26 participants received placebo in the RC phase.

The patient exposure in the clinical trials with the indication of treatment of acromegaly was considered of sufficient size to enable the safety assessment. The length of the follow-up (approximately 4 years) was considered sufficient.

## 5.4.3. Adverse events

## Overview of Adverse Events in the Primary Group

Table 59 provides an overview of TEAEs for the RC phases of the individual phase 3 studies and in the Primary Group overall. Paltusotine was well tolerated in the pivotal randomized controlled phase 3 studies and there were no new, unexpected safety findings. The overall safety profile was largely comparable between the 2 phase 3 studies during the RC phases.

Percentages are calculated based on the number of participants in the Safety Population. TEAEs of special interest are defined as symptoms related to acromegaly (acromegaly-related TEAEs).

In the primary group, TEAEs were reported at similar rates in the paltusotine and placebo groups (86.9% vs. 90.6%, respectively). Most were mild or moderate. Severe TEAEs were more common in placebo (9.4%) than in paltusotine group (2.4%) and only 2 severe not related TEAEs occurred in placebo group, both in study CRN00808-08 (headache and pain in extremity).

No SAEs were reported in the paltusotine group. In contrast, two SAEs occurred in the placebo group (benign parathyroid tumour and cholecystitis).

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

TEAEs leading to treatment discontinuation occurred more frequently in the placebo group than in the paltusotine group.
