RC = randomized controlled

MedDRA version 24.1. Percentages are calculated based on the number of participants in the Safety Population. The Primary Group comprises the RC Phases of Studies CRN00808-08 and CRN00808-09.

The  most  commonly  reported  adverse  events  in  the  primary  group  were  gastrointestinal  disorders, including diarrhoea, abdominal pain, and nausea. These events were more frequent in the paltusotine group, particularly among patients who were non-pharmacologically treated (CRN00808-08) compared to  those  previously  treated  with  SRLs  (CRN00808-09).  This  may  reflect  a  period  of  physiological adaptation in SRL-naïve patients, where initial GI and pancreatic effects of octreotide analogues are known and expected. GI [especially diarrhoea (50% vs. 33.3%)] and hepatobiliary AEs (18.2% vs. 0) occurred more frequently in treatment-naïve patients.

In  clinical  trial  CRN00808-08,  treatment-naïve  patients  initiated  paltusotine  at  20  mg,  allowing progressive pharmacodynamic adaptation and minimising early GI and hepatobiliary intolerance.

To ensure tolerability and consistency with clinical trial conditions, the recommended starting dose in medically naïve patients was set to be 20 mg once daily and titrate up to 40 mg after tolerance was established (two weeks). This approach is standard for other SRLs and aligns with the principle of using the  lowest  effective  dose  to  minimise  dose-dependent  ADRs.  The  SmPC  section  4.2  was  aligned accordingly.

Post-initiation of treatment, GI events or recurrence of similar adverse reactions may occur during longterm treatment, particularly after dose increases. To ensure adequate management of adverse events throughout therapy, the SmPC wording was revised to allow for a temporary dose reduction until adverse reactions would be resolved.

Musculoskeletal  disorders  were  more  frequently  reported  in  the  previously  SRL-treated  patients (CRN00808-09)  compared  to  non-pharmacologically  treated  patients  (CRN00808-08)  [46.7%  vs. 27.8%].  The  observed  imbalance  between  studies  likely  reflects  differences  in  baseline  disease characteristics (e.g., longer disease duration, older age, higher prevalence of pre-existing musculoskeletal conditions in study CRN00808-09) rather than a treatment or exposure-related safety signal.

AEs within the SCOs 'Nervous system disorders' and 'Infections and infestations » were reported at similar overall frequencies but were slightly more common in the placebo groups than in the paltusotine treated participants.

Cardiac disorders were observed only in paltusotine group with sinus bradycardia occurring in 3 (3.6%) patients (all in study CRN00808-08) versus 0 in placebo group .

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

Among TEAEs occurring in ≥10% of patients in the paltusotine group, the most frequent were diarrhoea, headache, arthralgia, abdominal pain, and nausea. In the placebo group, commonly reported events included headache, arthralgia, peripheral swelling, diarrhoea, fatigue, upper respiratory tract infection, paraesthesia, and hyperhidrosis. Diarrheal, nausea, abdominal pain and fatigue are listed as ADRs in the product information.

## Overview of Adverse Events in the supportive Group

Table 61 Adverse events in the supportive group

|                                           | Paltusotine (N=233) n (%)   |
|-------------------------------------------|-----------------------------|
| Participants With                         |                             |
| Treatment-Emergent Adverse Events (TEAE)  | 214 (91.8)                  |

In the Supportive Group overall, 214 of 233 participants (91.8%) receiving paltusotine reported at least 1 TEAE. Twenty-nine (12.4%) participants experienced at least 1 SAE, including 6 participants (2.6%) with a treatment-related SAE. A single TEAE leading to death was reported (toxicity to various agents, study CRN00808-09, as noted in the previous paragraph). Thirty-one (13.3%) participants experienced at least 1 severe TEAE. Approximately half (48.1%) of participants had at least 1 TEAE that was related to study treatment. Nine (3.9%) participants reported TEAEs that resulted in treatment discontinuation, and 6 (2.6%) participants reported TEAEs that resulted in study discontinuation.

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

The applicant submitted grouped data on TEAEs reported in paltusotine and placebo groups. The most frequently reported related TEAEs by SOC were gastrointestinal disorders, followed by metabolism and nutrition disorders, cardiac disorders, musculoskeletal and connective tissue disorders, nervous system disorders, hepatobiliary disorders, and investigations.

Within the gastrointestinal SOC, the most common related TEAEs PT were diarrhoea (n=20, 23.8%), abdominal pain (n=9, 10.7%), and nausea (n=7, 8.3%) . These events are well-known ADRs to SRLs therapies.

Other frequently reported related TEAEs included decreased appetite (3.6%), sinus bradycardia (3.6%), headache (2.4%), muscle spasms (2.4%), cholelithiasis (2.4%), and fatigue (2.4 %).

The applicant has provided a justification for inclusion of the adverse events into the PI, which was focused on all TEAEs, irrespective of causality. However, in the end, the adverse events which have been included  in  section  4.8  of  the  paltusotine  SmPC  were  based  on  them  having  at  least  a  possible relationship to the treatment received. This also considered that the SRLs class effects remain relevant to paltusotine safety characterisation.

The applicant conducted a dose-based analysis of TEAEs in the primary group. A higher frequency of TEAEs was observed in patients treated with 40 mg compared to 60 mg, for both overall and treatmentrelated events. In the non-pharmacologically treated population, gastrointestinal disorders were slightly more common at 40 mg (56.3%) than 60 mg (52.6%). Overall, the findings indicate that both doses are similarly tolerated, and the observed variations are not clinically meaningful.

## Adverse Events in Medically Naïve, Previously Treated, and Washout Groups of RC Phase of Study CRN00808-08

The  applicant  also  submitted  a  subgroup  analysis  of  TEAEs  reported  in  medically  naïve,  previously treated,  and  washout  groups  in  the  RC  phase  of  study  CRN00808-08.  TEAEs  were  more  frequently reported in  medically  naïve  patients  (95.5%)  compared  to  previously  treated  (88.9%)  and  washout patients (85.7%).

GI-related AEs such as diarrhoea, abdominal discomfort, and nausea were more frequent in medically naïve patients. This aligns with the known observation that GI AEs are more common during the initial weeks of SRL treatment. Other AEs more common in medically naïve patients included increased lipase, cholelithiasis, eye disorders, metabolism and nutrition disorders, sinus bradycardia, and alopecia.

These differences may reflect a lack of physiological adaptation in SRL-naïve patients. Rapid hormonal suppression (e.g., GH/IGF-1) could lead to systemic effects such as bradycardia or alopecia, while abrupt changes in biliary motility may predispose to cholelithiasis.

TEAEs reported in the first 14 days were consistent with those reported throughout the RC phase. The most  frequent  SOCs  included  gastrointestinal,  metabolism  and  nutrition,  nervous  system,  and musculoskeletal disorders.

Note: Total duration of exposure was 373.0 person-years.

In the Supportive Group overall, a total of 112 of 233 (48.1%) participants experienced treatmentrelated TEAEs. Treatmentrelated TEAEs reported in ≥5% of participants were diarrhoea (n=42; 18.0%), abdominal pain (n=16; 6.9%), headache (n=13; 5.6%), and cholelithiasis (n=12, 5.2%). None of the frequently reported treatment-related TEAEs increased by &gt;1%. The overall safety profile of paltusotine appears consistent with that of other SRLs.

## 5.4.4. Adverse events of special interest, serious adverse events and deaths, other significant events

## Deaths

Two SAE of deaths were reported.

One death was reported in paltusotine-treated participants with acromegaly in the OLE phase of study CRN00808-09. The death was due to an SAE (PT term) of 'toxicity to various agents' and was not considered treatment-related by the Investigator.

The second case was reported in phase 1 study CRN00808-22 (open-label, randomized crossover study in healthy volunteers).

## Serious Adverse Events

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

## Serious Adverse Events in the Primary Group

During  the  RC  phases  of  either  phase  3  study,  no  treatment-emergent  SAEs  were  reported  in  the paltusotine group. In the placebo group, a total of 2 participants (one in each study) each experienced 1 treatment-emergent SAE: parathyroid tumour benign in study CRN00808-08 and cholecystitis in study CRN00808-09.  Both  participants  completed  the  RC  Phase  and  enrolled  in  the  OLE  phase  of  each respective study.

## Serious Adverse Events in the Supportive Group

Overall, across all acromegaly studies, a total of 29 of 233 participants (12.4%) treated with paltusotine experienced a total of 36 treatment-emergent SAEs:

- 5 participants in CRN00808-08 (OLE only) experienced a total of 14SAEs.
- 3 participants in CRN00808-09 (OLE only) experienced 8 SAE each.
- 1 participant in CRN00808-03 reported a single SAE.
- 11 participants in CRN00808-05 reported total of 13 SAEs.

No participants reported SAEs in study CRN00808-02.

SAEs reported in 2 or more participants each were cholelithiasis (n=4), deep vein thrombosis (n=2), pneumonia (n=2), and sinus arrest and urinary tract infection (n=2 each). All other SAEs were reported in 1 participant each.

Most participants treated with paltusotine experienced treatment-emergent SAEs that were considered unrelated (not related or unlikely related) to study treatment. Treatment-related and/or severe SAEs are summarized as follows; all these events resolved as of the respective study cut-off dates:

- Six participants experienced treatment-related (possibly or probably related) SAEs. Three participants experienced 1 SAE of cholelithiasis each (two mild and 1 moderate, all with no change in dose), 1 participant experienced sinus arrest (severe) and biliary colic (moderate) which resulted in study treatment interruption; 1 participant for whom study treatment was already interrupted prior to the onset of the SAE experienced bile duct stone (moderate), and 1 participant experienced an SAE of gastritis (severe). All participants subsequently continued paltusotine.
- Eight participants  experienced  severe  SAEs:  1  participant  experienced the  fatal  event of toxicity to various agents, 1 participant experienced urinary tract infection (during a period where study treatment was already interrupted due to a non-serious TEAE) and subsequently umbilical hernia, 2 participants experienced sinus arrest, 1 participant experienced influenza and pneumonia bacterial, and headache, gastritis, and atrioventricular block complete were experienced  by  1  participant  each,  respectively.  One  severe  TEAE  (sinus  arrest)  was considered  treatment-related.  SAEs  of  sinus  arrest  (n=1),  atrioventricular  block,  and gastritis  resulted  in  study  interruption.  Most  participants  continued  paltusotine;  the participant with the SAE of headache discontinued study drug due to a concurrent TEAE of rhinosinusitis that resulted in a need for a prohibited concomitant medication (ciprofloxacin).

Four of 29 SAEs in participants treated with paltusotine were considered acromegaly-related TEAEs by the investigator: pituitary tumour benign and pituitary tumour in study CRN00808-08 (both moderate and  considered  unlikely  related  to  study  treatment),  arthralgia  in  study  CRN00808-05  (moderate, considered not related to study treatment), and headache in study CRN00808-03 (severe, considered unlikely related to study treatment).

Most treatment-emergent SAEs in participants receiving paltusotine resolved by each respective study cut-off.  In  study  CRN00808-08,  the  status  of  the  abovementioned  SAEs  of  pituitary  tumour  was considered unknown, and the SAE of pituitary tumour benign was considered recovering/resolving. In study  CRN00808-05,  the  SAE  of  breast  cancer  was  considered  recovering/resolving,  and  renal oncocytoma was not considered recovered/resolved.

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

## 5.4.5. Discontinuation due to adverse events

## Primary Group

In the Primary Group, TEAEs leading to treatment discontinuation were reported less frequently in the paltusotine  group  (6.0%)  compared  with  the  placebo  group  (37.6%).  TEAEs  that  led  to  treatment discontinuation  in  2  or  more  participants  in  the  paltusotine  group  were  arthralgia  (3.6%)  and paraesthesia (2.4%). TEAEs that led to treatment discontinuation in 2 or more participants in the placebo group  were  arthralgia  (15.3%),  headache  (14.1%),  peripheral  swelling  (11.8%),  fatigue  (9.4%), hyperhidrosis  (7.1%),  paraesthesia  (5.9%),  muscular  weakness  (4.7%)  and  pain  in  extremity  and insomnia (3.5% each).

## Supportive Group

In the Supportive Group overall, TEAEs leading to treatment discontinuation were reported for 9 of 233 (3. 9%) participants. TEAEs leading to treatment discontinuation reported for 2 or more participants were arthralgia (1.3%), paraesthesia (0.9%), and lipase increased (0.9%).

Across  these  cases,  there  was  a  consistent  potential  signal  of  musculoskeletal  and  neuropathic  AEs leading to discontinuation (neuralgia, paraesthesia, pain in extremity, oral paraesthesia, and muscle spasms, etc.), which may overlap with acromegaly but cannot be definitively dissociated from paltusotine treatment.

Still, it was considered that treatment-related discontinuations (fatigue and decreased appetite) should be included in the SmPC to better describe the safety profile of paltusotine.

## 5.4.6. Safety in special populations

## -Age (Primary Group)

A total of 143 participants (67 in the paltusotine group and 76 in the placebo group) were &lt;65 years of age, and 26 participants (17 in the paltusotine group and 9 in the placebo group, respectively) were ≥ 65 years of age.

In general, while the small number of participants in the ≥ 65 years of age group, particularly at the study level, precludes a thorough analysis, the TEAE profiles appeared largely similar, and no safety concerns were identified based on these analyses.

Based  on  population  PK  analyses  of  intrinsic  factors  on  the  PK  of  paltusotine,  age  did  not  have  a meaningful effect on the exposure of paltusotine assessment.

## -Sex (Primary Group)

In study CRN00808-09, no notable sex-related differences in the incidence of TEAEs were observed. In contrast, study CRN00808-08 showed a higher incidence of TEAEs in women compared to men across both the paltusotine and placebo groups, with moderate TEAEs more frequently reported by women treated with paltusotine (38.5%) than by men (17.9%). Additionally, &gt;20% differences in TEAE incidence between sexes were noted in the SOCs of 'gastrointestinal disorders' and 'metabolism and nutrition disorders'. In the pooled Primary Group, the overall incidence of TEAEs remained higher in women than in  men,  though  no  consistent  &gt;20%  differences  were  observed  by  SOC.  In  study  CRN00808-08 gastrointestinal  disorders  are  more  common  in  paltusotine  females  (69.12%)  compared  to  males (39.3%) subgroup. However, a slightly higher incidence can be observed also in placebo arm (36.4% vs 29.2%,  respectively).  Conversely,  there  is  no  imbalance  in  study  CRN00808-09  (46.7%  vs  46.7%, respectively). A similar trend is observed in study 08 in the SOC of Metabolism and nutrition disorders, where a higher incidence is reported in females compared to males both in paltusotine (42.3% vs 14.3%) and placebo (15.2% vs 4.2%) groups. This type of imbalance is not observed in study 09, where the incidence is slightly higher in males (20.2%) compared to females (13.3%).

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

Therefore, considering the current data it is not possible to draw firm conclusion about a real sex related differences in paltusotine safety profile and it cannot be excluded that these may be due to a random effect.

## -Participants with Hepatic Impairment

Hepatic impairment was investigated in the phase 1 study CRN00808-10, in participants with mild (Child Pugh score of 5-6), moderate (score of 7-9), or severe hepatic impairment (score of 10-15), treated with a single dose of 20 mg paltusotine. 36 participants were enrolled in the study (8 each with mild and moderate impairment, and 6 with severe) and 14 matched control participants. Single oral administration of 20 mg paltusotine tablet was generally well tolerated in all groups. One SAE of death (not treatment related) occurred in participant with mild hepatic impairment who died of natural causes on Day 27. Peak and total systemic exposures of paltusotine were similar across all hepatic impairment groups when compared to participants with normal hepatic function. There were no changes in paltusotine plasma exposure that would be considered clinically meaningful or sufficient to warrant dose adjustment for mild, moderate, or severe hepatic impairment.

## -Use in pregnancy and lactation

Regarding the use of paltusotine in pregnant woman, the effects of the compound remain unknown, which  is  expected  for  a  newly  developed  molecule  given  the  lack  of  data  in  humans.  The  available information  and  knowledge  are  derived  from  non-clinical  developmental  and  reproductive  studies. Clinically, only two pregnancies were documented during the clinical development of paltusotine. The small number of exposed pregnancies and the limited follow-up restrict the ability to rule out potential risks.

From a non-clinical point of view, no treatment-related malformations or embryofoetal lethality were observed  in  rats  or  rabbits  in  embryofoetal  development  studies.  Moreover,  although  specific development  studies  in  rats  demonstrated  decreased  pup  body  weight  at  the  highest  dose  (500 mg/kg/day, the effects were minimal and inconsistent, and thus not considered adverse at human dose exposure (75 mg/kg/day).

Thus, regarding the pregnancy exposure aspects, non-clinical data suggest that paltusotine does not induce teratogenicity and has no significant adverse impact on foetal development at clinically relevant exposures. Observed effects on embryonic development only occurred at very high doses and were considered as minimal and inconsistent.

In contrast, the data related to fertility seems to be more problematic. Although observed at high doses, the adverse effects at ovarian/uterine examination in females of the fertility study may be related to the pharmacological  activity  of  paltusotine  considering  the  data  obtained  with  somatostatin  analogues. Therefore, section 4.6 of the SmPC states that changes in reproductive parameters were reported in female rats.

Regarding breast-feeding, additional specific studies confirmed that paltusotine is secreted in maternal milk. In lactation studies, drug concentrations were higher in milk than in plasma (by 2.4 to 3.8 times), and presence in pup plasma indicated exposure both in utero and via lactation. Foetal and neonatal drug levels  were  low  relative  to  maternal  exposure,  and  no  drug-related  toxicological  effects  were demonstrated at therapeutic dose equivalents.

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

## 5.4.7. Immunological events

Not applicable.

## 5.4.8. Safety related to drug-drug interactions and other interactions

No dedicated  PD  DDI  studies  were  conducted.  The  effect  of  paltusotine  on  the  glucose  metabolism remains unclear. Therefore, paltusotine potential PD interactions with glucose metabolism that may lead to DDI with some diabetes treatment was deemed unclear. However, in light of the observed imbalance in  glucose  dysregulation,  cases  of  drug-drug  interaction  between  paltusotine  and  concomitant antidiabetic agents were discussed, as reported in clinical trials.

Hyperglycaemia events were reported in both the primary and supportive groups of clinical trials. In the primary group, 6 of 85 participants (7.1%) receiving paltusotine experienced hyperglycaemia, compared with 3.5% in the placebo group. In the supportive group, an additional 12 participants (~7%) reported at least one event, with the cumulative incidence increasing from 7.3% (M2.7.4) to 10.3% (4MSU data cutoff). Events continued to be reported beyond 18 months of exposure. These findings indicate that paltusotine can affect glucose regulation, even in the absence of changes in antidiabetic therapy during the trials.

From a pharmacological perspective, paltusotine inhibits growth hormone, glucagon, and insulin, which may impact glucose homeostasis. Although paltusotine is highly selective for SST2, this mechanism can indirectly  affect  glycaemic  control  and  may  necessitate  adjustment  of  concomitant  antidiabetic medications in clinical practice.

In line with other SRL therapies, SmPC section 4.5 mentions that dose adjustments of insulin and antidiabetic medicinal products may be required when paltusotine is administered concomitantly

## 5.4.9. Vital signs and laboratory findings

## -Haematology

No concerns pertaining to haematology were identified from phase 2 and phase 3 analyses in acromegaly participants or in phase 1 studies in healthy volunteers.  In the RC phases of study CRN00808-08 and study CRN00808-09, when evaluated by study, changes from baseline in haematology parameters were generally small and comparable between treatment groups.

## -Chemistry

In  general,  no  safety  signals  were  apparent  from  analysis  of  serum  chemistry  parameters  across acromegaly studies. There were no signals to indicate drug-induced liver injury. In the RC phases of study CRN00808-08 and study CRN00808-09, changes from baseline in serum chemistry parameters were generally small and comparable between treatment groups.

## -Liver Function Tests

No toxicologically significant alterations in serum chemistry parameters, including ALT, AST, and ALP, were observed in non-clinical studies in rat and dog. Overall, no participants treated with paltusotine in the Primary Group had liver test abnormalities indicative of drug-induced liver injury. There was 1 case of elevated ALT &gt; 3×ULN in the placebo group.

Five participants treated with paltusotine in the Supportive had 1 abnormal liver function parameter that was &gt;3×ULN. Two of the 5 participants had 1 elevated liver function test (LFT) parameter at baseline. None of the 5 cases were indicative of DILI associated with paltusotine.

However, the occurrence of ALT/AST elevations at higher doses, in study CRN00808-11 [16.7% (n=3) of participants receiving 80 mg paltusotine experienced mild elevations in liver enzymes (ALT and/or AST)], which may suggest a dose-dependent effect. Moreover, transaminase elevations are a known and relatively frequent class effect of SRL therapies such as octreotide potentially related to the reduced biliary flow. Still, TEAEs of elevated transaminases were infrequent, non-dose-dependent and occurred in equally frequently in participants on placebo. Moreover, no cases of Hy's Law, potential Hy's Law, or drug-induced  liver  injury  (DILI)  were  observed  with  paltusotine  in  the  acromegaly  programme. Regarding  the  occurrence  of  ALT/AST  elevations  at  higher  doses  in  study  CRN00808-11  (carcinoid Syndrome Study) the confounding factors related to the background disease makes difficult any definite extrapolation of the effect of paltusotine on the acromegaly populational.

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

Considering  the  mild  to  moderate  nature  of  the  reported  transaminase  elevations,  the  absence  of treatment discontinuations, and the comparison with product information of other SRL therapies, no further modification of section 4.8 of the SmPC was requested.

However, given the occurrence of ALT/AST elevations &gt;3×ULN and the potential class-related hepatic effects, continued monitoring of liver enzyme abnormalities was considered warranted. The applicant will provide a specific and cumulative analysis of hepatic safety (liver enzymes abnormalities) data in future PSURs.

## -Amylase and Lipase Levels

In general, no notable differences were observed between paltusotine and placebo groups for amylase and lipase. In the medically naïve group, a small, non-important increase in mean and median lipase was observed at Weeks 8 and 12, trending back to baseline at Week 24; this was not seen in other groups.  In general, shifts to worst post-baseline category for both amylase and lipase were similar and infrequent in both paltusotine and placebo groups.

In the paltusotine group, for amylase, 1 of 30 participants with the baseline value of ≤ ULN shifted to &gt;1x to ≤ 2 × ULN compared to 2 of 27 participants in the placebo group.

In the medically naïve group of study CRN00808-08, a small increase in lipase with paltusotine was observed at Weeks 8 and 12, trending back to Baseline at Week 24; this was not clinically important and was not observed in the other groups.

## -Haemoglobin A1c

In the RC phases of study CRN00808-08 and study CRN00808-09, when evaluated by study, changes from baseline in HbA1c parameters were generally small and comparable between treatment groups.

Changes in worst post-baseline HbA1c values were small in both the paltusotine and placebo groups, with  no  clinically  significant  differences  observed  between  the  2  groups  (highest  postbaseline  mean change of 0.28% [SD: 0.370, range: -0.6% to 2.2%] for paltusotine and 0.10% [SD: 0.254, range: 0.5% to 1.0%] for placebo; lowest postbaseline mean change of 0.05% [SD: 0.348, range: -0.8% to 1.5%] for paltusotine and -0.14% [SD: 0.264, range: -1.0% to 0.9%] for placebo).

For study CRN00808-03, there were no clinically significant changes in HbA1c values. In Group 4 (pretrial  therapy  with  pasireotide  monotherapy,  n=4),  there  was  a  decrease  in  the  HbA1c  values  from baseline. The mean change from baseline in HbA1c values in Group 4 was -1.40% (SD 0.361, range 1.80  to  -1.10%).  This  was  consistent  with  withdrawal  from  the  known  hyperglycaemic  effect  of pasireotide.

In study CRN00808-02, no participants experienced HbA1c above the ULN.

In  the  OLE  study  CRN00808-05,  mean  and  median  HbA1c  values  were  consistent  from  Untreated Baseline to Week 207, and median HbA1c was stable at approximately 6.0% from baseline in parent studies (LA-SRL Baseline) to Week 207. Three participants had clinically significant HbA1c elevation at some point during the study (first participant: Screening and Week 1, second participant: Week 23 only, third participant: Weeks 103, 129, and 155).

In the OLE phases of study CRN00808-08 and study CRN00808-09, mean and median changes in HbA1c from  OLE  baseline  to  each  visit  were  small  for  the  total  populations  of  both  studies,  and  by  prior treatment groups in each respective RC Phase (and in the Washout Group of CRN00808-08. Changes in worst postbaseline HbA1c values were small (highest postbaseline mean change of 0.30% [, range: 0.9% to 5.7%]; lowest postbaseline mean of -0.10% [, range: -2.9% to 0.9%]).

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

## -ECG Measurements, QTcF Analysis, Effect on Heart Rate, and Effect on PR Interval

No safety signal of QT prolongation was identified in either the primary or supportive groups. QTcF interval  increases  &gt;450  msec  were  observed  in  similar  proportions  across  paltusotine  and  placebo groups, with no values &gt;500 msec and no QTcF changes &gt;60 msec. However, the number of patients with a change from baseline &gt; 30 msec was higher in paltusotine group (6% vs 2.4%). In the 4MSU cutoff dates, a cumulative total of 26 of 233 (11.2%) participants had worst post-baseline QTcF intervals of &gt;450 msec.

Therefore, while the thresholds of 60 msec increase and above 500-480-450 shift from baseline were similar between groups, there remained an observation of 30 msec increase from baseline in QT that was  more  frequent  in  paltusotine  group  tan  placebo. The  Applicant  provided  results  from  Study CRN00808-14, which was designed to specifically assess sufficient multiples (&gt;2-fold) of the high clinical exposure scenario, to support a concentration-QTc analysis). It is noted that the exposure reached in the tested experimental condition goes up to 1341 ng/mL, which is &gt;2-fold greater than the high clinical exposure scenario, which is steady state clinical exposure following 60 mg once daily (QD) (the maximum recommended  dose)  with  concomitant  medication  of  a  CYP3A4/P-gp/BCRP  inhibitor  (high  clinical exposure; estimated geometric mean Cmax of 583 ng/mL). It was therefore concluded that the provided evidence  satisfied  the  regulatory  requirements  for  a  valid  exposure-response  analysis  based  on modelling. The concentration-QTc analysis using data from CRN00808-14 predicted a placebo-corrected QTcF change from baseline of 1.4 msec (90% upper CI: 2.76 ms) for 80 mg (Cmax: 500.12 ng/mL); 2.8 msec (90% upper CI: 5.50 msec) for 160 mg (Cmax: 996.28 ng/mL), and 3.8 msec (90% upper CI: 7.41 msec) for 240 mg (Cmax: 1341.28 ng/mL), with all values, including the upper CIs, lying below the 10 msec threshold of regulatory concern.  Based on a totality of evidence approach, the waiver for a TQT was confirmed, and no further concerns were raised with respect to the proarrhythmic potential of paltusotine at the intended posology.

A slightly higher incidence of heart rate reduction (from ≥50 bpm to 40 -&lt;50 bpm) was observed in the paltusotine group compared to placebo in study CRN00808-08, particularly on triplicate ECGs, but this was not reproduced in study CRN00808-09 and did not lead to bradycardia &lt;40 bpm.

Regarding PR interval, more participants in the paltusotine group showed post-baseline shifts to &gt;200 msec. However, this finding is difficult to interpret due to a higher prevalence of prolonged PR interval at baseline in the placebo group, suggesting a potential regression to the mean rather than a drugrelated effect.

## 5.4.10. Post-marketing experience

Not applicable. The medicinal product was recently approved by the FDA on 25 September 2025, however, no post-marketing experience was gathered yet.

## 5.4.11. Overall discussion and conclusions on clinical safety

## 5.4.11.1. Discussion

## 5.4.11.1.1. Overall assessment of available safety data

## Safety data collection

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

The safety data of paltusotine for the treatment and long-term maintenance therapy of acromegaly was pooled into 2 analysis populations: the primary group and the supportive group.

The  primary  group  included  data  from  two  RC  phase  3  trials:  study  CRN00808-08  (treatment  of acromegaly) and study CRN00808-09 (long-term maintenance therapy of acromegaly). In these two studies the to-be marketed formulation (15% drug load SDD tablet) was used at doses ranging from 20 mg to 60 mg QD in study CRN00808-08 and 40 mg to 60 mg QD in study CRN00808-09. These data were supported by analyses of the RC phases of the individual studies.

The supportive group pooled data from 5 acromegaly studies (completed studies CRN00808-02 and CRN00808-03,  on-going  study  CRN00808-05,  completed  RC  phases  of  studies  CRN00808-08  and CRN00808-09, and ongoing OLE phases of studies CRN00808-08 and CRN00808-09), exposed for a maximum duration of 4 years to support the assessment of long-term safety of paltusotine. The doses ranged  from  10  mg  QD  to  60  mg  QD  and  included  both  the  capsule  and  to-be  marketed  tablet formulation.

In addition, a total of 11 clinical studies in healthy volunteers, one phase 1 clinical trial in participants with hepatic impairment (study CRN00808-10) and one phase 2 study for the treatment of carcinoid syndrome (study CRN00808-11) are used to support the safety of paltusotine.

## Patient exposure

The patient exposure in the clinical trials with the indication of treatment of acromegaly was considered of sufficient size to enable safety assessment considering the rarity of the disease. The length of the follow-up was considered sufficient for acromegaly indication (approximately 4 years).

In the primary group, 84 patients received at least one dose of paltusotine (54 from study CRN0080808 and 30 from study CRN00808-09). The mean duration in study CRN00808-08 RC was about 24 weeks in  both  paltusotine  and  placebo  groups.  While,  in  study  CRN00808-09  RC  the  median  duration  of exposure was 36 weeks in the paltusotine group and 22.1 weeks in the placebo group.

By dose, the median duration of exposure to 20 mg was 14 days for both the paltusotine and placebo groups, to 40 mg was 62 days and 29 days in the paltusotine and placebo groups, respectively, and to 60 mg was 126 days for both the paltusotine and placebo groups. The major exposure was observed for the 60 mg, although fewer participants in the paltusotine group than in the placebo group (54 versus 79 respectively), received 60 mg of study treatment at any point in the study.

In the supportive group, a total of 233 patients received at least one dose of paltusotine. A total of 12.0%, 18.9%, 40.3%, 13.3%, and 15.5% of participants received &gt;0 to ≤ 6 months, &gt;6 months to ≤ 12 months, &gt;12 to ≤ 24 months, &gt;24 to ≤ 36 months, and &gt;36 months of paltusotine treatment, respectively.

The patient exposure in the clinical trials with the indication of treatment of acromegaly is considered of sufficient  size  to  enable  safety  assessment.  The  length  of  the  follow-up  is  considered  sufficient  for acromegaly indication (approximately 4 years).

The majority of patients completed RC phase studies 08 and 09 (97.6% in paltusotine and 95.3% in placebo group). The most common reason of discontinuation is AEs (3.6%) in paltusotine group and use of rescue therapy (35.3%) in placebo group, as expected in this group. Study discontinuation was low and was observed in 6 patients in total. About 92% of patients enrolled in OLE phase.

Demographic characteristics in primary group are overall consistent between groups with the majority being ≤ 65 years, white and with a prior acromegaly treatment.

## Adverse events (AEs)

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

In the primary group, TEAEs were reported at similar rates in the paltusotine and placebo groups (86.9% vs. 90.6%, respectively). Most were mild or moderate. Severe TEAEs were more common in placebo (9.4%) than in paltusotine group (2.4%) and only 2 severe not related TEAEs occurred in placebo group, both in study CRN00808-08 (headache and pain in extremity). No SAEs were reported in the paltusotine group.  In  contrast,  two  SAEs  occurred  in  the  placebo  group  (benign  parathyroid  tumour  and cholecystitis).

The  most  common  TEAEs  (≥10%  incidence)  by  PT  in  paltusotine  group  were diarrhoea,  headache, arthralgia,  abdominal  pain  and  nausea.  However,  among  these  only  AEs  belonging  to  the  SOC  of gastrointestinal  disorders  were  more  common  in  paltusotine  group  compared  to  placebo  group. Moreover,  also  abdominal  pain  (13.1%  versus  5.9%),  abdominal  discomfort  (6.0%  versus  1.2%), decreased appetite, sinus bradycardia (4.8% versus 0 for each) and hyperglycaemia (4.8% versus 2.4%) were more common in study drug group compared to placebo group.

For  medically  naïve  patients,  progressive  pharmacodynamic  adaptation  is  necessary,  therefore  the recommended starting dose is 20 mg once daily, to be then titrated up to 40 mg after tolerance is established (two weeks). This approach is standard for other SRLs and aligns with the principle of using the lowest effective dose to minimise dose-dependent ADRs.

Cardiac disorders were observed only in paltusotine group with sinus bradycardia occurring in 3 (3.6%) patients (all in study CRN00808-08) versus 0 in placebo group .

Musculoskeletal  disorders  were  more  frequently  reported  in  the  previously  SRL-treated  patients (CRN00808-09)  compared  to  non-pharmacologically  treated  patients  (CRN00808-08)  [46.7%  vs. 27.8%].  The  observed  imbalance  between  studies  likely  reflects  differences  in  baseline  disease characteristics (e.g., longer disease duration, older age, higher prevalence of pre-existing musculoskeletal conditions in study CRN00808-09) rather than a treatment or exposure-related safety signal.

In the supportive group, 214 of 233 participants (91.8%) receiving paltusotine reported at least 1 TEAE. Twenty-nine (12.4%) participants experienced at least 1 SAE, including 6 participants (2.6%) with a treatment-related SAE. A single TEAE leading to death was reported (toxicity to various agents, Study CRN00808-09, as noted in the previous paragraph). Thirty-one (13.3%) participants experienced at least 1 severe TEAE. Approximately half (48.1%) of participants had at least 1 TEAE that was related to study treatment. Nine (3.9%) participants reported TEAEs that resulted in treatment discontinuation, and 6 (2.6%) participants reported TEAEs that resulted in study discontinuation.

## Treatment-related TEAEs

In the primary group, treatment-related TEAEs were more frequently observed in the paltusotine group (45.2%) compared to placebo (22.4%).

The  most  frequently reported  related  TEAEs  by  SOC  were  gastrointestinal  disorders,  followed  by metabolism and nutrition disorders, cardiac disorders, musculoskeletal and connective tissue disorders, nervous system disorders, hepatobiliary disorders, and investigations.

Within the SOC gastrointestinal disorders, the most common related TEAEs PT were diarrhoea (n=20, 23.8%), abdominal pain (n=9, 10.7%), and nausea (n=7, 8.3%). These events are well-known ADRs to SRLs therapies and were listed as 'very common' in the product information.

Other frequently reported related TEAEs included decreased appetite (3.6%), sinus bradycardia (3.6%), headache (2.4%), muscle spasms (2.4%), cholelithiasis (2.4%), and fatigue (2.4%). Section 4.8 of the SmPC reflects the observed treatment-related TEAE accordingly.

Both doses of 40mg and 60mg are similarly tolerated, and the observed variations are not clinically meaningful.

TEAEs  were  more  frequently  reported  in  medically  naïve  patients  (95.5%)  compared  to  previously treated (88.9%) and washout patients (85.7%). GI-related AEs such as diarrhoea, abdominal discomfort, and nausea were more frequent in medically naïve patients. This aligns with the known observation that GI AEs are more common during the initial weeks of SRL treatment. Other AEs more common in medically naïve  patients  included  increased  lipase,  cholelithiasis,  eye  disorders,  metabolism  and  nutrition disorders, sinus bradycardia, and alopecia.

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

These differences may reflect a lack of physiological adaptation in SRL-naïve patients. Rapid hormonal suppression (e.g., GH/IGF-1) could lead to systemic effects such as bradycardia or alopecia, while abrupt changes in biliary motility may predispose to cholelithiasis.

In the supportive group overall, a total of 112 of 233 (48.1%) participants experienced treatment-related TEAEs.  Treatmentrelated  TEAEs  reported  in  ≥5%  of  participants  were diarrhoea  (n=42;  18.0%), abdominal pain (n=16; 6.9%), headache (n=13; 5.6%), and cholelithiasis (n=12, 5.2%). None of the frequently reported treatment-related TEAEs increased by &gt;1%. However, the Applicant reports that cholelithiasis was previously below the 5% threshold (3.9%) suggesting an increase of frequency in the long-term.

These ADRs are widely acknowledged in SRL product information. The overall safety profile of paltusotine appears consistent with that of other SRLs.

Although the number of participants at 20 mg (n=4) was limited, a numerically higher proportion of patients experienced related TEAEs (50%) compared to those receiving 40 mg (43%) or 60 mg (32%). Gastrointestinal  adverse  events  appeared  more  frequent  in  the  20  mg  group.  No  dose-dependent increase in overall TEAEs or related TEAEs was observed between 40 and 60 mg, which indicates no apparent dose-related safety concerns at higher exposures.

Despite  limited  number,  the  trend  toward  more  frequent  GI  TEAEs  at  20  mg,  suggests  that  initial exposure may be associated with transient intolerance. Therefore, initiating treatment at 20 mg, rather than 40 mg, has been considered to improve tolerability and minimize early discontinuations.

## Serious adverse events (SAEs) and deaths

No treatment-related deaths were reported with either paltusotine or placebo in any of the 5 acromegaly studies as of each respective study cut-off.

In the RC phase 3 studies (primary group), no SAEs were reported in patients treated with paltusotine. In the supportive group, 29 of 233 patients (12.4%) experienced a total of 36 SAEs.

Treatment-related  SAEs  were  reported  in  six  participants.  Three  participants  experienced  1  SAE  of cholelithiasis each (two mild and 1 moderate, all with no change in dose), 1 participant experienced sinus arrest (severe) and biliary colic (moderate) which resulted in study treatment interruption; 1 participant for whom study treatment was already interrupted prior to the onset of the SAE experienced bile duct stone  (moderate),  and  1  participant  experienced  an  SAE  of  gastritis  (severe).  All  participants subsequently continued paltusotine.

## AESIs (Adverse events of special interest)

AESIs were reported more frequently in the placebo groups than in the paltusotine groups during the RC studies (65.9% vs 36.9%). The most frequently reported AESIs were similar across both treatment arms and consistent with the overall TEAE profile observed in the primary group. The most frequently reported acromegalyrelated  TEAEs (≥10% incidence) in the paltusotine group were headache and arthralgia while in the placebo group were arthralgia, peripheral swelling, headache, fatigue, paraesthesia, and hyperhidrosis.  In  the  Supportive  Group  overall,  in  general,  acromegaly  related  TEAE  profile  was consistent with the Primary Group. Of note, several AESIs such as arthralgia, fatigue and headache are listed as ADRs in the product information of other SRL therapies. In addition, the applicant performed an analysis of safety data reported in paltusotine-treated participants for the following categories of safety  events  known  to  be  associated  with  approved  SRL  medications,  such  as  cholelithiasis  and complications of cholelithiasis, hyperglycemia and hypoglycemia, thyroid function abnormalities, cardiac function abnormalities, and gastrointestinal events. Hyperglycemia has been added as ADR in section 4.8 of the SmPC and a warning has been added in section 4.4 of the SmPC.

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

TEAEs of bradycardia were reported for 5 (6.0%) participants in the paltusotine primary group, of which sinus bradycardia was reported in 4 (4.8%) participants. The events were considered asymptomatic; No clear association with dose has been identified. This information was reflected in the 4.4 and 4.8 sections of the SmPC.

There was an additional event of nonserious sinus bradycardia in the supportive group and an additional 3 nonserious events of bradycardia, as well as an additional event of nonserious sinus bradycardia in the participant who had 2 prior events of sinus bradycardia in the RC phase of study CRN00808-08. These events  were  non-serious,  asymptomatic,  did  not  lead  to  treatment  discontinuation  and  were  not associated with adverse cardiovascular outcomes.

No imbalances were observed in the SRL event categories of narrow or broad gallbladder abnormalities. Overall,  the  majority  of  TEAEs  of  cholelithiasis  occurred  between  6  and  9  months  after  the  start  of paltusotine. Section 4.8 of the SmPC reports that events occurred in the first nine months.

In  addition,  a  warning  advising  on  potential  occurrence  of  cholelithiasis  and  its  complications  and appropriate evaluation was included in section 4.4 of the SmPC.

A small number of cases of elevated lipase or elevated amylase were reported between paltusotine and placebo groups, in both the primary and the supportive group.

## Ocular TEAEs

In non-clinical phototoxicity studies, unilateral corneal dystrophy and retinal degeneration/necrosis were observed in paltusotine-treated rats at the high dose 250 mg/kg/day. It was concluded that the link between paltusotine administration and the adverse effect observed in retina in phototoxicity study after 3-day administration is questionable (please refer to the non-clinical part section).

In  phase  3  clinical  trials,  a  review  of  ocular-related  TEAEs  revealed  a  similar  incidence  between paltusotine and placebo groups (~8%). However, a numerical imbalance in event count was noted: 30 events reported in 22 paltusotine-treated participants vs. 16 events in 9 placebo-treated participants.

Overall, despite the absence of serious adverse events and comparable incidence rates with placebo, the combination of non-clinical findings, an event imbalance, insufficient detail on clinical cases, and ongoing adjudication justifies further investigation. Continued ophthalmic monitoring is appropriate, and additional data are needed to better assess the ocular safety profile of paltusotine.

Therefore, this topic  will  remain under  close  monitoring in  future  PSURs,  with  continued  cumulative review of ocular adverse events to confirm the absence of a safety signal over time. In addition, the open-label extension study CRN00808-05 will also collect safety information on ocular adverse events.

## Discontinuation due to adverse events

In the primary group, the incidence of TEAEs leading to study treatment discontinuation was lower in the  paltusotine  group  (6.0%)  compared  with  placebo  (37.6%),  with  most  placebo  discontinuations attributed to acromegaly symptoms. Similar trends were observed for dose reductions or interruptions. In the Supportive Group overall, TEAEs leading to study discontinuation were reported for 9 of 233 (3.9 %) participants, 5 from the Primary Group, and 3 from the OLE Phase 2/3 studies. TEAEs leading to treatment  discontinuation  reported  for  2  or  more  participants  were  arthralgia  (1.3%),  paresthesia

(0.9%), and lipase increased (0/9%)

Across these cases, there is a consistent potential signal of musculoskeletal and neuropathic AEs leading to discontinuation (neuralgia, paraesthesia, pain in extremity, oral paraesthesia, and muscle spasms, etc.),  which  may  overlap  with  acromegaly  but  cannot  be  definitively  dissociated  from  paltusotine treatment.

Treatment-related  discontinuations  (fatigue  and  decreased  appetite)  were  included  in  the  SmPC,  to better describe the safety profile of paltusotine.

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

## Safety in special populations

## Age

Overall, there seem to be no significant differences in TEAEs between participants in the ≥ 65 and &lt; 65 years of age group . A slightly higher incidence in musculoskeletal and connective tissue disorders was observed in older patients (41% vs 32.8%), as expected. However, the small number of patients ≥65 years does not allow to draw a firm conclusion.

## Sex

A higher incidence of TEAEs was reported in women compared to men. In the Primary Group, a total of 92.7% (38/41) women in the paltusotine group versus 81.4% (35/43) men in the paltusotine group reported at least 1 TEAE. Considering the current data, it is not possible to draw conclusion about a real sex related difference in paltusotine safety profile.

## Safety related to drug-drug interactions and other interactions

No dedicated DDI studies was conducted. That was accepted.

## Use in pregnancy and lactation

Current knowledge on the effects of paltusotine during pregnancy and lactation is limited.

Non-clinical data suggest that paltusotine does not induce teratogenicity and has no significant adverse impact on foetal development at clinically relevant exposures.

In contrast, the observed adverse effects at ovarian/uterine examination in females of the fertility study may  be  related  to  the  pharmacological  activity  of  paltusotine  considering  the  data  obtained  with somatostatin analogues. Section 4.6 of the SmPC 4.6 on fertility mentions that changes in reproductive parameters were reported in female rats.

Additional specific studies confirmed that paltusotine is secreted in maternal milk. In lactation studies, drug concentrations were higher in milk than in plasma (by 2.4 to 3.8 times), and presence in pup plasma indicated exposure both in utero and via lactation. Foetal and neonatal drug levels were low relative to maternal exposure, and no drug-related toxicological effects were demonstrated at therapeutic dose equivalents.

## Laboratory and other findings

No toxicologically significant alterations in serum chemistry parameters, including ALT, AST, and ALP, were observed in non-clinical studies in rat and dog. Overall, no participants treated with paltusotine in the Primary Group had liver test abnormalities indicative of drug-induced liver injury. There was 1 case of elevated ALT &gt; 3×ULN in the placebo group.

However, the occurrence of ALT/AST elevations at higher doses, in study CRN00808-11 [16.7% (n=3) of participants receiving 80 mg paltusotine experienced mild elevations in liver enzymes (ALT and/or AST)], which may suggest a dose-dependent effect. Moreover, transaminase elevations are a known and relatively frequent class effect of SRL therapies such as octreotide potentially related to the reduced biliary flow.

The Applicant clarified that in acromegaly program TEAEs of elevated transaminases were infrequent, non-dose-dependent and also occurred in equally frequently in participants on placebo. Moreover, no cases of Hy's Law, potential Hy's Law, or drug-induced liver injury (DILI) were observed with paltusotine in the acromegaly programme. Regarding the occurrence of ALT/AST elevations at higher doses in Study CRN00808-11 (carcinoid Syndrome Study) the confounding factors related to the background disease makes difficult any definite extrapolation of the effect of paltusotine on the acromegaly populational.

However, given the occurrence of ALT/AST elevations &gt;3×ULN and the potential class-related hepatic effects, continued monitoring of liver enzyme abnormalities was considered warranted. The applicant has agreed to provide a specific and cumulative analysis of hepatic safety (liver enzymes abnormalities) data in future PSURs.

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

No safety signal of QT prolongation was identified in either the primary or supportive groups. QTcF interval  increases  &gt;450  msec  were  observed  in  similar  proportions  across  paltusotine  and  placebo groups, with no values &gt;500 msec and no QTcF changes &gt;60 msec. However, the number of patients with a change from baseline &gt; 30 msec was higher in paltusotine group (6% vs 2.4%). In the 4MSU cutoff dates, a cumulative total of 26 of 233 (11.2%) participants had worst post-baseline QTcF intervals of &gt;450 msec.

Therefore, while the thresholds of 60 msec increase and above 500-480-450 shift from baseline were similar between groups, there remains an observation of 30 msec increase from baseline in QT that is more frequent in paltusotine group than placebo.

The  Applicant  provided  results  from  study  CRN00808-14,  which  was  designed  to  specifically  assess sufficient  multiples  (&gt;2-fold)  of  the  high  clinical  exposure  scenario,  to  support  a  concentration-QTc analysis). The provided evidence satisfies the regulatory requirements for a valid exposure-response analysis based on modelling. The concentration-QTc analysis using data from CRN00808-14 predicted a placebo-corrected QTcF change from baseline of 1.4 msec (90% upper CI: 2.76 ms) for 80 mg (Cmax: 500.12 ng/mL); 2.8 msec (90% upper CI: 5.50 msec) for 160 mg (Cmax: 996.28 ng/mL), and 3.8 msec (90% upper CI: 7.41 msec) for 240 mg (Cmax: 1341.28 ng/mL), with all values, including the upper CIs, lying below the 10 msec threshold of regulatory concern.  Based on a totality of evidence approach, the waiver for a TQT was confirmed, and no further concerns with respect to the proarrhythmic potential of paltusotine at the intended posology were raised.

Information on cardiac electrophysiology was included in section 5.1 of the SmPC as it is in line with the results  of  the  exposure-QTc  analysis  and  similar  to  other  recently  approved  products,  where  QT information is presented as descriptive pharmacodynamic data rather than a warning or precaution.

A slightly higher incidence of heart rate reduction (from ≥50 bpm to 40 -&lt;50 bpm) was observed in the paltusotine group compared to placebo in study CRN00808-08, particularly on triplicate ECGs, but this was not reproduced in study CRN00808-09 and did not lead to bradycardia &lt;40 bpm.

In addition, most PR interval shifts were small (≤220 msec) and the absence of higher -grade AV block, temporal relationship, or dose dependency, together with the presence of confounding cardiovascular comorbidities, argue against a direct drug effect. The phase 1 data and the lack of mechanistic plausibility further support this conclusion.

However, as minor PR interval prolongation and isolated cases of AV block were observed in treated patients (including elderly participants with cardiovascular risk factors) this potential adverse event was acknowledged in section 4.4 of the SmPC.

## Conclusion

The safety profile of paltusotine was well characterized and in line with other SRL treatments. The most common TEAEs (≥10% incidence) by PT were diarrhea, headache, abdominal pain and nausea. However, bradycardia and gallbladder abnormalities are also reported and addressed in the SmPC.

The applicant presented the section 4.8 of the SmPC based on phase 3 clinical trials data.

## 5.4.11.2. Conclusions on clinical safety

The safety profile of paltusotine for the treatment of acromegaly, based on pooled data from five clinical studies and supported by both primary and supportive analysis populations, is consistent with other SRL therapies. The safety population size and the median follow-up duration of approximately four years are considered acceptable for a rare disease such as acromegaly. Most TEAEs were mild or moderate in severity, and their frequency was comparable between paltusotine and placebo groups. The most reported ADRs were gastrointestinal disorders (e.g., diarrhoea, abdominal pain, nausea), sinus bradycardia, and cholelithiasis, predominantly occurring during the first three months of treatment and rarely leading to discontinuation.

In  addition,  other  ADRs  such  as  decreased  appetite,  headache,  fatigue,  abdominal  distension  and hyperglycaemia frequently observed during clinical trials and listed in the product information of other SRLs, were also reported with paltusotine and are now reflected in the SmPC. Ocular adverse events have been reported in clinical trials, some of which were assessed as related to paltusotine. All ocular TEAEs were nonserious, mostly mild or moderate, and did not lead to treatment discontinuation. Events were reported infrequently and without consistent temporal or clinical pattern, and potential confounding factors (such as age-related conditions or concomitant medications known to cause ocular symptoms). However,  given  the  biological  plausibility  based  on  non-clinical  observations  and  the  limited  clinical dataset, the possibility of a causal relationship cannot be fully excluded. Therefore, this topic will remain under close monitoring in future PSURs, with continued cumulative review of ocular adverse events to confirm the absence of a safety signal over time. In addition, the long-term safety and tolerability of paltusotine  will  be  evaluated  in  the  OLE  of  the  long-term  extension  study  CRN00808-05  (ACROBAT ADVANCE), which is on-going and a category 3 PASS in the RMP.

## 6. Risk management plan

## 6.1. Safety specification

## 6.1.1. Proposed safety specification

The applicant proposed the following summary of safety concerns in the RMP:

Table 65

Summary of safety concerns in the proposed RMP

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

| Summary of safety concerns   | Summary of safety concerns   |
|------------------------------|------------------------------|
| Important identified risks   | None                         |
| Important potential risks    | None                         |
| Missing information          | Long-term safety             |

## 6.1.2. Discussion on proposed safety specification

The assessment of safety of paltusotine for the medical treatment and long-term maintenance of therapy of acromegaly is based primarily on pooled data from the RC phases of the pivotal phase 3 studies (CRN00808-08 and CRN00808-09), which included 84 participants exposed to paltusotine for up to 36 weeks at doses ranging from 20 mg to 60 mg QD. A pooled analysis of safety data from the 2 phase 3 studies (including data from RC and OLE phases) and 3 phase 2 studies (CRN00808-03, CRN00808-02, and CRN00808-05) was also performed and included 233 participants exposed to paltusotine with a maximum duration of 6 years.

Results of the safety data from these clinical studies demonstrated that paltusotine was generally well tolerated.  There  were  no  unexpected  risks  identified,  and  the  proposed  adverse  drug  reactions  are generally mild to moderate with a low rate of treatment discontinuation and are consistent with those observed with approved SRL therapies. Across the acromegaly studies, the incidence of TEAEs generally did not increase with longer exposure to paltusotine. One notable exception was cholelithiasis, which was reported more frequently with &gt;6 to ≤ 9 months of paltusotine treatment in the Supportive Group. This is consistent with the mechanism that gallbladder sludge and gallstones build up over time leading to symptoms occurring later in treatment which is in line with the effects of approved SRL therapies. Cholelithiasis is an identified risk that is not considered important. No unexpected safety signals were identified with long-term paltusotine treatment.

However, long-term safety data is recognised to be limited for a treatment that is expected to be used as chronic medication and accordingly long-term safety is proposed as missing information. Population in need of further characterisation: the on-going OLE study CRN00808-05 will further characterise longterm safety of paltusotine. The duration of the OLE study is up to 6 years for CRN00808-05.

The applicant did not propose any important identified or potential risks to be addressed in the RMP. This was accepted.

## 6.2.2. Discussion on the pharmacovigilance plan

## 6.2.2.1. Routine pharmacovigilance activities

The proposed routine pharmacovigilance activities are acceptable.

## 6.2.2.2. Additional pharmacovigilance activities

The applicant has proposed adding one on-going open label extension study (CRN00808-05) as category 3 PASS in the RMP, which was accepted.

This is an open label, long-term extension study to evaluate the safety and efficacy of CRN00808 in subjects  with  acromegaly.  In  studies  CRN00808-02  (patients  with  normal  IGF-1  on  injectable  SRL monotherapy  at  baseline  and  responders  to  octreotide  LAR  or  lanreotide  depot)  and  CRN00808-03 (patients  with  suboptimal  control  with  injectable  SRL  octreotide  or  lanreotide  and  with  or  without cabergoline),  participants  were  switched  from  SRL-based  regimens  to  once-daily  paltusotine  for  the duration of the study, with the option of continuing in OLE study CRN00808-05. Participant enrolment has been completed, and the study is on-going.

During the follow up, the incidence of treatment-emergent AEs (TEAEs) throughout the study will be monitored as a primary endpoint, and the additional safety endpoints are: 'Clinical laboratory tests (hematology, serum chemistry, and urinalysis) at each post-baseline study visit', 'Vital signs at each post-baseline  study  visit',  '12-lead  electrocardiogram  (ECG)  at  each  post-baseline  study  visit',  and 'Ophthalmic assessments'.

The safety data from the Phase 2 studies (2+3+5) show that Paltusotine was generally well tolerated, with no unexpected safety signals from these studies.

The applicant commits to discuss the long-term safety data from the three studies in the PSUR or other relevant procedures studies if relevant safety data is identified, which was agreed.

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

## 6.3. Plans for post-authorisation efficacy studies

There are no planned or on-going post-authorisation efficacy studies. This was found to be acceptable.
