## 7.1. Pharmacovigilance system

The CHMP considers that the pharmacovigilance system summary submitted by the applicant fulfils the requirements of Article 8(3) of Directive 2001/83/EC.

## 7.2. Periodic safety update reports (PSURs) submission requirements

The active substance is not included in the EURD list, and a new entry will be required. The new list of Union reference dates (EURD list) entry uses the European birth date (EBD) or the international birth date (IBD) to determine the forthcoming Data Lock Points. The requirements for submission of periodic safety update reports for this medicinal product are set out in the Annex II, Section C of the CHMP Opinion. The applicant did request an alignment of the PSUR cycle with the IBD. The IBD is 25 September 2025. The applicant will use a data lock point (DLP) of 31 August for administrative convenience, to satisfy annual aggregate safety reporting requirements in the United States, EU and other regions.

## 8. Product information

## 8.1. Summary of product characteristics (SmPC)

## 8.1.1. SmPC section 4.1 justification

The approved indication is aligned with the population studied in the pivotal clinical trials.

## 8.1.2. SmPC section 5.1 justification

Information on the pivotal studies supporting the indication is included in the SmPC section 5.1. Besides key findings, some additional secondary endpoints have been included which are considered of clinical relevance to the prescriber.

## 8.2. Labelling

## 8.2.1. User consultation

## 8.2.1.1. Conclusion from the checklist for the review of user consultation

The results of the user consultation with target patient groups on the package leaflet submitted by the applicant show that the package leaflet meets the criteria for readability as set out in the Guideline on the readability of the label and package leaflet of medicinal products for human use.

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

## 8.2.2. Labelling exemptions

A request of translation exemption of the labelling as per Art.63.1 of Directive 2001/83/EC has been submitted by the applicant and has been found acceptable by the QRD Group.

The QRD group agreed on granting the requested translation exemption for the carton and bottle labels of Palsonify, use of two monolingual packaging materials: German only and English only.

However, the QRD group refused the request of the translation exemption for the package leaflet. Therefore, a printed package leaflet should be provided with the medicinal product in every national language. In the case of Norway, Denmark and Poland, a digital version of the package leaflet in their national languages available for patients is acceptable (no need of a printed version for these three countries).

The labelling subject to translation exemption as per the QRD Group decision above will, however, be translated in all languages in the Annexes published with the EPAR on the EMA website, but the printed materials will only be translated in the language(s) as agreed by the QRD Group.

## 8.3. Additional monitoring

Pursuant to Article 23(1) of Regulation No (EU) 726/2004, Palsonify (paltusotine) is included in the additional monitoring list since it contains a new active substance which, on 1 January 2011, was not contained in any medicinal product authorised in the EU.

Therefore, the summary of product characteristics and the package leaflet include a statement that this medicinal product is subject to additional monitoring and will allow quick identification of new safety information. The statement is preceded by an inverted equilateral black triangle

## 9. Benefit-risk assessment

## 9.1. Therapeutic context

## 9.1.1. Disease or condition, therapeutic indication

Acromegaly is a rare, chronic, progressive growth disorder characterised by excessive secretion of growth hormone (GH) and its effector hormone, insulin-like growth factor (IGF)-1. The disorder is usually the result of a GH-producing pituitary adenoma.

Acromegaly most commonly affects middle-aged adults and if left untreated can result in serious illness and  premature  death.  Patients  have  disproportionate  skeletal,  tissue,  and  organ  growth.  Once recognised, acromegaly is treatable in most patients. If left uncontrolled, acromegaly is associated with multisystem morbidities and increased mortality, predominantly from cardiovascular, cerebrovascular, and respiratory disease.

The pathophysiology starts with hyper-production of GH by the pituitary adenoma. GH (also known as somatotropin) is a peptide hormone synthesised, stored and secreted by the somatotroph cells of the pituitary gland in a pulsatile manner throughout the day. GH exerts its effects by binding to the Growth Hormone Receptor (GHR) on target cells. The GHR is a transmembrane glycoprotein that binds GH in its extracellular domain and induces downstream signalling leading to receptor dimerisation and activation of intra- and intercellular signal transduction pathways, which trigger production and subsequent release of its effector insulin-like growth factor-1 (IGF-1) (either paracellularly or into the bloodstream).

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

GHR is expressed in many tissues, the most abundant being the liver (primarily hepatocytes) and adipose tissue. The liver is the major site of IGF-1 production. IGF-1, also known as somatomedin C, has growthstimulating effects on a wide variety of tissues including activity on osteoblasts and chondrocytes to promote bone growth.

The diagnosis of acromegaly is confirmed on the basis of elevated serum insulin-like growth factor 1 (IGF-1) and lack of growth hormone suppression after glucose administration.

According to the last consensus conference ( Giustina et al 2024 ), the diagnosis is based on IGF-1 levels &gt;1.3 times the upper limit of normal for age and characteristic clinical signs of disease. The therapeutic efficacy target is IGF-1 normalized for age.

Of note, in the 13 th  consensus on the multidisciplinary management of acromegaly ( Giustina et al 2020 ), although the absolute cut-off for defining biochemical control is the upper limit of normal (ULN) (SR), values slightly higher than this cut-off (e.g., within 1.2- 1.3 × ULN) could be considered as a target of treatment depending on the clinical scenario.

The initially  claimed  indication  was  'medical  treatment  and long-term  maintenance therapy  of  adult patients with acromegaly'.

## 9.1.2. Available therapies and unmet medical need

For a detailed description, please see section 2.1 of this document.

According to the Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline (co-sponsored by the Endocrine Society and the European Society of Endocrinology), the clinical aims in the management of acromegaly include the following: (1) to control biochemical indices of activity (principally GH and IGF-I), (2) to control tumour size and prevent local mass effects, (3) to reduce the signs and symptoms of disease, (4) to prevent or improve comorbidities, and (5) to prevent early mortality.

The  optimal  treatment  should  provide  robust  efficacy  in  controlling  IGF-1  levels  and  minimising acromegaly symptoms from treatment initiation throughout treatment with an acceptable safety profile, with the ultimate objective to improve patient QoL and satisfaction. Unmet needs include optimizing biochemical and symptom control, reducing treatment burden and enhancing QoL. Of note, recurring symptoms and compromised QoL can be also seen in patients that are biochemically controlled.

The existing therapeutic strategy includes transphenoidal surgery (first-line therapy) and/or medical treatment and/or radiotherapy.

Long-acting somatostatin analogues are used as first line pharmacological treatments in patients not cured by surgery/radiotherapy or requiring additional treatment pre- or postoperatively. For patients not cured by surgery or for whom surgery is contra-indicated, somatostatin receptor ligands (SRLs) are established as first-line medical treatment with potential for further improvements and lead to limited disease control with partial response rate of 30-55% and continued symptoms.

Five injectable SRLs are currently approved for medical treatment of acromegaly in the EU and are administered by daily (up to three times daily [t.i.d.]): octreotide (S.C Sandostatin and monthly IM Sandostatin LAR), monthly lanreotide (Somatuline Autogel), monthly IM pasireotide (Signifor LAR) and monthly SC octreotide hydrochloride (Oczyesa).

Treatment guidelines mention that in case of partial response, adjunctive therapies can be considered in association to SRLs such as dopamine agonists (cabergoline, bromocriptine) which are used off-label. In case of no response, pegvisomant can be considered in monotherapy.

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

Despite currently available pharmacological therapies, each of the available acromegaly treatments have safety and/or efficacy limitations that directly impact potential use based on route of administration, treatment-limiting AEs, potential off-target effects due to lack of selective somatostatin receptor binding, an inability to titrate quickly for efficacy or safety, delayed treatment effect, return of breakthrough symptoms at the end of the injection cycle, and/or inadequate biochemical and clinical symptom control.

There remains an unmet need for a targeted therapy that reduces the overall treatment burden and combines rapid, consistent, and durable disease control to treat a wide range of the acromegaly patient population that requires medical treatment.

Paltusotine is an orally administered, reversible-covalent, small molecule agonist of human somatostatin receptor 2 (SST2). Paltusotine tablets are available as immediate-release dosage form containing an amorphous spray-dried dispersion (SDD) of paltusotine in a water-soluble polymer, copovidone. The tablets are supplied in 2 strengths, 20 mg and 30 mg and are coated with a water-soluble film.

Acromegaly is caused by excessive growth hormone (GH) secretion and secondary elevation of insulinlike growth factor-1 (IGF-1). Because GH secretion is pulsatile, elevated serum IGF-1 levels are a useful screening tool for acromegaly. IGF-1 levels are an accepted measure of treatment efficacy in acromegaly as well through its activation of the SST2, paltusotine achieves a reduction of GH and IGF-1 levels, which are  both  elevated  in  subjects  with  acromegaly.  It  also  exhibits  bias  for  G-protein  activation  and subsequent  lowering  of  intracellular  cAMP  levels  compared  to  β -arrestin  activation  and  subsequent receptor internalization and desensitization.

## 9.2. Main clinical studies

For a detailed description of the main clinical studies supporting this application, please refer to the respective section of this document.

The efficacy of paltusotine has been evaluated based on 2 phase 2 dose studies ' CRN00808-03 and CRN00808-02', a long-term extension of these studies 'CRN00808-05 ' and 2 phase 3 studies 'CRN00808-08 and CRN08008-09'.

The pivotal studies are the 2 phase 3 studies CRN00808-08 and CRN00808-09. The 2 phase 2 studies CRN00808-02 and CRN00808-03 are only supportive and only provided limited clinical data as regards the choice of dose for the phase 3 studies. The OLE phases of all these studies are still on-going and will provide clinical data on the long-term use of paltusotine.

The phase 3 study CRN00808-08 'PATHFNDR-2: Paltusotine Acromegaly Therapy Featuring a Non-Invasive Daily Regimen' was a multicentre, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled study where participants with non-pharmacologically treated acromegaly including participants with no prior medical therapy, participants who last received medical therapy at least 4 months prior to screening, and participants who were controlled on medical therapy for at least 3 months but agreed to wash out prior to beginning study treatment, were randomly allocated to receive either paltusotine or placebo.

Disease characteristics  were  generally  consistent  between  the  paltusotine  and  placebo  groups.  Most participants had a screening IGF-1 &gt;1.3×ULN ( 83.8% ).

Baseline mean (SD) IGF-1×ULN levels were 1.985 [0.8086] and 2.207 [1.1049] in the Paltusotine and placebo groups respectively.

Study participants overall had a long-standing disease history, with a median of 72.2 months.

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

The majority of participants ( 89.2% ) had documented prior pituitary surgery; all participants in the medically naïve group had documented prior pituitary surgery.

The starting dose of paltusotine in the RC phase was 20 mg QD (1×20 mg tablet of active paltusotine or matching placebo) for oral self-administration. A dose of 20 mg was selected to minimize potential gastrointestinal side effects in participants washed out from medical therapy or naïve to medical therapy. After confirming tolerability with the participant, the dose was increased to 40 mg QD (2×20 mg tablets of active paltusotine or matching placebo) at the Week 2 visit .

Dose down-titration was allowed at any time during the RC phase of the study if the dose was not tolerated in the judgement of the investigator.

Overall, 114 participants enrolled in the OLE phase of study CRN00808-08. At the data snapshot date of 31 August 2025, twenty-two participants overall (19.3%) discontinued the OLE phase of the study.

The phase 3 study CRN00808-09 (PATHFNDR-1) was a randomized, placebo-controlled, multicentre study designed to evaluate the efficacy and safety of paltusotine in participants with acromegaly who were switched from monotherapy with injected long-acting octreotide or lanreotide. In the Randomized Controlled (RC) Phase, eligible participants were randomized (1:1 ratio) to daily oral administration of paltusotine or placebo when their next dose of octreotide/lanreotide was due to begin, for a Treatment Period of up to 36 weeks .

Participants who completed the RC phase could be eligible to enrol and receive paltusotine in the Openlabel Extension (OLE) (on-going) Phase for up to 200 weeks .

Randomization  was  stratified  by  IGF-1  level (&lt;0.86 × ULN, ≥ 0.86 × ULN) and  prior  treatment (lanreotide or octreotide).

Study  participants  overall  had  long-standing  disease  prior  to  enrolment,  with  a  higher  mean  (SD) duration since acromegaly diagnosis in the paltusotine group compared with the placebo group ( 186.6 [88.3] months versus 120.8 [81.9] months). The majority of participants overall ( 86.2% ) had prior pituitary surgery.

The starting dose (paltusotine or matching placebo) was 40 mg QD for oral self-administration. The dose could be increased from 40 mg to 60 mg QD through Week 24 based on IGF-1 response and tolerability. Participants who required dose up-titration after Week 24 were considered non-responders. Dose levels could be decreased based on tolerability at any time during the study.

Rescue  criteria  were  provided  for  participants  who  required  resumption  of  standard  acromegaly treatment during the RC Phase.

In the OLE phase of study CRN00808-09, of the 58 participants randomized in this study, 53 participants (91.4%) entered the OLE phase.

## 9.3. Favourable effects

In the RC phase of study CRN00808-08 ,

1) The  study  met  its  primary  endpoint  ' proportion  of  participants  who  achieved  biochemical response (IGF1 level ≤ 1.0×ULN) at EOR '. In the paltusotine group, this proportion is (30 of 54 [ 55.6% ]) compared to those in the placebo group (3 of 57 [ 5.3% ]) ( p&lt;0.0001 ).

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

Sensitivity analyses were performed in different stratum of the target population. These results were consistent with the results of the primary endpoint and assess the robustness and consistence of the primary with the primary efficacy endpoint results.

2) Regarding the Key secondary endpoint ' LS means (±SE) change from Baseline to EOT in IGF1×ULN' were -0.819 (±0.0789) for the paltusotine group and 0.087 (±0.0751) for the placebo group, with a treatment difference of -0.906 (95% CI: -1.106, -0.706) ( p&lt;0.0001 ).

Sensitivity Analyses of Key Secondary Endpoint were performed by Stratum and confirmed the robustness and consistence of the results of the key secondary endpoint.

3) Other  secondary  endpoints  favoured  paltusotine  compared  to  placebo. The  sensitivity analyses performed in different stratum of the target population were consistent with the results of the secondary  endpoints  and assess  the  robustness  and  consistence  of  the  efficacy  secondary  endpoint results.

- The 'proportion of participants who achieved biochemical response IGF-1 level &lt;1.3×ULN' was 66.7% (36 of 54) in the paltusotine group compared with 14.0% (8 of 57) in the placebo group ( p&lt;0.0001 ). A Sensitivity Analysis 'Proportion of Participants with IGF-1 &lt;1.3×ULN at EOR' was performed by IRT Stratification Factor and has confirmed the results.

- -The proportion  of  participants  who  achieved  GH  level  &lt;1.0  ng/mL  was 57.4% (31  of  54)  in  the paltusotine group compared with 17.5% (10 of 57) in the placebo group ( p&lt;0.0001 ).

A Sensitivity Analysis 'Proportion of Participants with GH&lt;1.0 ng/mL at Week 22' was performed by Stratification Factor and has confirmed the results.

-The LS means (±SE) change from Baseline to EOT in total ASD score were -2.669 (±1.4221) for the paltusotine  group  and 2.754 (±1.3641)  for  the  placebo  group,  indicating  an  improvement  and stabilization of symptoms for the paltusotine group, and worsening of symptoms for the placebo group. This difference of -5.423 (95% CI: -9.070, -1.776) was statistically significant ( p=0.0039 ).

A  Sensitivity  Analysis  'Change  from  Baseline  to  EOR  in  Total  ASD  Score'  was  performed  by  IRT Stratification Factor and has confirmed the findings.

4) Regarding the main exploratory endpoints, they all favoured paltusotine compared to placebo.

.  The proportion of participants who received rescue therapy was 3.7% (2  of  54) in the paltusotine group and 22.8% (13 of 57) in the placebo group affirming the treatment effect of paltusotine versus placebo.

- . Regarding the exploratory PROs endpoints , results from the Treatment Preference Questionnaire at Week 24 indicated that 77.8% (42 of 54) of participants in the paltusotine group and 63.2% (36 of 57) of participants in the placebo group preferred oral medication over previous injections for acromegaly.

The OLE  phase of  study CRN00808-08 (data  snapshot  date:  31  August  2025) has  provided reassuring results, in line with the findings of the RC part. For example, the proportion of participants who achieved IGF-1 level ≤ 1.0 × ULN ranged from 51.3% to 100% from Week 36 to Week 144 for the overall population (51.0% to 100% for the prior paltusotine group and 37.0% to 100% for the prior placebo group) and ranged from 50.0% to 80.0% for the directly enrolled group from Week 36 to Week 120. For a full description of the OLE results, please check the clinical efficacy section of this report.

In the RC phase of study CRN00808-09 ,

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

## 1) Primary endpoint : ' proportion of patients with IGF1 level ≤ 1.0×ULN at EOR'

The  study  met  statistical  significance  ( p&lt;0.0001 )  based  on  the  proportion  of  participants  in  the paltusotine group (25 of 30 [ 83.3%]) who maintained biochemical response (IGF-1 level ≤ 1.0 × ULN) at EOR compared to those in the placebo group (1 of 28 [ 3.6%]).

## 2) Significant differences in favour of paltusotine were observed for all secondary efficacy endpoints:

- The LS means (±SE) for the change from Baseline to EOT in IGF-1×ULN were 0.042 (±0.093) for the paltusotine group and 0.833 (±0.096) for the placebo group, with a treatment difference of -0.791 (95% CI: -1.057 to -0.525) ( p&lt;0.0001 ).

- The proportion of participants who maintained GH level &lt;1.0 ng/mL , out of those who had GH level &lt;1.0 ng/mL at Baseline at Week 34, was 87.0% (20 of 23) in the paltusotine group compared with 27.8% (5 of 18) in the placebo group ( p=0.0003 ).

- The LS means (±SE) for the change from Baseline to EOT in total ASD score were -0.606 (±1.504) for the paltusotine group and 4.588 (±1.593) for the placebo group, indicating stabilization of symptoms for the paltusotine group and worsening of symptoms for the placebo group, with a treatment difference of -5.164 (95% CI: -9.536 to -0.792) ( p=0.0216 ).

No  single  ASD  item  predominantly  affected  the  overall  total  ASD  score,  indicating  that  there  was improvement  and  stabilization  across  all  acromegaly  symptoms  evaluated  in  the  paltusotine  group compared with the placebo group.

3) Results of pre-specified sensitivity analyses to assess the robustness of the primary and secondary efficacy analyses were consistent with the primary and secondary efficacy endpoint results.

As regards the secondary endpoints,

- -A sensitivity analysis was performed using a multiple imputation approach for missing data for the secondary efficacy endpoint of change from Baseline to EOR in IGF-1×ULN. In this analysis, IGF-1 results for all assessments following the first occurrence of intercurrent events, such as dropouts, taking rescue therapy or prohibited medications, or up titration in dose after Week 36, were evaluated as missing data.

For this analysis, the LS means (±SE) for the change from Baseline to Week 36 in IGF-1×ULN were 0.045 (±0.108) for the paltusotine group and 0.767 (±0.316) for the placebo group, with a treatment difference of -0.722 (95% CI: -1.373 to -0.071; nominal p=0.0301 ).

- -In addition, missing data on IGF-1×ULN were imputed using a multiple imputation procedure that utilized all observed data from both treatment groups to overcome the convergence issue.

For this analysis, the LS means (±SE) for the change from Baseline to Week 36 in IGF-1×ULN were 0.041 (±0.089) for the paltusotine group and 0.783 (±0.120) for the placebo group, with a treatment difference of -0.742 (95% CI: -1.032 to -0.453; nominal p&lt;0.0001 ).

## 4)  Results of multiple exploratory endpoint analyses also favoured paltusotine.

-  The  proportion  of  participants  who  received  rescue  therapy  at  EOR  was 6.7% (2  of  30)  in  the paltusotine  group  and 60.7% (17  of  28)  in  the  placebo  group  (nominal p&lt;0.0001 ),  affirming  the treatment effect of paltusotine versus placebo.

The OLE phase of  study CRN00808-09 ( data snapshot date of 31 August 2025 )  has provided reassuring results, in line with the findings of the RC part. For example, the proportion of participants who achieved IGF1 level ≤1.0×ULN ranged from 74.5% to 93.3% from Week 48 to Week 180 for the overall population (50.0% to 96.0% for the prior paltusotine group and 77.3% to 100% for the prior placebo group). (Note: Only 1 participant reached the timepoints for Week 192 and Week 204 at the time of the data snapshot.). For a full description of the OLE results, please check the clinical efficacy section of this report.

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

## 9.3.1. Uncertainties and limitations about favourable effects

In the pivotal study CRN00808-08 , the efficacy of paltusotine at the maximal dose of 60 mg/day seems limited especially in medically naïve patients. In patients with high IGF-1 levels (&gt;1.7 xULN) (medically naïve, uncontrolled, more severe disease) at baseline, the normalisation of IGF-1 is questionable as the efficacy  of  paltusotine  in  these  patients  was  lower  (about  32%)  compared  to  the  whole  population Therefore, the efficacy of paltusotine in these patients is lower.  This is consistent with the fact that based on an ER analysis, at doses of paltusotine up to 60 mg, the maximal reduction of the IGF-1 levels is 47%.

The applicant provided a plot showing the simulations of post-treatment IGF-1 based on varying levels of baseline IGF-1 for a QD dose of 60 mg and concluded that a dose of 60 mg is expected to provide IGF-1 levels ≤ 1.0xULN across a broad range of baseline IGF-1 levels (&gt;1.7xULN to 4.0xULN). However, such conclusion can only be agreed for IGF-1 levels at baseline &lt; 2.5x ULN and by considering an IMAX effect of 57%. Therefore, the applicant was asked to compute for each Baseline IGF-1(xULN) of 2.0, 2.5, …, 5.0, the proportion of subjects who are expected to achieve Post-treatment IGF-1(xULN) of 0.5&lt;1, 1-&lt;1.5, 1.5-&lt;2, 2-above, and to thoroughly discuss the results.

The results of the simulation clearly demonstrate that the probability of achieving IGF-1 normalisation (≤ 1xULN) or near -normalisation (≤ 1.3xULN) markedly decreases with elevated baseline IGF -1 levels.

For baseline IGF-1 of 2.5xULN, 12.8% are predicted to achieve normalisation and less than 40.1% near-normalisation. For baseline IGF-1 of 3xULN, ~6% are predicted to achieve normalisation and less than 23.2% near-normalisation.

Based  on  observed  (vs  predicted)  values  from  the  40  medically  naïve  patients  enrolled  in  study CRN00808-08 with a mean IGF-1 of 2.2xULN at baseline, 42.5% (vs 45%) achieved a normalisation, less than 25% (vs 22.5%) achieved near-normalisation and 32.5% (vs 32.5%) had IGF-1 levels over &gt; 1.5 xULN. More precisely, a near normalisation of IGF-1 &lt;1.3xULN is achieved in 55% of the 40 naïve  patients.  These  results  highlight  that  the  achievement  of  IGF-1  normalisation  or  near normalisation is dependent on the IGF-1 levels at baseline and that a 60 mg QD dose cannot achieve IGF-1 normalisation (or near-normalisation) across a broad range of baseline IGF-1 values.

This was consistent with the E-R analysis which showed that paltusotine doses of 20 mg, 40 mg, and 60 mg corresponded to IGF-1 level reductions of approximately 37%, 43%, and 47% , respectively.

In study CRN00808-08, the baseline IGF-1 decreased from 0.8 to 1xULN up to 24 weeks. However, in the medically naïve patients with a baseline IGF-1 &gt;2.5xULN, a normalisation or a near normalisation of IGF-1 cannot be achieved.

Thus, uncertainties related to the efficacy of paltusotine in patients with high IGF1 levels (i.e≥ 2.5 ULN) still remain especially for the medically naïve patients given that IGF-1 levels reduction is 47% at the maximal dose of paltusotine 60 mg/day. Some of these patients will need a longer duration of treatment and/or will need adjunctive therapies to achieve the normalization of their IGF-1 levels and some of them will never normalize their IGF-1 levels. This has been reflected in section 4.2 of the SmPC.

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

Although the withdrawal of patients was higher among the medically naïve participants, there was a majority of participants in the OLE Phase of Study CRN00808-08, with approximately 80% of participants overall  and  68%  of  medically  naïve  participants  with  durable  and  sustained  clinical  benefits  of paltusotine.

This was consistent with the results of other SRL treatments of acromegaly indicated in medically naïve patients. Indeed, the rates of discontinuation due to treatment failure in the literature were 17.9% for oral octreotide ( Samson 2020 ).

Another point is the need of adjunctive therapies in the OLE phase of study CRN00808-08 and in study CRN00808-09. Concomitant administration of adjunctive therapies was only possible in the OLE phase of study CRN00808-08 from W48 and from W60 in study CRN00808-09. Thus, it is difficult to interpret the  long-term  results,  and  to  determine  to  what  extent  the  observed  increase  of  the  proportion  of medically naïve patients with IGF1 levels ≤ULN during the OLE phase can be attributed solely to the effect of paltusotine as monotherapy.

At the data snapshot date of 21 May 2025, the use of adjunctive therapies was limited and did not lead to a significant increase of the proportion of patients with IGF-1 levels ≤ ULN nor a significant increase of the proportion of patients with IGF-1 levels &lt; 1.3x ULN. This can show that the addition of adjunctive therapies did not have at this stage an impact on the long-term efficacy results of paltusotine.

## 9.4. Unfavourable effects

The patient exposure in the clinical trials with the indication of treatment of acromegaly is considered of sufficient size to enable safety assessment. The length of the follow-up was considered sufficient for acromegaly indication (approximately 4 years).

In the primary group, 84 patients received at least one dose of paltusotine (54 from study CRN0080808 and 30 from study CRN00808-09). Among them, 3 patients received a dose of 20 mg QD, 29 received a dose of 40 mg QD and 52 received a dose of 60 mg QD which is the final dose. The mean duration in study CRN00808-08 RC was 24 weeks in both paltusotine and placebo groups. While, in study CRN0080809 RC the median duration of exposure was 36 weeks in the paltusotine group and 22.1 weeks in the placebo group.

By dose, the median duration of exposure to 20 mg was 14 days for both the paltusotine and placebo groups, to 40 mg was 62 days and 29 days in the paltusotine and placebo groups, respectively, and to 60 mg was 126 days for both the paltusotine and placebo groups.

In the supportive group, a total of 233 patients received at least one dose of paltusotine. A total of 12.0%, 18.9%, 40.3%, 13.3%, and 15.5% of participants received &gt;0 to ≤ 6 months, &gt;6 months to ≤ 12 months,  &gt;12  to ≤ 24  months,  &gt;24  to ≤ 36  months,  and  &gt;36  months  of  paltusotine  treatment, respectively.- In the primary analysis group, TEAEs were reported at similar rates in the paltusotine and placebo groups (86.9% vs. 90.6%, respectively). The majority of TEAEs were mild or moderate. Severe TEAEs were reported in 2 out of 84 participants (2.4%) in the paltusotine group, both occurring in study CRN00808-08 (headache and pain in extremity), compared to 8 out of 85 participants (9.4%) in the placebo group.

- Treatment-related TEAEs were more frequently observed in the paltusotine group (45.2%) compared to placebo (22.4%).

- No SAEs were reported in the paltusotine group. In contrast, two SAEs occurred in the placebo group (benign parathyroid tumour and cholecystitis).

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

- In the Primary Group, treatment-related TEAEs were reported more frequently in the paltusotine group (38 of 84 participants, 45.2%) compared with the placebo group (19 of 85 participants, 22.4%).

- The most frequently reported TEAEs and related-TEAEs by were:

Gastrointestinal disorders: ADRs of diarrhoea (n=20, 23.8%), abdominal pain (n=9, 10.7%), and nausea (n=7, 8.3%). The GI events were predominately mild in severity, the majority of events of diarrhoea and nausea occurred in the first 14 days. In the Primary Group, there was 1 study drug discontinuation due to nausea in Study CRN00808-08, in the paltusotine group. Furthermore, 1 participant in study CRN00808-08 (previously treated group) had mild TEAEs of diarrhoea and abdominal pain on the 60 mg dose, requiring dose reduction to 40 mg and then to 20 mg. These events are well-known ADRs to SRLs therapies and are listed in the product information.

Bradycardia:  Paltusotine  is  associated  with  a  reduction  in  heart  rate  consistent  with  other  SRL treatments. There was an increased rate of sinus bradycardia/bradycardia TEAEs in the paltusotine group (4 participants with at least 1 TEAE of sinus bradycardia and 1 participant with a TEAE of bradycardia) vs 0 in the placebo group. Related sinus bradycardia/bradycardia were reported at 3.6% (3 cases) and 1.2% (1 case) respectively in the paltusotine group. The TEAEs were all non-serious, and the participants were asymptomatic. Of the 5 events, 4 were of mild severity and 1 was moderate. There were no severe events of bradycardia. In all 5 participants, the first events of bradycardia occurred in the first 3 months of treatment. No participants discontinued paltusotine because of bradycardia. This event is listed as 'common' ADR in the product information.

Cholelithiasis:  in  acromegaly  studies,  cholelithiasis  appears  to  be  causally  related  to  paltusotine treatment, consistent with known effects of other SRLs. This is supported by ultrasound findings, delayed onset of events, no imbalance in risk factors, and a higher incidence of biliary TEAEs in treatment-naïve patients  compared  to  those  previously  treated  in  the  Primary  Group.  Overall,  there  was  a  similar incidence of TEAEs of cholelithiasis between paltusotine (n=2, 2.4%) and placebo (n=3, 3.5%) treatment groups. The two cases in the paltusotine group were related (2.4%). The 2 events on paltusotine both occurred in participants who had received a maximum dose of 60 mg, and who were in the medically naïve group. In the OLE of Phase 3 studies, there were a total of 7 participants with biliary-related TEAEs. Cholelithiasis is listed as 'common' ADR in the product information.

Hyperglycaemia: the incidence of hyperglycaemia was higher in the paltusotine group compared to the placebo group 4.8% (n=4) vs. 2.4% (n=2) respectively. From a pharmacological perspective, paltusotine inhibits growth hormone, glucagon, and insulin, which may impact glucose homeostasis. Therefore, this adverse effect was listed in the product information.

## 9.4.1. Uncertainties and limitations about unfavourable effects

Ocular AEs will be closely monitored in future PSURs, with continued cumulative review of ocular adverse events to confirm the absence of a safety signal over time.

Given the occurrence of ALT/AST elevations &gt;3×ULN and the potential class-related hepatic effects, continued monitoring of liver enzyme abnormalities is considered warranted. The applicant has agreed to provide a specific and cumulative analysis of hepatic safety (liver enzymes abnormalities) data in future PSURs.

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

## 9.6. Benefit-risk assessment and discussion

## 9.6.1. Importance of favourable and unfavourable effects

The efficacy of paltusotine was demonstrated in the 2 pivotal phase 3 studies CRN00808-08 and CRN00808-09 . The results of the primary endpoints, secondary and exploratory endpoints are in favour of paltusotine compared to placebo. Results of pre-specified sensitivity analyses to assess the robustness of the primary and secondary efficacy analyses were consistent with the primary and secondary efficacy endpoint results.

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

However, in study CRN00808-08 , the efficacy of paltusotine was mainly driven by the high effect size of paltusotine in the washout subgroup of patients. Indeed, the proportion of patients with IGF-1 level ≤1.0×ULN was 92.9% (13 of 14) in the paltusotine group versus 13.3% in the placebo group. However, the efficacy of paltusotine in the whole population may be overestimated as these patients were already controlled by other SRLs and thus more susceptible to respond to paltusotine.

Although the effect of paltusotine was overall demonstrated in this study as regards to the primary, secondary  and  exploratory  endpoints,  there  were  some  uncertainties  related  to  the  efficacy  of paltusotine especially in medically naïve patients.

In the 2 phase 2 studies CRN00808-02 and CRN00808-03 , used for phase 3 dose selection, there were no medically naïve patients. The choice of dose for these patients for the phase 3 program is not based on the clinical data of these phase 2 studies.

As regards of the primary endpoint 'proportion of patients with IGF-1 IGF1 level ≤ 1.0×ULN at EOR' in study CRN00808-08 , looking at the sensitivity results within the stratum 1, the effect of paltusotine on the primary endpoint in 'medically naïve' patients was 22.7% (5/22) of responder versus 4.2% (1/24) in the placebo group (p = 0.1509). This highlights a lower IGF-1 response observed in this subgroup compared to previously treated patients (% of responders in the Palsonify group and in the Placebo group respectively: 66.7% (12/18) versus 0% in group 2 'already treated patients', and 92.9% (13/14) versus 13.3% (2/15) in group 3 stratum 2 'washout patients'.

The sensitivity analysis of the primary endpoint did not show a clear difference between paltusotine and placebo within Stratum 1 in medically naïve patients. This finding was considered credible given the biological  and  mechanistic  plausibility  of  a  reduced  treatment  effect  in  this  population.  Since  this important subgroup (medically naïve patients) is included in the proposed indication and given that it was the only subgroup with limited effect (and not statistically significant) the Applicant was asked to substantiate the benefit/risk balance in medically naïve patients to be included in the indication.

The  applicant  adequately  justified  the  lower  efficacy  of  paltusotine  in  the  medically  naïve  patients. Indeed, the proportion of medically naïve participants with IGF-1 ≤ ULN at the end of RC phase was consistent with the literature ( Katznelson 2014, Colao 2016 ). This proportion increased overtime based on the non-controlled longer-term results of the OLE Phase. This suggests that these patients may need a longer duration of treatment to normalize their IGF-1 levels especially participants with high IGF-1 levels at baseline also in line with the literature ( Marques-Pamies 2024 ).

As regards the efficacy of paltusotine for the maintenance of treatment in already well controlled patients with other SRLs investigated in study CRN00808-09 , the efficacy was well demonstrated as regards primary, secondary and exploratory endpoints. Thus, the broad indication proposed by the applicant has been approved for medical treatment of adult patients with acromegaly which includes initiation and maintenance of treatment in adult patients with acromegaly.

The applicant initially recommended a posology of 40 mg/day for initiation for all the patients (medically naïve and non-medically naïve) with an increase to 60 mg/ day after 2 to 4 weeks on Palsonify 40 mg once daily, based on insulin-like growth factor-1 (IGF-1) levels or clinical signs and symptoms. As the starting dose of 40 mg/day has not been investigated in medically naïve patients and given the risk of adverse  events  especially  serious  gastro-intestinal  adverse  events,  the  posology  recommendations were adapted accordingly for the medically naïve patients.

In both phase 3 studies, at the end of the RC phase, most of the patients were treated with paltusotine 60  mg.  This  proportion  was  66.7%  in CRN00808-08 [18  naïve/22,  9  uncontrolled/18  and  9/14 washout]).

Of note, among the medically naive patients, 81.8% patients were treated with paltusotine 60 mg . This is consistent with higher IGF-1 levels at baseline.

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

In study CRN00808-09 , this proportion was 53% at the end of the RC phase.

Overall, the long-term efficacy results of the OLE phase of study CRN00808-08 and E-R analyses support paltusotine 60 mg QD as the maximum therapeutic dose. In the E-R analysis, based on a population maximum IGF-1 level reductions of 57%, paltusotine doses of 20 mg, 40 mg, and 60 mg corresponded to IGF-1 level reductions of approximately 37%, 43%, and 47%, respectively, while 80 mg would result in 49% reduction in IGF-. Thus, dose increases beyond 60 mg QD would have been not relevant, as providing only marginal decreases in IGF-1 levels. These results highlight that the achievement of IGF1 normalisation or near normalisation is dependent of IGF-1 baseline and a 60 mg QD dose cannot achieve IGF-1 normalisation (or near-normalisation) across a broad range of baseline IGF-1 values. This is consistent with the E-R analysis which showed that paltusotine doses of 20 mg, 40 mg, and 60 mg corresponded to IGF-1 level reductions of approximately 37%, 43%, and 47% , respectively.

Thus, based on all the updated clinical data of the OLE phase and on literature, the benefit-risk of paltusotine is considered positive in medically naïve patients in study CRN00808-08 at the dose regimen proposed in this study. But given that uncertainties on the possibility of patients with high IGF-1 levels at baseline (i.e ≥ 2.5xULN) including the medically naïve patients to normalise their IGF-1 levels, even at the 60 mg QD dose in monotherapy still remain, section 4.2 of the SmPC has been amended to mention that monitoring of IGF-1 levels and assessment of symptoms should be made periodically per the clinician's discretion and that normalization of IGF-1 levels may require a longer treatment duration in patients with high IGF-1 levels at baseline.

Although the withdrawal of patients was higher among the medically naïve participants, there was a majority of participants in the OLE phase of study CRN00808-08, with approximately 80% of participants overall  and  68%  of  medically  naïve  participants  with  durable  and  sustained  clinical  benefits  of paltusotine.

This is consistent with the results of other SRL treatments of acromegaly indicated in medically naïve patients. Indeed, the rates of discontinuation due to treatment failure in the literature were 17.9% for oral octreotide ( Samson 2020 ).

Another point is the need of adjunctive therapies in the OLE phase of study CRN00808-08 and in study CRN00808-09, which makes it difficult to interpret the long-term results, and in which extent the increase of the proportion of medically naïve patients with IGF1 levels ≤ULN over the OLE phase is only related to the effect of paltusotine as monotherapy. As the need for adjunctive therapies is currently limited, there is no impact on the proportion of patients who achieved normalization of their IGF-1 levels.

The safety profile of paltusotine for the treatment of acromegaly, based on pooled data from five clinical studies and supported by both primary and supportive analysis populations, appears consistent with other SRL therapies. The safety population size (84 in the primary group and 233 in the supportive group) and the median follow-up duration of approximately four years are considered acceptable for a rare disease such as acromegaly.

Most TEAEs were mild or moderate in severity, and their frequency was comparable between paltusotine and  placebo  groups.  The  most  frequently  reported  related  TEAEs  by  SOC  were  gastrointestinal disorders,  followed  by  metabolism  and  nutrition  disorders,  cardiac  disorders,  musculoskeletal  and connective  tissue  disorders,  nervous  system  disorders,  hepatobiliary  disorders,  and  investigations. Section 4.8 of the SmPC was aligned appropriately.

Ocular adverse events have been reported in clinical trials, some of which were assessed as related to paltusotine.  All ocular TEAEs were nonserious, mostly mild or moderate, and did not lead to treatment discontinuation. Events were reported infrequently and without consistent temporal or clinical pattern, and potential confounding factors (such as age-related conditions or concomitant medications known to cause ocular symptoms). However, given the biological plausibility based on nonclinical observations and the limited clinical dataset, the possibility of a causal relationship cannot be fully excluded. Therefore, this topic will remain under close monitoring in future PSURs, with continued cumulative review of ocular adverse events to confirm the absence of a safety signal over time.

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

In addition, the long-term safety and tolerability of paltusotine will be evaluated in the OLE of the longterm extension study CRN00808-05 (ACROBAT ADVANCE), which is on-going and a category 3 PASS in the RMP.

## 9.6.2. Balance of benefits and risks

The  efficacy  of  paltusotine  was  demonstrated  in  the  2  pivotal  phase  3  studies CRN00808-08 and CRN00808-09 at the dose regimen investigated in these pivotal studies. Paltusotine was able to achieve and maintain normal IGF-1 levels in most patients. An advantage of paltusotine is its oral administration, that could improve patient's compliance to treatment and reduce discomfort due to long-term treatment with injections.

However, the efficacy of paltusotine is lower in patients with high IGF1 levels (i.e ≥ 2.5x ULN) at baseline including  the  medically  naïve  patients,  and  the  efficacy  of  paltusotine  at  doses  up  to  60  mg/day  in monotherapy is still questionable for these patients. This has been reflected in section 4.2 of the SmPC for the prescribers of this new medicine.

The safety profile of paltusotine in acromegaly, based on pooled data from five clinical studies with adequate follow-up and population size for a rare disease, is overall consistent with other SRL therapies. Most  TEAEs  were  mild  or  moderate,  with  similar  frequency  between  paltusotine  and  placebo. Gastrointestinal  disorders  were  the  most  common  ADRs,  followed  by  sinus  bradycardia  and cholelithiasis, primarily occurring early in treatment.

Based on the results, from a clinical point of view, the benefit/risk balance of paltusotine in the following indication is positive. That is why the final indication reads:

'Palsonify is indicated for the medical treatment of adult patients with acromegaly.'

## 9.6.3. Additional considerations on the benefit-risk balance

Input  has  been  received  from  a  healthcare  professionals'  organisation, European  Society  of Endocrinology (ESE) . No new insight was provided since the points raised were already well understood in the current scientific literature and guidelines. The following points were mentioned by the ESE in the beginning of the procedure and considered during the assessment:

- medical therapy in patients in acromegaly is needed for patients who cannot undergo surgery or cannot be cured by surgery. The main drug classes used in the clinical practice in Europe nowadays are injectable somatostatin analogues and the growth hormone antagonist pegvisomant. To date, there is no approved oral acromegaly therapy available in the European market.

- treatment duration: in patients who are not cured by surgery or who cannot obtain surgery, acromegaly treatment is needed lifelong. In patients, who receive radiotherapy, and can be cured by radiotherapy, the medical treatment may be only needed till the radiotherapy reaches the maximal effects (up to a few years).

- new drugs are needed for use in patients who cannot tolerate other drugs because of side effects. Some patients remain refractory to the currently available drugs. In addition, an oral drug is expected to improve patients' compliance and satisfaction.

- the lack of oral therapies accounts among the unmet clinical needs in patients with acromegaly. Other unmet clinical needs are: 1) late diagnosis, patients leave for years with the disease prior to being diagnosed and this has a severe impact on comorbidities lifelong; 2) the limited success of neurosurgical techniques (surgery, radiotherapy) in some cases, especially in big tumours with extrasellar extension; 3) Inability to localise and neurosurgical treat the tumour in some cases with persistent disease, which then need lifelong medical therapy; 4) The reduced quality of life in patients with acromegaly, even when IGF1 gets normal under specific medical therapy.

<div style=\"page-break-after: always\"></div>

Feedback was also received from a patient organization, namely Dutch Pituitary Foundation , and has been taken into account; the points are summarised below:

- current medication is painful to administer, and it requires a HCP to give the injection. Patients are unable to administer the injection by themselves. Also, patients complain about the hardened injection spot.

- the wear-off effect is considered a big problem; a number of patients struggle to make is to the next injection. A new medicine that mitigates this wear-off effect will be welcomed by most patients. The wear-off phase will be noticed by increased tiredness and increased joint problems and mostly occurs in the last week before the new injection.

- keeping the current medication cooled is inconvenient when travelling. Also, the need to find a HCP for giving the injection during a stay abroad is considered difficult.

The current medication can cause serious side effects, as Diabetes Mellitus II and in some patients de gallbladder needs to be removed.

## 9.7. Benefit-risk conclusions

## 9.7.1. CHMP conclusions

The balance benefice-risks of paltusotine in the claimed indication is positive.
